Ayahs

طسٓ ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَكِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ (١)
Ta, Seen. These are the signs of the Quran and a manifest Book, (1)
هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢)
a guidance and good news for the faithful (2)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٣)
—those who maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and who are certain of the Hereafter. (3)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (٤)
As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have made their deeds seem decorous to them, and so they are bewildered. (4)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ (٥)
They are the ones for whom there is a terrible punishment, and they are the ones who will be the biggest losers in the Hereafter. (5)
وَإِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى ٱلْقُرْءَانَ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ (٦)
Indeed you receive the Quran from One who is all-wise, all-knowing. (6)

Content of Surah al-Naml

This Surah—as we have mentioned—was revealed in Mecca, and it is well-known that it followed Surah al-Shu‘ara.

The content of this Surah, in general, aligns with the themes of Meccan Surahs; from a doctrinal perspective, it emphasizes the Origin (Monotheism) and the Resurrection, speaking of the Quran, revelation, the signs of Allah in the world of creation, and the nature of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment. From a practical and ethical standpoint, a significant portion discusses the life stories of five great Divine Prophets and their struggles against deviant peoples. This serves both as a source of comfort and solace for the believers—who were in a severe minority in Mecca at that time—and as a warning to the stubborn and oppressive polytheists, so they might see their own end in the historical pages of past tyrants, perhaps waking up and coming to their senses.

One of the distinct features of this Surah is the detailed account of the story of "Solomon" and the "Queen of Sheba," how she came to believe in Monotheism, and the communication of birds like the Hoopoe and insects like the ant with Solomon.

Because of this, the Surah is named "al-Naml" (The Ant). Interestingly, in some narrations, it is referred to as the Surah of Solomon (sometimes the Surah of Solomon and sometimes the Ant!). As we shall see, these namings are very deliberate. These names, which originate from the teachings of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), sometimes highlight important realities that people overlook under normal circumstances.

Furthermore, this Surah speaks of the infinite knowledge of the Lord, His supervision over everything in the universe, and His sovereignty among His servants—awareness of which has an extraordinary educational effect on a human being.

The Surah begins with "glad tidings" and ends with a "threat": the glad tidings that the Quran has brought for the believers, and the threat that Allah is not heedless of the deeds of you, His servants.

Virtue of Surah al-Naml

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), it is stated:

"Whoever recites Ta-Sin of Solomon (Surah al-Naml), Allah will grant him ten rewards for every person who believed in Solomon or denied him, as well as for those who believed in or denied Hud, Shu‘ayb, Salih, and Abraham. On the Day of Resurrection, when he emerges from his grave, he will be calling out: La ilaha illa Allah (There is no god but Allah)." [^1] Although this Surah mentions Moses, Solomon, David, Salih, and Lot, and does not mention Hud, Shu‘ayb, or Abraham, this expression is not surprising since all Prophets are identical in their call.

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "Whoever recites the three Ta-Sins (Surahs al-Shu‘ara, al-Naml, and al-Qasas, which all begin with Ta-Sin) on Friday night, he will be among the friends of Allah, and will be in His proximity and under the shadow of His grace and protection." [^2]

[^1]: Majma‘ al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [^2]: Thawab al-A‘mal, as quoted from Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 74.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

طس تِلْكَ آياتُ الْقُرْآنِ وَ كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ (1) هُدىً وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (2) الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ هُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (3) إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (4) أُوْلئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الْعَذابِ وَ هُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ (5) وَ إِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى الْقُرْآنَ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ (6)

Commentary

The Quran is from a Wise, All-Knowing One

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter the "Disconnected Letters" (Muqatta‘at) of the Quran. Given that it immediately speaks of the greatness of the Quran, it seems that one of its secrets is that this great Book and these clear verses are composed of simple alphabet letters. It is worthy of praise to that Creator who brought forth such an original masterpiece from such simple materials. We have had detailed discussions on this subject at the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and al-A‘raf (refer to volumes 1, 2, and 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh).

Then He adds: "These are the verses of the Quran and a clear Book" (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْقُرْآنِ وَ كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ).

The reference to the distant (using the word "تِلْكَ" - those) is to express the greatness of these heavenly verses. The term "mubin" (clear/clarifying) is an emphasis that the Quran is both manifest in itself and clarifying of realities. [^1] Although some commentators have suggested that "Quran" and "Kitab Mubin" refer to two separate meanings—the latter referring to the "Preserved Tablet" (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz)—the apparent meaning of the verse shows that both describe one reality: one in the form of words and recitation, and the other in the form of writing and scripture.

In the second verse under discussion, two other descriptions for the Quran are stated: "A Quran that is a source of guidance and a means of glad tidings for the believers" (هُدىً وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

"Those who establish prayer and give zakat and they have certainty in the Hereafter" (الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ هُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ). Thus, both their belief in the Origin and the Resurrection is firm, and their connection with God and the creation is established. Therefore, the above descriptions refer to their complete faith and comprehensive practical program.

Here, the question arises: if this group of believers has already chosen the straight path in both their doctrinal and practical foundations, what need is there for the Quran to come for their guidance?

However, considering that guidance has various stages—where each stage is a prerequisite for a higher and even higher one—the answer to this question becomes clear.

Furthermore, the continuity of guidance is itself a major issue; it is the very thing we ask of Allah day and night in our prayers by saying: "Guide us to the straight path" (اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ), asking Him to keep us firm on this path and grant us continuity, for without His grace, this continuity is impossible.

Beyond this, benefiting from the verses of the Quran and the Clear Book is only possible for those who possess a spirit of truth-seeking, even if they have not yet reached complete guidance. If we see that in one place the Quran is introduced as a source of guidance for "the God-fearing" (al-Muttaqin) (Baqarah, verse 2), and in another place for "the Muslims" (Nahl, verse 102), and here for "the believers," one reason is that unless there is at least a degree of piety, submission, and faith in realities within a person's heart, they will not pursue the truth and will not benefit from the light of this Clear Book; for the receptivity of the vessel is also a condition.

Above all, "guidance" and "glad tidings" coupled together are only for the believers; others have no such glad tidings.

From this, it becomes clear that if in some verses the Quranic guidance is described broadly for all people (هُدىً لِلنَّاسِ) (Baqarah, 185), it refers to all those who have a favorable ground for accepting the truth. Otherwise, the prejudiced and stubborn ones are blind-hearted; even if thousands of suns were to shine upon them instead of one, they would not benefit in the least.

Then, He proceeds to describe the state of the group that stands in opposition to the believers, recounting one of their most dangerous states: "Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have made their deeds seem fair to them, so they wander blindly" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ).

Pollution appears as purity to them, ugliness as beauty, meanness as pride, and misery and wretchedness as happiness and victory.

Yes, such is the state of those who step onto the wrong path and persist in it. It is clear that when a person performs an ugly and incorrect act, its repulsiveness gradually diminishes in their eyes, and they become accustomed to it. After a while of being habituated, they fabricate justifications for it; gradually, it appears beautiful to them and even manifests as a duty. How many criminals and polluted individuals there are who truly take pride in their actions and consider them a positive point!

This inversion of values and the collapse of standards in a person's view—the result of which is wandering lost in the byways of life—is one of the worst states that can afflict a human being.

It is interesting that in the verse under discussion and another verse of the Quran (al-An‘am, 108), this "beautification" (tazyin) is attributed to "Allah," while in eight instances it is attributed to "Satan," and in ten instances it appears in the passive voice (zuyyina - was made fair). If we reflect deeply, all of these express a single reality.

As for its attribution to Allah, it is because He is the "Cause of Causes" in the universe, and every effect produced by any being ultimately traces back to Him. Indeed, Allah has placed this property in the repetition of an act: that a person gradually becomes accustomed to it, and their sense of discernment changes, without the person's responsibility being removed or there being any flaw or defect attributed to Allah (reflect carefully).

And if it is attributed to Satan or carnal desires, it is because they are the immediate and direct agents.

And if it sometimes appears in the passive voice, it indicates that the nature of the act itself necessitates that through repetition, it creates a "state," a "disposition," a "love," and an attachment.

Then, He addresses the result of the "beautification of deeds" and describes the end of such people: "Those are the ones for whom there is an evil punishment" (أُوْلئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الْعَذابِ). In this world, they will be wandering, hopeless, and distressed, and in the Hereafter, they will be caught in a terrifying punishment.

"And in the Hereafter, they are the greatest losers" (وَ هُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ هُمُ الْأَخْسَرُونَ).

The reason they are the greatest losers is the same as what is mentioned in Surah al-Kahf, verse 103: "Say: Shall We inform you of the greatest losers in respect of their deeds? Those whose efforts are lost in the life of this world while they think they are doing good work!" What loss is greater than for a person to see their ugly deeds as beautiful and to exert all their strength for them, thinking they are doing something positive, only to see in the end that it brought nothing but misery and wretchedness?

In the final verse under discussion, as a completion to the previous references regarding the greatness of the Quran's content and as an introduction to the stories of the Prophets that begin immediately after, He says: "And indeed, you are receiving the Quran from the presence of One Wise and All-Knowing" (وَ إِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى الْقُرْآنَ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ). Although "Wise" (Hakim) and "All-Knowing" (‘Alim) both refer to the knowledge of the Lord, "Wisdom" usually expresses practical aspects, while "Knowledge" expresses theoretical aspects. In other words, "‘Alim" informs of Allah's infinite awareness, and "Hakim" refers to the insight, calculation, and purpose employed in creating this world and revealing the Quran.

Such a Quran, revealed from such a Lord, must be a Clear Book and a clarifier, a source of guidance and glad tidings for the believers, and its stories must be free from any superstition or distortion. [^1]

Key Point: Realism and Faith

The important issue in human life is to perceive realities as they are and to take a clear stance toward them. Fantasies, prejudices, deviant inclinations, loves, and hates should not prevent the perception and vision of realities as they are. The most important definition given for philosophy is this: "Perceiving the realities of things as they are."

For this reason, one of the most important requests the Infallibles had from Allah was: "O Allah, show me things as they are" (so that I may recognize values correctly and fulfill their rights).

[^1]: "Tulaqqa" is the passive present tense from the form "Taf‘il." Its triliteral root (laqiya) is transitive to one object, and its augmented form is transitive to two objects. In the verse under discussion, Allah is the Agent and the One who bestows the Quran, the Prophet is the first object, and the Quran is the second object. Since it appears here in the passive voice, the first object takes the place of the subject, and the second object is explicitly mentioned in the speech.

And this state is not possible without "faith," because rebellious whims and carnal desires are the greatest veils and barriers on this path. Removing this veil is impossible except in the light of piety and the control of carnal desires.

Thus, we read in the above verses: "Those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have made their ugly deeds seem fair to them, and they wander blindly."

We clearly see a manifest and objective example of this meaning in the lives of a group of world-worshippers of our time.

They take pride in issues and count them as part of their civilization, which in reality are nothing but shame, pollution, and scandal.

They consider unbridled behavior and lack of restraint as a sign of "freedom."

The nakedness and pollution of women as proof of "civilization."

The race for luxury-worship as a sign of "personality."

Drowning in types of corruption as a manifestation of "liberty."

Murder, crime, and destruction as proof of "power."

Sabotage and the usurpation of others' wealth as "colonialism" (literally: making prosperous!).

Employing mass media for the most offensive anti-ethical programs as proof of "respecting human desires."

Trampling the rights of the deprived as a sign of "respecting human rights"!

Slavery in the clutches of addictions, whims, shames, and scandals as "a form of freedom."

Cheating, deception, and acquiring wealth by any means possible as proof of "talent and merit"!

Observing the principles of justice and respecting the rights of others as a sign of "incompetence and lack of merit."

Lies, breach of promise, hypocrisy, and deception as a sign of "politics."

In short, their evil and shameful deeds have been so beautified in their eyes that they not only feel no shame but even boast and take pride in them. It is clear what the face of such a world will look like and which direction the path it follows is heading!

Ayahs

إِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَهْلِهِۦٓ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا سَـَٔاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ ءَاتِيكُم بِشِهَابٍ قَبَسٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ (٧)
When Moses said to his family, ‘Indeed I descry a fire! I will bring you some news from it, or bring you a firebrand so that you may warm yourselves.’ (7)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهَا نُودِىَ أَنۢ بُورِكَ مَن فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨)
When he came to it, he was called: ‘Blessed is He who is in the fire and who is [as well] around it, and immaculate is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’ (8)
يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٩)
‘O Moses! Indeed I am Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (9)
وَأَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَا تَخَفْ إِنِّى لَا يَخَافُ لَدَىَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (١٠)
‘Throw down your staff!’ When he saw it wriggling, as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], without looking back. ‘O Moses! ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed the apostles are not afraid before Me, (10)
إِلَّا مَن ظَلَمَ ثُمَّ بَدَّلَ حُسْنًۢا بَعْدَ سُوٓءٍ فَإِنِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١١)
nor those who do wrong and then make up for [their] fault with goodness, for indeed I am all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (11)
وَأَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِى جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ۖ فِى تِسْعِ ءَايَـٰتٍ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (١٢)
‘Insert your hand into your shirt. It will emerge white and bright, without any fault—among nine signs meant for Pharaoh and his people. Indeed they are a transgressing lot.’ (12)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا مُبْصِرَةً قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٣)
But when Our signs came to them, as eye-openers, they said, ‘This is plain magic.’ (13)
وَجَحَدُوا۟ بِهَا وَٱسْتَيْقَنَتْهَآ أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا ۚ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (١٤)
They impugned them, wrongfully and out of arrogance, though they were convinced in their hearts [of their veracity]. So observe how the fate of the agents of corruption was! (14)

Commentary

Moses Comes Here in Hope of a Flame!

As we have mentioned, in this Surah, after explaining the importance of the Quran, a portion of the life stories of five great Prophets and their peoples is presented, and the promise of victory for the believers and the punishment of the disbelievers is clearly recounted therein.

First, it begins with the Arch-Prophet (Ulul-Azm) Moses عليه السلام and goes directly to the most sensitive moments of his life—the moment when the first spark of revelation shone in his heart and he became acquainted with the Divine message and speech. It says: "Remember when Moses said to his family: 'I have perceived a fire from afar!'" (إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِأَهْلِهِ إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً) [^1] "Stay here; I will soon bring you news from it, or a burning brand of fire, so that you may warm yourselves" (سَآتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ آتِيكُمْ بِشِهابٍ قَبَسٍ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ‌) [^2]. This occurred on the very night when Moses, accompanied by his wife (the daughter of Shu'ayb), was traveling toward Egypt and became stranded in a dark and gloomy wilderness. He lost his way, winds and storms began to blow, and at that same moment, his wife went into labor. Moses felt a desperate need to light a fire and use its warmth, but nothing was visible in that desert.

As soon as he saw a flame of fire from afar, he became happy, taking it as a sign of the presence of a human or humans. He said, "I am going; I will either bring you news or a flame of fire with which you can warm yourselves."

It is noteworthy that Moses says: "I will bring you news or a flame" (using the plural pronoun for "you"). This expression might be because, in addition to his wife, a child or children were with him—since ten years had passed since his marriage in Midian—or because in a terrifying desert, this phrasing provides more comfort to the listener.

[^1]: "آنست" (Anastu) is from the root Inas, meaning to observe or see something accompanied by a sense of tranquility; the word for human (Insan) is derived from this same root for this reason.

[^2]: "شهاب" (Shahab) means a light that shines like a pillar of fire; any light that has a linear extension like a pillar is called Shahab. Originally, it refers to those wandering celestial stones (meteors) that ignite upon hitting the compressed air around the Earth due to their staggering speed, drawing a vertical line of fire in the air. "قبس" (Qabas) means a flame of fire taken from a larger mass. "تصطلون" (Tastalun) is from the root Istila', meaning to warm oneself with fire.

Moses left his family there and moved toward where he had seen the fire. "When he reached it, a voice called out: 'Blessed is He who is in the fire and whoever is around it, and glorified is Allah, the Lord of the worlds!'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَها نُودِيَ أَنْ بُورِكَ مَنْ فِي النَّارِ وَ مَنْ حَوْلَها وَ سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

Regarding who is meant by "the one in the fire" and "the one around it," commentators have expressed various possibilities. What seems closest to the truth is that "the one in the fire" refers to Moses عليه السلام, who had come so close to that flame appearing from within the green tree that it was as if he were inside it. "The one around it" refers to the archangels of the Lord who surrounded that holy land at that specific moment.

Alternatively, it could be the opposite: those in the fire are the Divine angels, and the one around it is Moses عليه السلام.

In any case, it is mentioned in some narrations that when Moses عليه السلام approached the fire, he stood and looked closely; he saw a flame of fire shining from within a green branch. The flame grew brighter moment by moment, and the tree became greener and more beautiful. Neither did the heat of the fire burn the tree, nor did the moisture of the tree extinguish the flame! He was amazed. With a small branch he held in his hand, he leaned forward to take some of it; the fire moved toward him. He became frightened and stepped back. Sometimes he would move toward the fire, and sometimes the fire toward him, until suddenly a voice arose and the glad tidings of revelation were given to him.

The point is that Moses became so close to the fire that it justified the phrase "مَنْ فِي النَّارِ" (the one in the fire).

A third interpretation given for this phrase is that "مَنْ فِي النَّارِ" refers to the Light of God manifesting in the flame, and "من حولها" refers to Moses who was near it. However, in any case, to prevent any misconception regarding God having a "physical body," the end of the verse says: "سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌" (Glorified is Allah, Lord of the worlds), which clarifies that Allah is free from any defect, deficiency, physicality, or the attributes of matter.

Once again, a voice arose, addressing Moses and saying: "O Moses! Indeed, it is I, Allah, the Almighty, the All-Wise" (يا مُوسى‌ إِنَّهُ أَنَا اللَّهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌). This sentence was meant to remove any doubt or hesitation from Moses, so he would know that it is the Lord of the worlds speaking to him, not a flame or a tree—the God who is "Invincible" and "Possessor of Wisdom and Governance."

This expression is, in fact, a prelude to the miracle mentioned in the next verse, for miracles stem from these two attributes of the Lord: His "Power" and His "Wisdom." But before reaching the next verse, the question arises: how did Moses become certain that this voice was a Divine voice and not something else?

In response to this question, it can be said that the accompaniment of this voice with a clear miracle—namely, fire shining from within a green tree branch—was living proof that this was a Divine matter.

Furthermore, as we will see in the next verse, this call was followed by a command to Moses involving the miracles of the Staff and the White Hand, which were two other truthful witnesses to the reality of this call.

Beyond all this, a Divine call naturally possesses a unique quality and characteristic that distinguishes it from any other call. When a human hears it, it impacts the heart and soul in such a way that no doubt remains that this call is from Allah.

Since the mission of prophethood, especially against a tyrant and oppressor like Pharaoh, requires outward and inward strength and power, as well as a firm document of truthfulness, Moses عليه السلام was commanded here: "Throw down your staff" (وَ أَلْقِ عَصاكَ‌).

Moses threw his staff, and suddenly it turned into a massive serpent. "When Moses looked at it, he saw it moving and writhing rapidly like a small snake; he was terrified and turned back, not even looking behind him" (فَلَمَّا رَآها تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّها جَانٌّ وَلَّى مُدْبِراً وَ لَمْ يُعَقِّبْ‌) [^1]. It is also possible that the staff initially turned into a small snake and in later stages into a massive dragon!

[^1]: Some commentators believe that "جان" (Jann) is from the same root as "جن" (Jinn), meaning a hidden being, because small, thin snakes often move secretly and invisibly among the grass and narrow crevices of the earth, keeping themselves hidden.

At this point, Moses was addressed again: "O Moses! Do not fear, for indeed, the messengers do not fear in My presence" (يا مُوسى‌ لا تَخَفْ إِنِّي لا يَخافُ لَدَيَّ الْمُرْسَلُونَ‌).

This is the station of proximity and the sanctuary of security of the Almighty Lord. This is not a place where fear and terror exist. That is to say: O Moses, you are in the presence of the Great Lord, and His presence is synonymous with absolute security!

We read a similar expression in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 31: "O Moses! Draw near and do not fear; indeed, you are of those who are secure" (يا مُوسى‌ أَقْبِلْ وَ لا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ).

However, in the next verse, an exception is stated for the sentence "Indeed, the messengers do not fear in My presence," saying: "Except those who have done wrong and then substituted good after evil, for I am All-Forgiving, All-Merciful." I accept their repentance and grant them security as well (إِلَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ ثُمَّ بَدَّلَ حُسْناً بَعْدَ سُوءٍ فَإِنِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

Regarding how this exception relates to the preceding sentence, two different views have been expressed by commentators:

First, that there is an implied meaning at the end of the previous verse, which is: those other than the Prophets are not secure. Then the exception says: except those who repent and reform after wrongdoing and sin, for they too are included in Divine security.

Second, that the exception is from the sentence itself, and "wrongdoing" refers to the "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) that sometimes occurs from Prophets and does not contradict the station of infallibility ('ismah). That is, if Prophets perform a tark al-awla, they too are not in [absolute] security, and Allah deals strictly with them, as mentioned in the Quran regarding "Adam" and "Jonah."

Except for those Prophets who quickly realize their tark al-awla and return to the loving embrace of the Lord, compensating for it with righteous deeds and virtues, as mentioned regarding Moses in the story of killing the Coptic man, where Moses admitted his tark al-awla and said: "My Lord! Indeed, I have wronged myself, so forgive me" (Al-Qasas: 16).

Then He presented the second miracle to Moses عليه السلام and said: "Put your hand into your bosom; it will come out white and shining without any defect" (وَ أَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ). This indicates that this whiteness is not the whiteness resulting from leprosy, but rather a beautiful radiance, brilliance, and whiteness that itself signifies the existence of a miracle and a supernatural event.

Again, to show more grace to Moses عليه السلام and to give the misguided more opportunity for guidance, He says: your miracles are not limited to these two; rather, "these two miracles are among nine miracles with which you are sent to Pharaoh and his people, for they have been a rebellious and defiant people" and need guidance equipped with many great miracles (فِي تِسْعِ آياتٍ إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ وَ قَوْمِهِ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ‌) [^1]. From the appearance of this verse, it is understood that these two miracles were part of the nine famous miracles of Moses.

[^1]: The prepositional phrase "فِي تِسْعِ آياتٍ‌" is either related to the [implied] command "Go" or one of the general verbs that are omitted; it is also possible that "في" means "مع" (with). "And to Pharaoh" is also related to this same omitted sentence or the sentence "you are sent with them," which is also omitted.

In the detailed discussion we had in the commentary of Surah Al-Isra, verse 101, we concluded that the other seven miracles were: "the flood," "the blight on crops," "locusts," "the plague of frogs," and "the turning of the Nile's color into blood." Each of these five events struck the Pharaoh's people as a warning; whenever they were in dire straits, they would turn to Moses to remove the affliction.

The other two miracles were "drought" and "shortage of various fruits," which are mentioned in verse 130 of Surah Al-A'raf: "And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and deficiency in fruits that perhaps they would take heed" (For more explanation on this, refer to Volume 12, page 309 onwards).

Finally, Moses عليه السلام was armed with the strongest weapon of miracles and went to Pharaoh and his people, inviting them to the religion of truth. The Quran says in the next verse: "But when Our enlightening signs came to them, they said: 'This is obvious magic'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَتْهُمْ آياتُنا مُبْصِرَةً قالُوا هذا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ‌).

We know that this accusation was not unique to Moses عليه السلام; rather, stubborn bigots would raise the accusation of magic to justify their opposition to the Prophets and to create a barrier for others. This itself was a clear sign of the greatness of their supernatural work.

While we know that the Prophets were selfless, truth-seeking, and pious men, and magicians were deviant, materialistic individuals possessing all the traits of a "deceiver."

Furthermore, magicians always had the power to perform limited acts, but the Prophets—whose message and lifestyle testified to their truthfulness—performed miracles in an unlimited manner that bore no resemblance to magicians.

Interestingly, in the last verse under discussion, the Quran adds that these accusations were not because they were truly in doubt; rather, "they rejected them out of injustice and arrogance, while their inner selves were convinced of them" (وَ جَحَدُوا بِها وَ استَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ ظُلْماً وَ عُلُوًّا).

From this expression, it is clearly understood that faith (iman) is a reality distinct from knowledge and certainty (yaqin); it is possible for disbelief (kufr) to arise from denial and rejection despite knowledge and awareness.

In other words, the reality of faith is "submission in both outward and inward" to the truth. Therefore, if a person is certain of something but is not submissive to it inwardly or outwardly, they do not have faith; rather, they possess "disbelief of denial" (kufr al-juhud). This is an extensive topic that we will pass over with this brief mention for now.

Hence, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, while enumerating the five types of disbelief, he counts one type as "disbelief of denial" (kufr al-juhud), and describes one of its branches as follows: "It is when the denier denies while he knows it is the truth that has been established with him." He then cites the verse under discussion as evidence [^1]. It is noteworthy that the Quran counts two things as the motivation for the Pharaoh's people's denial: one is "injustice" (zulm) and the other is "arrogance/superiority" ('uluw).

"Injustice" might refer to usurping the rights of others, and "arrogance" might refer to their desire for superiority over the Children of Israel. That is, they saw that if they submitted to the signs and miracles of Moses, both their illegitimate interests would be endangered and they would have to stand on equal footing with their slaves, the Children of Israel—neither of which was tolerable for them.

Or, "injustice" could mean injustice to themselves or injustice to the signs, and "arrogance" could mean injustice to others, as stated in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 9: "Because they used to wrong Our signs."

In any case, at the end of this verse, as a lesson, it points to the evil end of the Pharaoh's people and their drowning and destruction with a short and very meaningful sentence: "So see how was the end of the corrupters!" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ‌).

The Quran does not unveil the details here because they had read the painful story of this disbelieving people in other verses, and with this short reference, they would understand what they needed to understand.

Additionally, here, out of all their ugly traits, it emphasizes the title "corrupter" (mufsid), which has a comprehensive meaning. It includes corruption in belief, speech, and action; corruption in the individual and in the social system. In reality, all their actions are gathered under the term "corruption" (ifsad).

[^1]: Al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapter: Aspects of Disbelief, page 287.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ عِلْمًا ۖ وَقَالَا ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى فَضَّلَنَا عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّنْ عِبَادِهِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٥)
Certainly, We gave knowledge to David and Solomon, and they said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who granted us an advantage over many of His faithful servants.’ (15)
وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ دَاوُۥدَ ۖ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ ٱلطَّيْرِ وَأُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٦)
Solomon inherited from David, and he said, ‘O people! We have been taught the speech of the birds, and we have been given out of everything. Indeed this is a manifest advantage.’ (16)

Commentary

The Governance of Dawud and Sulayman

Following a brief account of the story of Musa (عليه السلام), the discussion turns to two other great Divine prophets, Dawud and Sulayman. Regarding the former, there is only a brief mention, but concerning Sulayman, the discussion is more detailed.

The mention of a portion of the story of these two prophets after the story of Musa (عليه السلام) is because they were also among the prophets of the Children of Israel. The difference between their history and that of other prophets is that, due to the readiness of the intellectual and social environment of the Children of Israel, they succeeded in establishing a magnificent government and expanding the Divine religion by utilizing the power of governance. Therefore, the tone found in the stories of other prophets—who faced intense opposition from their people and were sometimes driven out of their cities and lands—is absent here, and the expressions used are entirely different.

This clearly demonstrates how many problems are solved and how the path is smoothed when Divine callers find the opportunity to form a government.

In any case, the discourse here is about knowledge, power, ability, and grandeur; it is about the submission and obedience of others, even the Jinn and devils, before the Divine government; it is about the submission of the birds of the air and other creatures; and finally, it is about the intense struggle against idolatry through logical invitation followed by the utilization of the power of governance.

These are the elements that distinguish the story of these two prophets from others.

Interestingly, the Quran begins the discourse with the issue of the "gift of knowledge," which is the foundation of a righteous and powerful government, saying: "And We certainly gave Dawud and Sulayman considerable knowledge" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُدَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ عِلْمًا).

Although many commentators have exerted themselves to identify what specific knowledge this was that is mentioned here in general terms, some—based on other verses—have considered it the knowledge of judgment and arbitration: "And We gave him wisdom and decisive speech" (وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ الْخِطَابِ) (Surah Sad, Verse 20); and "To each of them We gave judgment and knowledge" (وَكُلًّا آتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا) (Al-Anbiya, 79).

Others, based on the verses under discussion which speak of mantiq al-tayr (the speech of birds), have considered this knowledge to be the science of communicating with birds. Still others, based on verses speaking of the knowledge of weaving coats of mail and the like, have focused specifically on that science.

However, it is clear that "knowledge" here has a broad and expansive meaning that encompasses the knowledge of monotheism, religious beliefs, Divine laws, as well as the knowledge of judgment and all the sciences necessary for establishing such a vast and powerful government. This is because the establishment of a Divine government based on justice and equity—a prosperous and free government—is impossible without utilizing abundant knowledge. In this way, the Quran identifies the status of knowledge in human society and in the formation of government as the first foundational stone.

Following this sentence, it quotes Dawud and Sulayman as saying: "And they said: 'Praise be to Allah, who has favored us over many of His believing servants'" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي فَضَّلَنَا عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّنْ عِبَادِهِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

It is noteworthy that immediately after mentioning the great gift of "knowledge," "gratitude" is mentioned, to clarify that every blessing requires gratitude. The reality of gratitude is that the blessing be used in the very path for which it was created, and these two great prophets utilized the blessing of their knowledge to the maximum in organizing a Divine government.

Furthermore, they summarized the criterion of their superiority over others in "knowledge," not in power or governance, and they counted their praise and thanks as being for knowledge rather than other gifts. This is because every value belongs to knowledge, and every power originates from knowledge.

It is also noteworthy that they give thanks for governing a believing nation, as governing a corrupt and faithless group is no honor.

A question arises here: why did they say in their expression of gratitude that He favored them over "many" of the believers, rather than "all" believers, even though they were prophets who were the most excellent people of their time?

This expression might be to observe the principles of etiquette and humility, such that a person never considers himself superior to everyone else regardless of his rank. Or it may be because they were not looking at a specific point in time but were considering all times, and we know there have been prophets greater than them throughout human history.

In the next verse, it first refers to Sulayman inheriting from his father Dawud, saying: "And Sulayman inherited Dawud" (وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُدَ).

Regarding what is meant by "inheritance" here, there is much discussion among commentators. Some have restricted it to the inheritance of knowledge and wisdom, because they imagine that prophets do not leave an inheritance of their wealth. Others have mentioned exclusively the inheritance of wealth and governance, as this word brings that concept to mind before anything else. Some others mentioned the knowledge of speaking with birds (mantiq al-tayr).

However, considering that the verse is absolute and the subsequent sentences mention both knowledge and all gifts (أُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ), there is no reason to limit the concept of the verse. Therefore, Sulayman inherited all the gifts of his father Dawud.

In the narrations that have reached us from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), this verse has been used as an argument against those who claimed that prophets do not leave an inheritance and relied on the hadith: "We, the assembly of prophets, do not leave inheritance" (نحن معاشر الانبياء لا نورث). It was taken as proof that the aforementioned hadith is void of credibility because it contradicts the Book of Allah.

In a hadith narrated through the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt, we read: When Abu Bakr decided to take Fadak from Fatimah (عليه السلام), this news reached Fatimah (سلام الله عليها). She came to Abu Bakr and said:

"Is it in the Book of Allah that you inherit from your father and I do not inherit from my father? You have brought forth a monstrous thing! Have you intentionally abandoned the Book of Allah and cast it behind your backs when it says: 'And Sulayman inherited Dawud'?" [1]

Then the Quran adds: "And Sulayman said: 'O people! We have been taught the speech of birds'" (وَقَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ الطَّيْرِ). "And we have been given of all things; indeed, this is evident favor" (وَأُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَهُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْمُبِينُ).

Although some claim that the expression "speech" (nutq) regarding non-humans is only possible as a metaphor, if a non-human also emits sounds and words from its mouth that convey meanings, there is no reason not to call it speech. For "speech" is any utterance that expresses a reality and a concept. [2]

[1] The book Al-Ihtijaj by Tabarsi, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 75.

[2] Ibn Manzur says in Lisan al-Arab: Nutq is the same as speaking. Then he adds: "The speech of everything is its mantiq, and from this is the word of the Almighty: 'We have been taught the speech of birds' (mantiq al-tayr)." Then he quotes from another scholar of literature, Ibn Sidah (contrary to what some have said that nutq is exclusive to humans), that sometimes nutq is used for non-humans as well. It is also necessary to note that logicians and philosophers have applied nutq to the power of thought which gives humans the ability to speak.

Of course, we do not mean to say that those specific sounds sometimes made by certain animals during anger, satisfaction, pain, or longing for their offspring are "speech"; rather, these are sounds that arise from their mouths concurrent with a state. However, as will be detailed in the following verses, we see that Sulayman exchanges information with the "Hoopoe" (Hudhud), sends a message through it, and seeks the feedback of his message from it.

This shows that animals, in addition to sounds that express their states, have the ability to speak under specific conditions by the command of Allah. The same applies to the discussion regarding the speech of the "ant" that will appear in future verses.

Of course, sometimes nutq is used in a broad sense in the Quran, expressing the essence and result of "speech," which is "expressing what is in the mind," whether through words and speech or through other states, such as the verse: "This is Our record which speaks against you with truth" (هَٰذَا كِتَابُنَا يَنطِقُ عَلَيْكُم بِالْحَقِّ) (Al-Jathiyah, 29). But there is no need for us to interpret nutq in the case of Sulayman's conversation with birds in this sense; rather, according to the apparent meaning of the above verses, Sulayman could distinguish the specific words of birds used to convey information and speak with them.

In this regard, we will speak further in the section of Key Points, God willing.

As for the phrase "And we have been given of all things" (أُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَيْءٍ): contrary to the limitations some commentators have placed on it, it has a broad and expansive meaning and includes all the means that were necessary, spiritually and materially, for the formation of that Divine government. Fundamentally, without this, the statement would be incomplete and would have no clear link to the past.

Here, Fakhr Razi has raised a question: are the expressions "We have been taught" (ullimna) and "We have been given" (utina) of the type of speech used by the arrogant?

He then answers: The "plural" pronoun here refers to Sulayman himself and his father, or himself and his assistants in the government. It is common that whenever someone is at the head of an organization, they refer to themselves with a plural pronoun.

Key Points

1- The Relationship Between Religion and Politics

Contrary to what some narrow-minded people think, religion is not a collection of advice and exhortations or issues related to personal and private life. Religion is a set of laws for life and a comprehensive program that encompasses all of human life, especially social issues.

The mission of the prophets is for "the establishment of justice and equity" (Surah Al-Hadid, Verse 25). Religion is for breaking the chains of human captivity and ensuring human freedom (Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 157). Religion is for saving the "oppressed" (mustad'afin) from the clutches of the "oppressors and tyrants" and ending the era of their dominance. Finally, religion is a collection of education and training in the path of purification and building the perfect human (Surah Al-Jumu'ah, Verse 2).

Obviously, these great goals are impossible without the formation of a government. Who can establish justice and equity and shorten the hand of the oppressors from the necks of the oppressed through moral recommendations alone? Who can remove and break the chains of captivity from the hands and feet of bound humans without relying on power? And who can implement the correct principles of education and training and cultivate moral virtues in hearts in a society where the means of cultural dissemination and propaganda are in the hands of the corrupt and corrupters?

This is why we say "religion" and "politics" are two inseparable elements. If religion is separated from politics, it completely loses its executive arm, and if politics is separated from religion, it turns into a destructive element in the path of the interests of the self-willed.

If the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) succeeded in rapidly spreading this heavenly religion in the world, the reason was that he took action to establish a government at the first opportunity and pursued Divine goals through the Islamic government. Some other prophets who also found such success were better able to spread their Divine invitation, but those who were placed in a tight spot and circumstances did not allow them to form a government did not succeed in doing much.

2- The Tools of Divine Government

Interestingly, in the story of Sulayman and Dawud, we clearly see that they quickly uprooted the traces of polytheism and idolatry and established a Divine system—a system whose primary tools, according to the verses under discussion, were knowledge, science, and awareness in various fields.

A system where the name of "Allah" was at the top of all its programs. A system that utilized all capable forces, even using the power of a bird to reach its goals. A system that had bound the demons and put the oppressors in their place. And finally, a system that had sufficient military power, an intelligence apparatus, and individuals who had sufficient expertise and awareness in various economic and production fields, and placed all of these under the umbrella of faith and monotheism.

3- The Speech of Birds

In the above verses, and the verses that will follow in the story of the "Hoopoe" and "Sulayman," there is an explicit reference to the speech of birds and a degree of understanding and affairs for them.

Undoubtedly, birds—like other animals—manifest various sounds in different states which, with careful study, can reveal their states from the type of sound: which sound relates to states of anger and which to satisfaction, which sound indicates hunger and which is a sign of desire? With which sound does it call its offspring and with which sound does it inform them of the occurrence of a terrifying event?

This part of the birds' sounds is not subject to any doubt, and we are all more or less familiar with it.

However, the verses of this Surah apparently express something more than this. They discuss their speaking in a mysterious way in which more precise matters are reflected, and they discuss their understanding and conversation with a human. Although this meaning seems strange to some, considering the various matters scientists have written in books and the personal observations of some regarding birds, it is not a strange matter.

We know of matters regarding the intelligence of animals, especially birds, more strange than this. Some of them have such skill in building houses and nests that they sometimes surpass our engineers! Some birds have such information about the state of their future infants and their needs and problems, and act so precisely to solve them, that it is amazing to us all!

Their prediction of weather conditions even several months in advance, and their awareness of earthquakes before they occur—even before our seismographs record the slightest tremors—is well known. The training given to animals in our era and their extraordinary feats in circuses have been seen by many, indicating their amazing intelligence.

The wonderful works of "ants" and their magnificent civilization. The wonders of the life of "honeybees" and their astonishing tracking. The awareness of "migratory birds" that sometimes traverse the distance between the North and South Poles, regarding the state of the routes in this extraordinarily long path. The extraordinary information of "salmon" in collective migration in the depths of the seas—all are matters that are scientifically certain and proof of the existence of an important stage of understanding or instinct, or whatever we may call it, in these animals.

The existence of extraordinary senses in animals, such as the radar-like system of the bat, the very strong sense of smell of some insects, and the extraordinarily powerful vision of some birds and the like, is another proof that they are not behind us in everything! Considering these matters, it is not surprising that they have a specific speech and can speak with someone who is aware of the alphabet of their speech.

In the verses of the Quran, this matter has been referred to under various titles, including in Verse 38 of Surah Al-An'am where we read: "There is no creature on the earth nor a bird that flies with its two wings except that they are communities like you" (وَمَا مِن دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا طَائِرٍ يَطِيرُ بِجَنَاحَيْهِ إِلَّا أُمَمٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ). [1]

In Islamic narrations, there are also many matters expressing the speech of animals and especially birds, and even for each of them, a slogan-like saying has been narrated, the explanation of which would be lengthy. [2] In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that the Commander of the Faithful Ali (عليه السلام) said to Ibn Abbas:

"Indeed, Allah taught us the speech of birds just as He taught Sulayman son of Dawud, and the speech of every creature on land or sea." [3]

4- The Narration "We, the assembly of prophets, do not leave inheritance"

The Sunnis have narrated a hadith in their various books from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) to the effect that he said:

"We, the assembly of prophets, do not leave inheritance; what we leave is charity" (نحن معاشر الانبياء لا نورث ما تركناه صدقة). Sometimes they have narrated it by omitting the first sentence as "What we leave is charity."

The chain of this hadith in the famous books of the Sunnis mostly ends with "Abu Bakr," who took the reins of Muslim affairs after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). When Lady Fatimah (عليه السلام) or some of the Prophet's wives requested their inheritance from him, he refused to give them the inheritance based on this hadith! This hadith was recorded by Muslim in his Sahih (Vol. 3, Book of Jihad and Expeditions, p. 1379) and Bukhari in the eighth part of the "Book of Inheritance" (Kitab al-Fara'id, p. 185) and a group of others in their books.

It is noteworthy that in the latter source, in a hadith from Aisha, we read:

"Fatimah (عليه السلام) and Abbas came to Abu Bakr (after the death of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)) and wanted their inheritance from the Prophet, and they were at that time demanding their land in 'Fadak' and their share from 'Khaybar'. Abu Bakr said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) say: 'We do not leave anything as inheritance, and what we leave is charity'... When Fatimah (عليه السلام) heard this, she left Abu Bakr in a state of anger and did not speak a single word to him until the end of her life." [1]

Of course, this hadith is subject to critique and analysis from various aspects, but what fits within the scope of this commentary are the following matters:

1- This hadith is not consistent with the text of the Quran, and according to the rules of principles (usul) we have, any hadith that does not agree with the "Book of Allah" is void of credibility and cannot be relied upon as a hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) or the other Infallibles (عليهم السلام).

In the above verses, we read that Sulayman inherited from Dawud, and the appearance of the verse is absolute and includes wealth as well. Regarding "Yahya" and "Zakariyya," we read: "Grant me a child who will inherit from me and inherit from the family of Ya'qub" (يَرِثُنِي وَيَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ) (Maryam, 6). Especially regarding "Zakariyya," many commentators have emphasized the financial aspects.

[1] In the commentary of Verse 38 of Al-An'am, we had another discussion in this regard (refer to Volume 5 of the Tafsir, page 220 onwards).

[2] For more information, you can refer to Tafsir al-Qurtubi under the verses discussed and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 77 onwards.

[3] Same source, p. 81.

[1] Sahih al-Bukhari, Part 8, p. 185.

Furthermore, the appearance of the verses of "inheritance" in the Holy Quran is general and includes everyone.

Perhaps for this reason, Qurtubi, the famous Sunni scholar, was forced to take the hadith as a frequent and majority action rather than a general rule, saying it is like the sentence the Arabs say: "We, the assembly of Arabs, are the most hospitable of people to guests" (while this is not a universal rule). [1] But it is clear that this statement negates the value of this hadith, because if we resort to this excuse in the case of Sulayman and Yahya, its inclusion regarding other cases is also not certain.

2- The above narration conflicts with other narrations showing that Abu Bakr decided to return "Fadak" to "Fatimah" (سلام الله عليها), but others prevented him, as we read in Sirah al-Halabiyyah:

"Fatimah, the daughter of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), came to Abu Bakr while he was on the pulpit and said: 'O Abu Bakr, is it in the Book of Allah that your daughter inherits from you and I do not inherit from my father?' Abu Bakr wept and his tears flowed, then he descended from the pulpit and wrote a letter to hand over Fadak to Fatimah (سلام الله عليها). At this moment, Umar entered and said: 'What is this?' He said: 'I wrote a letter to hand over Fatimah's (عليه السلام) inheritance from her father to her.' Umar said: 'If you do this, from where will you provide the expenses for fighting the enemies while the Arabs have risen against you?' Then Umar took the letter and tore it!" [2]

How is it possible that there was an explicit prohibition from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and Abu Bakr dared to oppose it? And why did Umar rely on war needs and not on the narration of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)?

A careful examination of the above narration shows that the issue of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) prohibition was not the point; what was important here were the political issues of the day. It is these things that remind one of the words of Ibn Abi al-Hadid, the Mu'tazilite scholar, who says: I asked my teacher Ali ibn Fariqi: "Was Fatimah (عليه السلام) truthful in her claim?" He replied: "Yes." I said: "Then why did Abu Bakr not give Fadak to her, even though he considered her truthful and honest?!" My teacher gave a meaningful smile and said a beautiful, subtle thing, even though he was not accustomed to joking: "If he had given her Fadak today merely based on her claim, she would have come to him tomorrow and claimed the Caliphate for her husband! And she would have displaced him from his position, and he would have had no excuse to offer nor any possibility of agreement!" [1]

3- A famous narration from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) appears in many Sunni and Shiite books:

"The scholars are the heirs of the prophets" (العلماء ورثة الانبياء). [2] And it is also narrated from the noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) that:

"The prophets did not leave a dinar or a dirham as inheritance." [3]

From the sum of these two hadiths, it appears that the main goal was to clarify that the pride of the prophets and their capital was knowledge and science, and the most important thing they left behind was the program of guidance. Those who took a greater share of this knowledge are the true heirs of the prophets, without looking at the wealth that remains from them. Later, this hadith was narrated by meaning and misinterpreted, and possibly the sentence "What we leave is charity," which was the inference of some from the narration, was added to it.

Lest the discourse become too long, we end our words with a discussion from the famous Sunni commentator Fakhr Razi, which he brought under Verse 11 of Surah Al-Nisa:

He says: One of the specifications applied to this verse (the verse of children's inheritance) is something that is the school of most jurists (Sunnis), that the prophets (عليهم السلام) do not leave inheritance. The Shiites (generally) have opposed this discussion. It is narrated that when "Fatimah" (عليه السلام) demanded her inheritance, they prevented her from her inheritance based on a hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "We, the assembly of prophets, do not leave inheritance; what we leave is charity." At this time, Fatimah (عليه السلام) argued with the generality of the above verse (the verse of children's inheritance), as if she wanted to point to the fact that the generality of the Quran cannot be specified by a solitary report (khabar al-wahid).

Then Fakhr Razi adds: The Shiites say: even assuming that specifying the Quran by a solitary report is permissible, it is not permissible here for three reasons:

First, this is contrary to the explicit Quran which says "Zakariyya" asked Allah to give him a child who would inherit from him and the "family of Ya'qub," and also the Quran says: Sulayman inherited from Dawud. For these verses cannot be interpreted as the inheritance of knowledge and religion, because this is a type of metaphorical inheritance, since these prophets taught knowledge and religion to their children, not that they took it from themselves and handed it over to them. True inheritance is only conceived in wealth (which is taken from one and given to another).

Second, how is it possible that "Abu Bakr" was aware of this issue for which he had no need, but Fatimah, Ali, and Abbas—who were among the greatest ascetics and scholars and were concerned with the issue of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) inheritance—remained unaware of it? How is it possible that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) taught this hadith to someone who did not need it and withheld it from those who did?

Third, the sentence "What we leave is charity" follows "We do not leave inheritance," and its meaning is that the wealth we have designated as charity does not fall into the circle of inheritance, not other than that..."

Then Fakhr Razi gives a short answer to the famous argument above and says:

"Fatimah (عليه السلام), after talking with Abu Bakr, became satisfied with that conversation; furthermore, a consensus (ijma) has been formed that Abu Bakr's word is correct! [1]"

But it is clear that Fakhr Razi's answer is not worthy of the above arguments, because as we quoted from famous and reliable Sunni sources above, Fatimah (عليه السلام) not only did not become satisfied but became so angry that she did not speak a single word to Abu Bakr until the end of her life.

Beyond this, how is it possible for there to be a consensus on this issue when personalities such as Ali, Fatimah (عليه السلام), and Abbas—who were raised in the center of revelation—opposed it?!

Ayahs

وَحُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ جُنُودُهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلطَّيْرِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ (١٧)
[Once] Solomon’s hosts were marched out for him, comprising jinn, humans and birds, and they were held in check. (17)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ وَادِ ٱلنَّمْلِ قَالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّمْلُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مَسَـٰكِنَكُمْ لَا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (١٨)
When they came to the Valley of Ants, an ant said, ‘O ants! Enter your dwellings, lest Solomon and his hosts should trample on you while they are unaware.’ (18)
فَتَبَسَّمَ ضَاحِكًا مِّن قَوْلِهَا وَقَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَدْخِلْنِى بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِى عِبَادِكَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٩)
Whereat he smiled, amused at its words, and he said, ‘My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents, and that I may do righteous deeds which please You, and admit me, by Your mercy, among Your righteous servants.’ (19)

Commentary

Solomon in the Valley of the Ants!

From the verses of this Surah, as well as the verses of Surah Saba, it is clearly understood that the story of Prophet Solomon’s (عليه السلام) rule was not an ordinary affair; rather, it was coupled with various supernatural events and miracles. Some of these (such as Solomon’s rule over the Jinn and birds, his understanding of the speech of ants, and his conversation with the Hoopoe) are mentioned in this Surah, while another portion is found in Surah Saba.

In truth, Allah demonstrated His power by manifesting this magnificent government and the forces that were subjugated to it. We know that from the perspective of a monotheist, these matters are simple and easy before the power of Allah.

In the verses under discussion, He first says: "And gathered before Solomon were his hosts of jinn and men and birds" (وَ حُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمانَ جُنُودُهُ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَ الْإِنْسِ وَ الطَّيْرِ).

The multitude of his troops was so vast that for the sake of the army's discipline, "orders were given to halt the front ranks and move the rear ranks so that they all might join together" (فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ).

"Yūza‘ūn" is derived from the root "waz‘" (rhyming with jam‘), meaning to restrain or withhold. When this expression is used regarding an army, it means holding back the front of the army until the rear catches up, preventing them from becoming scattered or disorganized.

The word "waz‘" has also been used to mean intense greed or passion for something that restrains a person from other matters.

From this expression, it is understood that Solomon’s troops were both very numerous and under a specific system of discipline.

"Hashr" is from the root "hashr" (rhyming with nashr), meaning to bring a multitude out of their station and move them toward a battlefield or the like. From this expression, as well as the expression in the following verse, it is understood that Solomon had set out on a military expedition toward a certain point. But which of Solomon’s expeditions was this?

It is not precisely known. Some have inferred from the next verse, which speaks of Solomon reaching the "Valley of the Ants" (Wād al-Naml), that it was a region near Ta'if, while others have said it was a region near Sham (Greater Syria).

However, in any case, since stating this subject has no impact on the moral and educational aspects of the verse, it has not been mentioned.

Furthermore, the debate that has engaged a group of commentators—whether all humans, jinn, and birds were among his troops (making the word "min" [from] explanatory) or whether a portion of them formed his army (making "min" partitive)—seems to be a largely redundant discussion. This is because, undoubtedly, Solomon did not rule over the entire earth; his realm was the region of Sham and Jerusalem, and possibly some surrounding areas.

In fact, it is understood from the subsequent verses that he had not yet gained authority over the land of Yemen, and only after the incident of the "Hoopoe" and the submission of the Queen of Sheba did he gain control over it.

The phrase "He inspected the birds" in the following verses shows that among the birds under his command, there was one Hoopoe; when Solomon did not see it, he inquired about its status. If all birds [in the world] were present, including thousands of Hoopoes, this expression would not be correct (reflect on this).

In any case, Solomon moved with this great army "until they came upon the valley of the ants" (حَتَّى إِذا أَتَوْا عَلى‌ وادِ النَّمْلِ). Here, an ant from among the ants addressed its fellow kind and said:

"O ants, enter your dwellings lest Solomon and his hosts crush you while they perceive not!" (قالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يا أَيُّهَا النَّمْلُ ادْخُلُوا مَساكِنَكُمْ لا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمانُ وَ جُنُودُهُ وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ) [1]. Regarding how this ant became aware of the presence of Solomon and his troops in that land and how it conveyed its voice to others, we shall discuss this in the "Key Points" section, God willing.

Additionally, it is understood from this sentence that Solomon’s justice was apparent even to the ants, for the implication is that if they were aware, they would not crush even a weak ant, and if they were to do so, it would be due to their lack of awareness.

"So Solomon smiled, laughing at her speech" (فَتَبَسَّمَ ضاحِكاً مِنْ قَوْلِها). Commentators have various views on what caused Solomon to laugh:

The apparent meaning is that the very nature of this event was a wondrous matter—that an ant would warn its fellows about Solomon’s great army and attribute to them a lack of awareness; this wondrous matter caused Solomon to laugh.

Some have also said this was a laugh of joy, because Solomon realized that even the ants recognized his justice and that of his troops and acknowledged their piety!

Others have said his joy was because Allah had given him such power that, despite the great noise and excitement of the army, he did not remain heedless of the voice of an ant!

In any case, at this point, Solomon turned toward the threshold of Allah and made several requests.

First, "he said: 'My Lord, inspire me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents'" (وَ قالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَ عَلى‌ والِدَيَ) [2], so that I may utilize all these great blessings in the way You have commanded and which is a source of Your pleasure, and that I do not deviate from the path of truth; for expressing gratitude for all these blessings is not possible except by Your aid and assistance.

Second: "and that I may do righteous deeds that please You" (وَ أَنْ أَعْمَلَ صالِحاً تَرْضاهُ).

This is an indication that what is important to me is not the survival of this army, troops, government, and vast organization; what is important is that I perform a righteous deed that becomes a source of Your pleasure. Since "a‘mala" (I do) is in the present/future tense, it indicates that he requested the continuation of this success.

And finally, his third request was: "and admit me by Your mercy among Your righteous servants" (وَ أَدْخِلْنِي بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِي عِبادِكَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

[1]: Some commentators have specified that the "tā" in "namlah" is to indicate a single unit, and the use of the feminine verb is to respect the outward form of the word. [2]: "Awzi‘nī" is from the root "īzā‘," meaning inspiration, or meaning to restrain from deviation, or meaning to create love and passion; however, most commentators have chosen the first meaning.

Key Points

1. Solomon’s Awareness of the Speech of Animals

We do not have much information regarding the world of animals, and despite all the progress made in this field, many ambiguities still cast a shadow over it.

We see signs of intelligence, precision, cleverness, and skill in the actions of many of them: the house-building of honeybees, the order governing the hive, the precision of ants in gathering winter necessities, their method of storage and warehousing, animals defending themselves against enemies, and even their awareness of the treatment for many diseases, finding their nests and homes from very long distances, traversing long paths to reach a destination, their prediction of future events, and the like—all are things that show that in the mysterious world of animals, many means remain unresolved for us.

Beyond this, many animals perform amazing feats through training and education that even humans are incapable of.

However, it is not precisely clear to what extent they are aware of the human world. Do they truly know who we are and what we do? We might not find such intelligence and perception in them, but does this mean it is negated?! On this basis, if we read in the above story that the ants became aware of the arrival of Solomon’s army in that land and announced a retreat to their nests so as not to be crushed under the feet of the army, and Solomon also became aware of this event, it is not a cause for great surprise.

Furthermore, Solomon’s rule—as we have said—was coupled with supernatural events and miraculous acts. Based on this very principle, some commentators have expressed the opinion that possessing this level of awareness by a class of animals in the era of Solomon was itself a miracle and a supernatural event, and there is no obstacle to us not seeing the same in other eras and centuries.

The point is that there is no reason for us to interpret the story of Solomon and the ant, or Solomon and the Hoopoe, as a metaphor, allegory, or "language of disposition" (zabān-e hāl) and the like, when preserving its literal form and interpreting it according to its true meaning is possible [1].

2. Solomon and the Inspiration of Gratitude to the Lord

One of the best signs for distinguishing Divine rulers from tyrannical rulers is that the latter, upon reaching power, become drowned in pride and negligence, consigning all human values to oblivion and sinking deeply into autocracy.

However, Divine rulers, upon attaining power, feel a heavy burden of responsibility on their shoulders; they turn to the threshold of Allah more than ever and seek from Him the ability to fulfill their mission. Just as Solomon, after all that power, the most important thing he requests from Allah is the fulfillment of gratitude to Him and the use of these bounties in the path of His pleasure and the comfort of Allah’s servants.

Interestingly, he begins this request with the word "awzi‘nī," the concept of which is an internal inspiration and the gathering of all inner forces to achieve this great goal. That is: "O God, grant me such power that I may mobilize all my internal forces to fulfill gratitude and perform my duty, and You show me the way, for it is a very difficult, long, and perilous path—the path of fulfilling the rights of all people in such a vast and expansive government!"

He not only requests the ability to be grateful for the blessings given to him personally but also requests to fulfill the gratitude for the favors bestowed upon his parents. This is because many of the blessings of a human's existence are inherited from parents, and undoubtedly, the resources Allah gives to parents serve as an effective aid for children in reaching their goals.

3. Solomon and Righteous Deeds

It is interesting that Solomon, despite having that unique power and rule, his request from Allah is to perform righteous deeds continuously, and higher than that, to be among the righteous servants of Allah.

From this expression, it becomes clear, firstly, that the ultimate goal of obtaining power is to perform righteous deeds—deeds that are worthy and valuable—and everything else is considered a preliminary to that.

Righteous action is also a preliminary to seeking the pleasure and satisfaction of Allah, which is the ultimate goal and the "End of all Ends."

Secondly, being included among the righteous is a stage beyond performing righteous deeds; the former is inherent righteousness (salāh-e dhātī) and the latter is righteousness of action (salāh-e ‘amalī) (reflect on this).

In other words, sometimes a person performs a righteous deed, but this meaning has not become part of his essence, soul, and the fabric of his being. Solomon asks Allah to be so encompassed by the Lord’s grace that being righteous transcends his actions and penetrates into the depths of his soul and the core of his being, and this is not possible except by Divine mercy.

Truly, how precious and valuable it is to be a righteous servant of Allah, such that Solomon, with all his majesty, status, and glory—which left no room for doubt for anyone—still requests that Allah, by His mercy, place him in the line of righteous servants and protect him from the slips that are possible at any time for a human, especially a human at the head of a vast organization.

[1]: We have also spoken regarding this in Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 224 (under verse 38 of Surah Al-An'am).

Ayahs

وَتَفَقَّدَ ٱلطَّيْرَ فَقَالَ مَا لِىَ لَآ أَرَى ٱلْهُدْهُدَ أَمْ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَآئِبِينَ (٢٠)
[One day] he reviewed the birds, and said, ‘Why do I not see the hoopoe? Or is he absent?’ (20)
لَأُعَذِّبَنَّهُۥ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا أَوْ لَأَا۟ذْبَحَنَّهُۥٓ أَوْ لَيَأْتِيَنِّى بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢١)
‘I will punish him with a severe punishment, or I will behead him, unless he brings me a credible excuse.’ (21)
فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ فَقَالَ أَحَطتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍۭ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ (٢٢)
He did not stay for long [before he turned up] and said, ‘I have alighted on something which you have not alighted on, and I have brought you from Sheba a definite report. (22)
إِنِّى وَجَدتُّ ٱمْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَأُوتِيَتْ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ (٢٣)
I found a woman ruling over them, and she has been given everything, and she has a great throne. (23)
وَجَدتُّهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ (٢٤)
I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of Allah, and Satan has made their deeds seem decorous to them—thus he has barred them from the way [of Allah], so they are not guided— (24)
أَلَّا يَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى يُخْرِجُ ٱلْخَبْءَ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُخْفُونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ (٢٥)
so that they do not prostrate themselves to Allah, who brings forth the hidden in the heavens and the earth, and He knows whatever you hide and whatever you disclose. (25)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ ۩ (٢٦)
Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Lord of the Great Throne.’ (26)

Commentary

The Story of the Hoopoe and the Queen of Sheba

In this section of the verses, reference is made to another episode of the wondrous life of Solomon (peace be upon him), recounting the story of the hoopoe and the Queen of Sheba.

First, it says: "Solomon did not see the hoopoe and went in search of him" (وَ تَفَقَّدَ الطَّيْرَ).

This expression clearly illustrates the fact that he carefully monitored the state of the country and the affairs of his government, such that even the absence of a single bird did not remain hidden from his eyes!

Undoubtedly, the "bird" intended here is the hoopoe, as the Quran adds in the continuation of the speech: Solomon "said: 'What is the matter with me that I do not see the hoopoe?'" (فَقالَ ما لِيَ لا أَرَى الْهُدْهُدَ).

"Or is he among the absentees?" (أَمْ كانَ مِنَ الْغائِبِينَ).

How did Solomon notice that the hoopoe was not present in his gathering?

Some have said it was because when he traveled, the birds would cast a shadow over his head, and he became aware of the hoopoe's absence through a gap in this vast canopy.

Others have attributed a specific mission to the hoopoe within his administration, considering him responsible for finding water sources; thus, when there was a need to search for water, he found him absent.

In any case, the expression where he first said, "I do not see him," and then added, "or is he among the absentees?" might imply: Is he absent without a justified excuse, or has he gone missing with a valid reason?

Regardless, an organized, disciplined, and powerful government has no choice but to oversee all activities occurring within the environment of the country and its territory, not overlooking even the presence or absence of a bird—an ordinary official. This is a great lesson.

So that he would not pass judgment in absentia, and to ensure that the hoopoe's absence would not affect the rest of the birds—let alone the humans holding sensitive positions—Solomon added: "I will surely punish him with a severe punishment!" (لَأُعَذِّبَنَّهُ عَذاباً شَدِيداً).

"Or I will surely slaughter him!" (أَوْ لَأَذْبَحَنَّهُ).

"Or he must bring me a clear reason [for his absence]" (أَوْ لَيَأْتِيَنِّي بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ).

The word Sultan here refers to a proof or evidence that grants a person authority to prove their objective. Its emphasis with Mubin (clear) is because this violator must certainly provide a completely clear justification for his transgression.

In truth, Solomon (peace be upon him), without judging in absentia, issued the necessary threat in case the violation was proven. He even established two stages for his threat, proportionate to the degree of the offense: the stage of punishment without execution, and the stage of capital punishment.

Furthermore, he demonstrated that even before a weak bird, he is submissive to evidence and logic, and never relies solely on his power and might.

"But the hoopoe's absence did not last long" (فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ). He returned, faced Solomon, and "said: 'I have gained knowledge of something which you do not know, and I have brought to you from Sheba certain (and first-hand) news'" (فَقالَ أَحَطْتُ بِما لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ وَ جِئْتُكَ مِنْ سَبَإٍ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ).

The hoopoe seemingly observed signs of anger on Solomon's face, and to dispel his discomfort, he first gave a brief and cryptic report of an important matter that even Solomon, with all his knowledge and wisdom, was unaware of! Once Solomon's anger subsided, he proceeded to detail it, as will appear in the following verses.

It is noteworthy that Solomon's soldiers, and even the birds obedient to his command, were given such freedom, security, and courage by Solomon's justice that the hoopoe could tell him openly and bluntly without fear: "I have gained knowledge of something that you are not aware of."

His interaction with Solomon was not like that of sycophantic courtiers with tyrannical monarchs, who, to state a fact, first spend time flattering, portraying themselves as insignificant specks, and then present their point amidst a hundred types of adulation to the "royal feet," never being direct and always using metaphors "softer than a flower" lest any dust settle on the Sultan's heart! Yes, the hoopoe said clearly: My absence was not without reason; I have brought important news of which you are unaware! Furthermore, this expression is a great lesson for everyone: that a small creature like a hoopoe may know something that the most knowledgeable human of his time is unaware of, so that man does not become arrogant regarding his own knowledge, even if he is Solomon with the vast knowledge of prophethood.

In any case, the hoopoe recounted the event as follows: "I had gone to the land of Sheba; I found there a woman ruling over them, who has been given everything, and especially, she has a magnificent throne!"
