(إِنِّي وَجَدْتُ امْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَ أُوتِيَتْ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ لَها عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ). With these three sentences, the hoopoe described almost all the characteristics of the country of Sheba and its form of government to Solomon.

First, it is a prosperous country possessing all kinds of blessings and facilities. Second, a woman rules over it, and she has a very magnificent court—perhaps even more magnificent than Solomon's establishment, for the hoopoe had certainly seen Solomon's throne, yet he refers to the throne of the Queen of Sheba as a "magnificent throne" (Arshun 'Azim)!

With these words, he made Solomon understand: Do not imagine that the entire world is under your domain and that greatness and a large throne belong only to you.

Solomon fell into thought upon hearing this, but the hoopoe gave him no respite and added another point—a strange and distressing matter he had seen there: "I found her and her people prostrating to the sun—instead of Allah!" (وَجَدْتُها وَ قَوْمَها يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). "Satan has gained mastery over them and made their deeds seem fair to them" (and they take pride in prostrating to the sun!) (وَ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

And in this way, "Satan has barred them from the [Right] Way" (فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ). They are so deeply immersed in idolatry that I do not believe they will easily return from this path; "they will not be guided" (فَهُمْ لا يَهْتَدُونَ).

Thus, he also defined their religious and spiritual state: that they are deeply mired in idolatry, the government promotes sun-worship, and the people follow the religion of their kings. Their idol temples and other conditions indicate that they persist in this wrong path, love it, and boast about it. In such circumstances, where the masses and the government are aligned, their being guided is very unlikely.

Then he added: "Why do they not prostrate to Allah, who brings forth what is hidden in the heavens and the earth, and knows what you conceal and what you reveal?" (أَلَّا يَسْجُدُوا لِلَّهِ الَّذِي يُخْرِجُ الْخَبْ‌ءَ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ يَعْلَمُ ما تُخْفُونَ وَ ما تُعْلِنُونَ) [1]. The word khab’ (rhyming with sabr) means anything hidden or covered, and here it refers to the encompassment of the Lord's knowledge over the unseen of the heavens and the earth; meaning, why do they not prostrate to the God who knows the unseen of the heavens and the earth and its hidden secrets?

The fact that some have interpreted it specifically as rain (regarding the heavens) and plants (regarding the earth) is, in fact, an explanation of a clear manifestation (misdaq). The same applies to those who interpreted it as bringing forth beings from the unseen of non-existence into existence.

It is interesting that He first speaks of Allah's knowledge of the hidden secrets of the earth and heaven, and then of the hidden secrets within human hearts! As for why the hoopoe emphasized the attribute of Allah being the Knower of the unseen and the manifest in the macrocosm and microcosm, it might be appropriate because Solomon, despite all his power, was unaware of the existence of the country of "Sheba" and its characteristics. He says one must seek the grace of a God from whom nothing is hidden.

Or, it may be appropriate because—according to popular accounts—the hoopoe possessed a special sense to detect water deep within the earth; hence, he speaks of a God who is aware of all that is hidden in the universe.

[1]: The word alla (أَلَّا), according to a group of commentators, is composed of an (أَنْ) and la (لَا) here, and they consider it related to the phrase saddahum or zayyana lahum, assuming a latent lam (لـ), making the meaning: "He barred them from the way so that they would not prostrate to Allah." However, it appears that alla here is a particle of incitement (tahdid) and carries the meaning of halla (why not?), and as we stated above, this sentence is apparently a continuation of the hoopoe's speech, although some have considered it a new sentence (isti’nafiyah) and the words of Allah.

Finally, he concludes his speech: "Allah—there is no deity except Him, the Lord of the Magnificent Throne" (اللَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ). In this way, he emphasizes the "Oneness of Worship" (Tawhid al-Ibadah) and the "Oneness of Lordship" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah) of the Lord, negating all forms of polytheism, and ends his discourse.

Key Points

A—Instructive Lessons [from the Story of Solomon]

What we read in this section of the verses contains numerous points that can be effective in the lives of all humans and the conduct of all governments:

  1. The head of a government or a manager must be so precise in his organizational structure that he senses and follows up on even the absence of an ordinary and minor individual.
  2. He should be watchful of an individual's violation and, to ensure it does not affect others, take firm measures and necessary precautions.
  3. One should never judge someone in absentia; they must be allowed to defend themselves if possible.
  4. The penalty must be proportionate to the crime, a suitable punishment should be considered for every offense, and the hierarchy of discipline should be observed.
  5. Everyone, even the greatest social powers, must submit to evidence and logic, even if the evidence comes from the mouth of a small individual.
  6. Such directness and freedom must prevail in the social environment that even an ordinary individual can say to the head of government when necessary: "I know something that you do not know!"
  7. It is possible for the smallest individuals to become aware of matters that the greatest scholars and powerful figures are unaware of, so that man never becomes arrogant about his knowledge, even if he is Solomon with the vast knowledge of prophethood.
  8. In human social organization, mutual needs are so great that sometimes "Solomons" find themselves in need of a bird.
  9. Although there are many merits in the female gender—and even this story shows that the Queen of Sheba possessed extraordinary understanding and sagacity—nevertheless, leading a government is not very compatible with their spiritual and physical disposition, such that the hoopoe also marveled at this and said: "I found a woman ruling over them!"
  10. People often follow the same religion as their rulers; hence, in this story, we read that the hoopoe says he saw that woman and her people prostrating to the sun (first mentioning the Queen's prostration, then her people's).

B—Answering Several Questions

Some commentators have raised questions here: Among them: How was Solomon, with all that knowledge and the resources of his government, unaware of the existence of such a country? Furthermore, how did the hoopoe traverse the distance between Yemen and the center of Solomon's government (which was apparently the land of Sham/Levant)? Moreover, had the hoopoe lost his way to end up there, or did he have another purpose?

Regarding the first question, it can be answered that Solomon likely knew of the existence of such a country but did not know its specific features and characteristics. Additionally, the desert of Hijaz lay between these two countries, and communication methods at that time were never like those of our time (of course, knowledge through the "Unseen" and divine inspiration is a different matter).

As for traversing this distance, it is not impossible for a hoopoe, as we know of birds that travel the distance between the North and South Poles, while the distance from Yemen to Sham is negligible in comparison.

The hoopoe's arrival in that land might have been because, according to some histories, Solomon had come from the land of Sham to the land of "Mecca" to visit the House of God and perform the Abrahamic rites (Hajj). Then, on his path, he inclined toward the south to the extent that he was not far from the land of Yemen. While Solomon was resting, the hoopoe took the opportunity and came near the palace of the Queen of Sheba, and this strange scene caught his attention. [1]

[1]: For more explanation regarding this event, refer to Da’irat al-Ma’arif Farid Wajdi, Vol. 10, p. 470 (under the entry "Hoopoe"), although the detailed narration is not free from exaggerations.

Ayahs

۞ قَالَ سَنَنظُرُ أَصَدَقْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٢٧)
He said, ‘We shall see whether you are truthful, or if you are one of the liars. (27)
ٱذْهَب بِّكِتَـٰبِى هَـٰذَا فَأَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا يَرْجِعُونَ (٢٨)
Take this letter of mine and deliver it to them. Then draw away from them and observe what [response] they return.’ (28)
قَالَتْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ إِنِّىٓ أُلْقِىَ إِلَىَّ كِتَـٰبٌ كَرِيمٌ (٢٩)
She said, ‘O [members of the] elite! Indeed a noble letter has been delivered to me. (29)
إِنَّهُۥ مِن سُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَإِنَّهُۥ بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٣٠)
It is from Solomon, and it begins in the name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (30)
أَلَّا تَعْلُوا۟ عَلَىَّ وَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ (٣١)
[It states,] ‘‘Do not defy me, and come to me in submission.’’ ’ (31)
قَالَتْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ أَفْتُونِى فِىٓ أَمْرِى مَا كُنتُ قَاطِعَةً أَمْرًا حَتَّىٰ تَشْهَدُونِ (٣٢)
She said, ‘O [members of the] elite! Give me your opinion concerning my matter. I do not decide any matter until you are present.’ (32)
قَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أُو۟لُوا۟ قُوَّةٍ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ وَٱلْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ فَٱنظُرِى مَاذَا تَأْمُرِينَ (٣٣)
They said, ‘We are powerful and possess a great might. But it is up to you to command. So consider what orders you will give.’ (33)
قَالَتْ إِنَّ ٱلْمُلُوكَ إِذَا دَخَلُوا۟ قَرْيَةً أَفْسَدُوهَا وَجَعَلُوٓا۟ أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِهَآ أَذِلَّةً ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ (٣٤)
She said, ‘Indeed when kings enter a town, they devastate it, and make the mightiest of its people the weakest. That is how they act. (34)
وَإِنِّى مُرْسِلَةٌ إِلَيْهِم بِهَدِيَّةٍ فَنَاظِرَةٌۢ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٣٥)
I will send them a gift, and see what the envoys bring back.’ (35)

Commentary

Kings are Destroyers!

Solomon (سليمان) listened carefully to the hoopoe's words and fell into deep thought. It is likely that Solomon’s strongest inclination was that this news was true, as there was no reason for a lie of such magnitude. However, since it was not a simple matter and was tied to the fate of a country and a great nation, he could not rely solely on the report of one informant; rather, he had to conduct further investigations into this sensitive subject.

Therefore, He said: "We shall see whether you have spoken the truth or are of the liars" (قالَ سَنَنْظُرُ أَ صَدَقْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ‌).

This statement clearly proves that in important and decisive matters, one must pay attention even to information coming from a small individual and must promptly (as the "seen" in the word "sananzuru" implies) carry out the necessary investigations regarding it.

Solomon neither accused nor condemned the hoopoe, nor did he believe its words without proof; rather, he made them the basis for an investigation.

In any case, Solomon wrote a very short yet profound letter and gave it to the hoopoe, saying: "Go with this letter of mine and deliver it to them; then withdraw from them and see what response they return" (اذْهَبْ بِكِتابِي هذا فَأَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَانْظُرْ ما ذا يَرْجِعُونَ‌) [1]. From the expression "deliver it to them" (أَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ), it is understood that he should drop it before them while the Queen of Sheba is present among her assembly, so that there remains no room for forgetting or concealment. From this, it becomes clear that what some commentators have said—that the hoopoe entered the palace of the Queen of Sheba and her bedchamber and dropped the letter on her chest or throat—has little evidence, although it is not inconsistent with the phrase in the following verse: "Indeed, a noble letter has been delivered to me" (إِنِّي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيَّ كِتابٌ كَرِيمٌ‌).

The Queen of Sheba opened the letter and became aware of its contents. Since she had previously heard of Solomon's name and reputation, and the content of the letter showed that Solomon had made a firm decision regarding the land of Sheba, she fell into deep thought. Because she used to consult with her associates on important state matters, she summoned them and, turning to them, "said: O chiefs and nobles! Indeed, a noble letter has been delivered to me" (قالَتْ يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ إِنِّي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيَّ كِتابٌ كَرِيمٌ‌).

Did the Queen of Sheba truly not see the messenger, but sensed the authenticity of the letter from the clues within it, and did not consider it possible for it to be a forgery? Or did she see the messenger with her own eyes, and its wondrous state was itself proof that there was a reality at hand and the matter was not ordinary? Whatever it was, she relied on the letter with confidence.

As for the Queen saying: "This is a noble and valuable letter," it might be because of its profound content, or because it began with the name of God and ended with a proper seal and signature [1]—each of which has been suggested by some commentators—or perhaps all of these, as there is no contradiction between these points and they could all be encompassed in this comprehensive concept.

It is true that they were sun-worshippers, but we know that many idolaters also believed in "Allah" and called Him "the Lord of lords" (رب الارباب) and considered His glorification and respect important.

Then the "Queen of Sheba" proceeded to mention the content of the letter and said: "Indeed, it is from Solomon, and indeed, it is: In the name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ سُلَيْمانَ وَ إِنَّهُ بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌).

"My recommendation to you is: Do not be haughty against me, and come to me in submission to the truth" (أَلَّا تَعْلُوا عَلَيَّ وَ أْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ‌) [1]. It seems unlikely that Solomon wrote the letter with these exact Arabic words and phrases; therefore, the above sentences could be a paraphrase or a condensed summary of Solomon's letter that the Queen of Sheba recounted to her people. Interestingly, the content of this letter consisted of only three points:

  1. The name of God and the description of His attributes of Mercy (Rahmaniyyah and Rahimiyyah).
  2. A recommendation to control one's ego and abandon haughtiness, which is the source of many individual and social corruptions.
  3. Submission to the truth!

If we look closely, there was nothing else that needed to be mentioned.

[^1]: Some commentators have said that the phrase "ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ" (then withdraw from them) is delayed in meaning but placed forward in the expression, and the intended meaning was: "See what they return, then withdraw from them." This is because they took this phrase to mean returning from that people, whereas the apparent meaning of the verse is: turn away from them, then wait in a corner to see what reaction they show.

[^1]: It is mentioned in a Hadith: "The nobility and value of a letter lies in its sealing" (Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Al-Mizan, and Qurtubi). In another Hadith, it is stated that when the Prophet of Islam wanted to write a letter to the Persians, they told him that they do not accept a letter without a seal. The Prophet ordered a ring to be made with the inscription "لا اله الا اللَّه محمدا رسول اللَّه" and sealed the letter with it (Qurtubi, under the verse in question).

[^1]: The phrase "أَلَّا تَعْلُوا عَلَيَ‌" may collectively be a substitute for "the letter" (kitab) and an explanation of its content, or "an" (أَنْ) here may mean "ay" (أَيْ) for explanation. There is also the possibility that it relates to a hidden verb such as "I advise you" (اوصيكم).

After mentioning the content of Solomon's letter, she turned to her people and "said: O chiefs and advisors! Give me your opinion in this important matter of mine, for I have never decided any important matter without your presence and your opinion!" (قالَتْ يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِي فِي أَمْرِي ما كُنْتُ قاطِعَةً أَمْراً حَتَّى تَشْهَدُونِ‌).

She wanted, through this consultation, to solidify her position among them and draw their opinion toward herself, while also examining the extent of their alignment with her decisions.

"Aftuni" (أفتوني) comes from the root "fatwa," which originally means giving a precise and correct judgment on complex issues. By using this term, the Queen of Sheba both pointed out the complexity of the matter to them and alerted them to the fact that they must exercise precision in expressing their opinions so as not to follow the path of error.

"Tashhadun" (تشهدون) comes from the root "shuhud," meaning "presence"—a presence accompanied by cooperation and consultation.

The "nobles of the people" replied to her, saying: "We are possessors of strength and possessors of great military might, but the final decision is yours; so see what you will command" (قالُوا نَحْنُ أُولُوا قُوَّةٍ وَ أُولُوا بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ وَ الْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ فَانْظُرِي ما ذا تَأْمُرِينَ‌). In this way, they showed both their submission to her orders and their inclination to rely on power and presence on the battlefield!

When the Queen observed their inclination toward war, while she herself was inwardly disinclined toward it, she said—both to quench this thirst for war and to approach the situation in a calculated manner: "Indeed, kings, when they enter a prosperous region, they corrupt it and bring it to ruin!" (قالَتْ إِنَّ الْمُلُوكَ إِذا دَخَلُوا قَرْيَةً أَفْسَدُوها).

"And they make the noble among its people humiliated" (وَ جَعَلُوا أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِها أَذِلَّةً).

They kill some, take others captive, and make a group displaced and homeless, and as much as they can, they resort to looting and pillaging.

Then, for further emphasis, she said: "And thus they do" (وَ كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ‌).

In reality, the Queen of Sheba, who was a monarch herself, knew kings well—that their program is summarized in two things: "corruption and destruction" and "humiliating the noble," because they think of their own interests, not the interests of nations or their prosperity and dignity, and these two are always in opposition to each other.

Then the Queen added: "Before doing anything, we must test Solomon and his associates to see who they truly are. Is Solomon a king or a prophet? Is he a destroyer or a reformer? Does he lead nations to humiliation or to honor? For this purpose, we must use a gift." Therefore, "I am sending a significant gift to them to see what reaction my messengers bring back to us from them" (وَ إِنِّي مُرْسِلَةٌ إِلَيْهِمْ بِهَدِيَّةٍ فَناظِرَةٌ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ الْمُرْسَلُونَ‌).

Kings have a strong passion for "gifts," and this is their point of weakness and vulnerability; they can be made to submit with expensive gifts. If we see that Solomon submits to these gifts, it will be clear that he is a "king"! We will stand against him and rely on our power, for we are strong. But if he shows indifference to us and insists on his words and proposals, it will be clear that he is a Prophet of God; in that case, we must act wisely.

Regarding what gifts the Queen of Sheba sent to Solomon, the Quran says nothing and only shows its greatness by using the indefinite form of the word "gift" (hadiyyah). However, commentators have mentioned many things, which are sometimes not free from exaggeration and legend.

Some have written that she sent five hundred male servants and five hundred female servants of high quality to Solomon, while dressing the males in female clothing and the females in male clothing. She placed earrings in the ears of the males and bracelets on their hands, and beautiful hats on the heads of the females. In her letter, she emphasized: "If you are a prophet, distinguish the males from the females!" She had them ride on precious mounts adorned with gold and jewelry and sent a significant amount of jewels with them.

Additionally, she instructed her messenger: "If, upon arrival, you see Solomon's gaze toward you is angry, know that this is the posture of kings; but if he meets you with a pleasant face and kindness, know that he is a Prophet!"

Key Points

1- The Etiquette of Letter Writing

What appears in the above verses regarding Solomon's letter to the people of Sheba is a model for the style of letter writing, which is sometimes among the most important and decisive matters. It begins with the name of God, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful, and expresses the core of the message with two calculated sentences.

It is well-established from Islamic history and narrations that our great leaders always insisted on writing letters concisely and briefly, free from padding and redundancies, and completely calculated.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, wrote in a circular to his officials and representatives:

"Sharpen your pens, bring your lines closer together, remove redundancies from your letters to me, aim for the core meanings, and beware of wordiness, for the wealth of the Muslims cannot bear such harm" [1]. Sharpening the pens, which causes words to be written smaller, bringing the lines closer, and removing formalities and additions, is not only a saving of public or private funds but also a saving of the time of the writer and the reader. Sometimes it even prevents the essential goal of the letter from being lost amidst formal phrasing.

In recent times, contrary to the practice of early Islam, it became common to fill letters with numerous titles, excessive words, introductions, margins, and additions, wasting precious time and resources.

It is particularly noteworthy that in the conditions of that time, when sending a letter via a special messenger could take weeks and involve significant costs, extreme brevity was nonetheless employed. Examples of this can be seen in the letters of the Prophet of Islam to Khosrow Parviz, the Caesar of Rome, and others.

Fundamentally, a person's letter is evidence of their personality, just as their messenger is. As we read in Nahj al-Balagha from Ali عليه السلام:

"Your messenger is the interpreter of your intellect, and your letter is the most eloquent thing that speaks on your behalf" [1]. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says:

"A man's letter is evidence of his intellect and the extent of his insight, and his messenger is a sign of his understanding and cleverness" [2]. It is also necessary to mention that it is understood from Islamic narrations that responding to a letter is necessary, just like responding to a greeting (Salam).

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Returning the answer to a letter is obligatory, just as returning the Salam is obligatory" [3]. Since every letter is usually accompanied by a greeting, it is not unlikely that it falls under the noble verse: "And when you are greeted with a greeting, greet [in return] with one better than it or return it [in like manner]" (Surah An-Nisa, Verse 86).

2- Did Solomon Call for Blind Imitation?

Some commentators seemingly inferred from the appearance of Solomon's letter that he wanted to force the people of Sheba to accept his call without mentioning proof.

They then answered that the hoopoe's arrival in that miraculous manner was itself proof of the truthfulness of his call [4].

[^1]: Al-Khisal by al-Saduq (as cited in Bihar, Vol. 76, p. 49). [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 301. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 76, p. 50. [^3]: Wasail, Vol. 8, p. 437 (Kitab al-Hajj, Chapters on Social Etiquette, Chapter 33). [^4]: Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, under the verses in question.

However, we believe there is no need for such answers. The duty of a Prophet is to invite, and the duty of others is to investigate. In other words, the invitation is a motivation for investigation, just as the Queen of Sheba did, and she set out to investigate and test Solomon to clarify whether he was a king or a prophet.

3- Profound Indications in Solomon's Story

In this part of Solomon's story, brief indications of important matters are also seen:

  1. The spirit of the Prophets' call is summarized in the negation of haughtiness, which is the negation of any form of colonialism and submission to the law of truth.
  2. While the associates of the Queen of Sheba declared readiness for war, her delicate feminine nature was not in favor of war; therefore, she directed their attention to other matters!
  3. Furthermore, if she had submitted to the warmongering of her associates, she would have remained far from the truth. We will see that her action to test Solomon by sending a gift brought a very good result for herself and for the people of Sheba, causing them to find the path of truth without resorting to bloodshed.
  4. Additionally, it becomes clear from this event that consultative programs do not always lead to the truth, because here the opinion of the majority of her associates was that resorting to military force took precedence, while the Queen of Sheba's opinion was the opposite. At the end of the story, we see that she was right.

It can be said that this type of consultation is different from what is common among us today. We take the majority opinion as the criterion and grant them the right of decision-making, whereas in this type of consultation, the right of decision-making lies with the leader of the group, and the advisors only express their opinions. The verse "And consult them in the matter; then when you have decided, rely upon Allah" (Al-Imran, 159) also refers to this second type of consultation, while Verse 38 of Ash-Shura, "And whose affair is [determined by] consultation among themselves," suggests that the affairs of believers should be conducted through consultation, apparently referring to the first type [1].

  1. The advisors of the Queen of Sheba said to her: "We are possessors of strength (quwwah) and possessors of great military might (ba's shadid)." The difference between the two might be that "quwwah" refers to the vast quantity of the army, and "ba's shadid" refers to the quality of experience and the spirit of courage and bravery of the soldiers. That is, we have full readiness for battle with the enemy in terms of both the "quantity" of the army and its "quality."

6- The Sign of Kings!

It is clearly understood from these verses that monarchy and autocratic rule everywhere cause corruption, ruin, and the humiliation of the noble ones of a nation, because they sideline people of character and invite sycophantic flatterers into service. They seek their own benefit and profit in everything; they are people of gifts, bribery, and gold and jewelry, and naturally, oppressive tyrants who have access to these things are more beloved to them.

Kings have their thoughts and hearts pledged to status, gifts, and gold and jewelry, whereas Prophets think of nothing but the welfare of nations.

[^1]: For further explanation regarding "consultation," refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 142 to 149.

Ayahs

فَلَمَّا جَآءَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ قَالَ أَتُمِدُّونَنِ بِمَالٍ فَمَآ ءَاتَىٰنِۦَ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّآ ءَاتَىٰكُم بَلْ أَنتُم بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ (٣٦)
So when he came to Solomon, he said, ‘Are you aiding me with wealth? What Allah has given me is better than what He has given you. Indeed, you are proud of your gift! (36)
ٱرْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم بِجُنُودٍ لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بِهَا وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم مِّنْهَآ أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ (٣٧)
Go back to them, for we will come at them with hosts which they cannot face, and we will expel them from it, abased and degraded.’ (37)

Commentary

Do Not Deceive Me with Wealth!

The envoys of the Queen of Sheba, accompanied by a caravan of gifts, left the land of Yemen and headed toward Sham and the headquarters of Solomon, imagining that Solomon would be delighted by the sight of these gifts and would welcome them with joy.

However, as soon as they encountered Solomon, a strange scene unfolded before them. Solomon not only refrained from welcoming them but said: "Do you intend to help me with wealth? While these possessions are worthless in my sight,

What Allah has bestowed upon me is better and more precious than what He has given you" (أَ تُمِدُّونَنِ بِمالٍ فَما آتانِيَ اللَّهُ خَيْرٌ مِمَّا آتاكُمْ‌).

What value does wealth hold compared to the station of prophethood, knowledge, wisdom, guidance, and piety?

"It is you who rejoice in your gifts" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ‌).

Yes, it is you who, whenever you send such glittering and expensive gifts to one another, become so elated that the spark of joy appears in your eyes; but these are insignificant and of little value in my view.

In this manner, Solomon belittled their standards of value and made it clear that there are other criteria for worth, compared to which the well-known standards of world-worshippers are colorless and worthless.

Then, to demonstrate his decisiveness in the matter of "Truth and Falsehood," he said to the special envoy of the Queen of Sheba: "Return to them (and take these gifts back with you), but know that we will soon come to them with armies that they will have no power to resist" (ارْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُمْ بِجُنُودٍ لا قِبَلَ لَهُمْ بِها). "And we shall certainly drive them out from that prosperous land in disgrace, and they will be humbled and abased" (وَ لَنُخْرِجَنَّهُمْ مِنْها أَذِلَّةً وَ هُمْ صاغِرُونَ‌).

"Disgraced" (أذلة) is actually the first state, and "they will be humbled" (وَ هُمْ صاغِرُونَ‌) is the second state, indicating that not only will we expel them from their land, but we will do so in a state of humiliation and abasement, such that they will lose all their palaces, wealth, and grandeur, because they did not submit to the religion of Truth and instead resorted to trickery and deception.

Of course, this threat was a serious and significant one for the envoys who had seen Solomon's situation up close and observed his army and military forces.

Considering what we read in the previous verses—that Solomon had demanded two things from them: "abandoning superiority" and "submission to the Truth"—their failure to respond to these two commands and their recourse to sending gifts was evidence of their refusal to accept the Truth and their persistence in arrogance. For this reason, he threatened them with military pressure.

Had the Queen of Sheba and her associates requested evidence, proof, or a miracle, he would have given them the right to investigate further. However, sending a gift appeared to be an act of denial.

We also know that the most significant distressing news the Hoopoe gave Solomon about this people was that they were sun-worshippers; they had abandoned the Great Allah, who has authority over the unseen and the seen of the heavens and the earth, and were prostrating before a created being.

Solomon was troubled by this issue, and we know that idolatry is not something that divine religions remain silent about, nor do they tolerate idolaters as a religious minority. Rather, if necessary, they will resort to force to destroy idol temples and uproot the creed of polytheism and idolatry.

From the explanations provided above, it becomes clear that Solomon's threat does not contradict the fundamental principle of "There is no compulsion in religion" (لا إِكْراهَ فِي الدِّينِ‌), because idolatry is not a religion but rather a superstition and a deviation.

Key Points [Asceticism in the Logic of Divine Religions]

  1. It is noteworthy that asceticism (Zuhd) in the logic of divine religions does not mean that a person should be deprived of wealth, riches, and worldly facilities. Rather, the reality of asceticism is that one should not become a "prisoner" to these things, but rather be a "commander" over them. Solomon, this great Prophet of Allah, by rejecting the precious gifts of the Queen of Sheba, showed that he was a "commander," not a "prisoner"! In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"The world is too insignificant in the sight of Allah and in the sight of His Prophets and His Awliya (saints) for them to rejoice over anything from it or to grieve over its loss. Therefore, it is not fitting for a scholar or a wise person to rejoice over the fleeting goods of the world." [^1]

  1. Again, in this part of Solomon's story, there are significant lessons hidden within the meaningful expressions of the verses:

A: The goal of military mobilization is not the slaughter of humans; rather, the goal is for the enemy to perceive themselves in a weak position and see no power to resist (بِجُنُودٍ لا قِبَلَ لَهُمْ بِها).

This expression is similar to what Muslims have been commanded: "And prepare against them whatever force you can... to strike terror into the enemy of Allah" (وَ أَعِدُّوا لَهُمْ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ ...تُرْهِبُونَ بِهِ عَدُوَّ اللَّهِ‌) (Al-Anfal: 60).

B: Solomon does not threaten his opponents with death; rather, he threatens to drive them

[^1]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan," under the verse being discussed.

out from their palaces and castles in disgrace and humiliation, and this is noteworthy.

C: Solomon does not take his opponents by surprise; rather, he explicitly informs them of his impending attack beforehand.

D: Solomon has no greed for the wealth of others; instead, he says that what Allah has given him is better. He does not summarize divine blessings in material and financial power; he takes pride in knowledge, faith, and spiritual blessings.

Ayahs

قَالَ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ أَيُّكُمْ يَأْتِينِى بِعَرْشِهَا قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ (٣٨)
He said, ‘O [members of the] elite! Which of you will bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?’ (38)
قَالَ عِفْرِيتٌ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن تَقُومَ مِن مَّقَامِكَ ۖ وَإِنِّى عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِىٌّ أَمِينٌ (٣٩)
An afreet from among the jinn said, ‘I will bring it to you before you rise from your place. I have the power to do it and am trustworthy.’ (39)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِى عِندَهُۥ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِندَهُۥ قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّى لِيَبْلُوَنِىٓ ءَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ ۖ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّى غَنِىٌّ كَرِيمٌ (٤٠)
The one who had knowledge of the Book said, ‘I will bring it to you in the twinkling of an eye.’ So when he saw it set near him, he said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord, to test me if I will give thanks or be ungrateful. Whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful [should know that] my Lord is indeed all-sufficient, all-generous.’ (40)

Commentary

In the Twinkling of an Eye, Her Throne is Present!

Finally, the envoys of the Queen of Sheba## Commentary

In the Twinkling of an Eye, Her Throne is Present!

Finally, the envoys of the Queen of Sheba packed up their gifts and belongings and returned to their country. They described the events to the "Queen" and her inner circle, recounting the miraculous grandeur of Solomon’s (سليمان) kingdom and his administration. Each of these details served as proof that he was no ordinary individual or mere king; he was truly a messenger of Allah, and his government was a divine one.

At this point, it became clear to them that not only were they incapable of military confrontation with him, but even if they could theoretically resist, they would likely be opposing a powerful Prophet of God.

Therefore, the Queen of Sheba, along with a group of her nation's nobles, decided to travel to Solomon to personally investigate this important matter and discern what faith Solomon followed.

This news reached Solomon through whatever means, and he decided to perform a magnificent display of power while the Queen and her companions were en route. His goal was to further acquaint them with the reality of his miracles and make them submit to his invitation.

Thus, Solomon turned to those around him and "said: 'O chiefs! Which of you can bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?'" (قَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَيُّكُمْ يَأْتِينِي بِعَرْشِهَا قَبْلَ أَنْ يَأْتُونِي مُسْلِمِينَ).

Although some commentators have struggled to find the reason for summoning the Queen of Sheba's throne—sometimes mentioning possibilities that do not align with the context of the verses—it is clear what Solomon’s objective was. He wanted, so to speak, to show his "strength of hand" and perform an extraordinary feat to pave the way for their unconditional surrender and their faith in the power of Allah, thereby eliminating the need for the battlefield and bloodshed.

He wanted faith to penetrate the depths of the Queen of Sheba's soul and those of her companions, so they might invite others to submit and embrace the faith as well.

At this point, two individuals declared their readiness: one was remarkable, and the other even more so. First, "An 'Ifrit (a powerful being) from among the Jinn turned to Solomon and said: 'I will bring it to you before you rise from your place'" (قَالَ عِفْرِيتٌ مِنَ الْجِنِّ أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَقُومَ مِنْ مَقَامِكَ). [^1] I will not perform this task with difficulty, nor will I commit any betrayal regarding this precious treasure, because "I am indeed strong and trustworthy for it!" (وَإِنِّي عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِيٌّ أَمِينٌ).

"Ifrit" (عفريت) refers to a rebellious and wicked individual. The phrase "وَإِنِّي عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِيٌّ أَمِينٌ", which is emphasized in several ways (the particle inna, the nominal sentence structure, and the lam of emphasis), also indicates that there might have been a fear of betrayal from this Ifrit; hence, he rose to defend himself and promised trustworthiness and loyalty.

In any case, the story of Solomon is full of wonders and supernatural events, and it is not surprising that such an Ifrit could perform such a significant task in a short period—perhaps one or a few hours—while Solomon sat in his assembly to judge among the people, attend to state affairs, or provide counsel and guidance.

[^1]: The word "آتِي" (Ati) could be a "subject active participle" (Ism Fa'il) or a "present/future verb" (Fi'l Mudari') from the root "aty," but the first possibility seems more appropriate (reflect on this).

The second person was a righteous man who possessed significant knowledge of the "Divine Book," as the Quran says regarding him: "One who had knowledge of the Book said: 'I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you'!!" (قَالَ الَّذِي عِنْدَهُ عِلْمٌ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ).

When Solomon agreed to this, the man used his spiritual power to bring the Queen of Sheba's throne before him in the "twinkling of an eye." "When Solomon saw it placed firmly before him, he opened his tongue in gratitude to his Lord and said: 'This is by the grace of my Lord, that He may test me whether I am grateful or ungrateful'!" (فَلَمَّا رَآهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِنْدَهُ قَالَ هَذَا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ).

He then added: "And whoever is grateful, he is only grateful for [the benefit of] his own soul; and whoever is ungrateful, then surely my Lord is Self-Sufficient, Bountiful" (وَمَنْ شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّي غَنِيٌّ كَرِيمٌ).

Regarding who this person was, where he obtained this extraordinary power, and what is meant by "knowledge of the Book," commentators have discussed much.

However, it appears that this person was one of the believing close associates and special friends of Solomon. In historical accounts, his name is often written as "Asif bin Barkhiya" (آصف بن برخيا), and they say he was Solomon's vizier and his nephew. [^1] As for "knowledge of the Book," it refers to his awareness of the Heavenly Books—a deep knowledge that enabled him to perform such a supernatural act. Some have suggested it refers to the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh al-Mahfuz), the tablet of Allah's knowledge, of which this man knew a portion, and for this reason, he was able to bring the Queen of Sheba's throne to Solomon in the twinkling of an eye.

Many commentators and others have said that this believing man was aware of the "Greatest Name of Allah" (Ism al-A'zam)—that great name before which everything humbles itself and which grants a human extraordinary power.

It is also necessary to mention that awareness of the Greatest Name, contrary to what many imagine, does not mean that a person simply speaks a word and it produces all those strange and great effects. Rather, it means embodying that name and attribute; that is, implementing that Divine Name within one's own soul and evolving so much in terms of awareness, ethics, piety, and faith that one becomes a manifestation of that name. This spiritual and soul-level perfection, which is a reflection of that Greatest Name of Allah, creates the power for such supernatural acts in a human. [^1] Regarding the phrase "قَبْلَ أَنْ يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ" (before your glance returns to you), commentators have offered various possibilities, but its reality can be understood by looking at other verses of the Quran. In Surah Ibrahim, verse 43, we read: "لَا يَرْتَدُّ إِلَيْهِمْ طَرْفُهُمْ" (their glance does not return to them). On the Day of Resurrection, people will be so terrified that their eyes will stare fixedly, and even their eyelids will not blink (we know that in a state of terror, the human eye remains open in a uniform, staring state like the eyes of the dead).

Therefore, the meaning was: "Before you blink your eye, I will bring the Queen of Sheba's throne before you." [^2]

[^1]: The claim by some that he was Gabriel (جبرئيل) or Solomon himself is without evidence; as for him being Solomon himself, it certainly contradicts the apparent meaning of the verses.

[^1]: We have also discussed the "Greatest Name of Allah" in Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 30 (under verse 180 of Surah Al-A'raf).

[^2]: The suggestion by some that the phrase "يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ" refers to looking at something and the return of the vision to the person and his awareness of it has no basis. Similarly, this expression is not evidence for the theory of "rays exiting the eye" mentioned in ancient philosophy (reflect on this).

Key Points

1- Answer to Several Questions: [Why did Solomon not personally bring the throne?]

One of the questions raised regarding the above verses is: why did Solomon not personally perform this supernatural act? Since he was a great Prophet of God and possessed miracles, why did he assign this mission to "Asif bin Barkhiya"?

It may have been because Asif was his successor (Wasi), and Solomon wanted to introduce his position to everyone at this sensitive moment. [^1] Furthermore, it is important for a teacher to test his students at necessary times and reveal their merits. Fundamentally, the merit of the students is a great proof of the merit of the teacher; if the students perform an extraordinary task, it is significant.

[Why did he summon the throne without its owner's permission?]

Another question: How did Solomon bring the Queen of Sheba's throne to himself without her permission?

It may have been for a greater goal, such as the matter of their guidance and showing a great miracle. Beyond this, we know that kings do not truly own wealth; their properties are usually obtained by usurping the rights of others!

[How did the Ifrit have the ability for this supernatural act?]

Another question: How does an Ifrit of the Jinn have the ability for such a supernatural feat?

[^1]: In a detailed narration quoted in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from the tenth Imam, Ali bin Muhammad al-Hadi عليه السلام, we read this same answer in response to Yahya bin Aktham (Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 91).

We have answered this question in discussions related to miracles: sometimes even non-believers, through arduous spiritual exercises and struggling against the self, gain the ability to perform certain supernatural acts. However, the difference between this and miracles is that their work, being reliant on limited human power, is always limited, whereas miracles rely on the infinite power of Allah, and His power, like His other attributes, is unlimited.

Thus, we see the Ifrit limits his ability to bringing the throne during the time Solomon remains in his assembly for judgment and state affairs, whereas "Asif bin Barkhiya" sets no such limit. Limiting it to the "twinkling of an eye" is, in fact, an allusion to the shortest possible time. It is certain that Solomon would support such an act that introduces a righteous person, rather than the work of an Ifrit which might lead the short-sighted to error and cause them to take it as proof of his purity.

It is obvious that anyone who performs an important task in society and is accepted will promote their line of thought and belief through it. In the divine government of Solomon, the initiative must not fall into the hands of Ifrits; rather, those who have knowledge from the Divine Book must govern the thoughts and emotions of the people.

2- Power and Trustworthiness: Two Important Conditions

In the above verses, as well as in "Surah Al-Qasas, verse 26," the two most important conditions for an exemplary employee or worker are stated: first, strength and ability, and second, trustworthiness and integrity.

Of course, sometimes a person's intellectual and moral foundations necessitate these qualities (as mentioned regarding Moses in Surah Al-Qasas), and sometimes the system of society and a righteous government necessitates that even an Ifrit of the Jinn be compulsorily characterized by these two traits. In any case, no task, large or small, can be accomplished in society without these two conditions, whether they stem from piety or from the legal system of society (reflect on this).

3- The Difference Between "Knowledge FROM the Book" and "Knowledge OF the Book"

In the verses under discussion, the person who brought the Queen of Sheba's throne in the shortest time is described as "مَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمٌ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ" (one who possessed a portion of the knowledge of the Book). In contrast, in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 43, regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the witnesses to his truthfulness, it says: "قُلْ كَفَى بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ" (Say: "Allah is sufficient as a witness between me and you, and [so is] he who possesses the knowledge of the Book").

In a tradition from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Abu Sa'id says: "I asked him about the meaning of 'الَّذِي عِنْدَهُ عِلْمٌ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ' (which appears in the story of Solomon). He said: 'He was the successor of my brother Solomon bin David.' I asked: 'Of whom does 'وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ' speak?' He replied: 'That is my brother Ali bin Abi Talib (علي بن ابي طالب)'!" [^1] Noting the difference between "knowledge from the Book," which denotes partial knowledge, and "knowledge of the Book," which denotes universal knowledge, makes it clear how much difference there was between "Asif" and "Ali" عليه السلام!

Thus, we read in many narrations that the Greatest Name of Allah consists of seventy-three letters, one of which was with "Asif bin Barkhiya," and he performed such a supernatural feat.

[^1]: This tradition has been narrated by a group of commentators and scholars of the Ahl al-Sunnah with this exact wording or similar. For further explanation, refer to Ihqaq al-Haqq, Vol. 3, pp. 280-281.

And with the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام are seventy-two letters of it, while one letter is exclusive to the Pure Essence of Allah. [^1]

4- "This is by the Grace of my Lord"

Arrogant world-seekers, when they attain power, forget everything but themselves. They attribute all the resources they have acquired to themselves—like Qarun (Korah) who said: "إِنَّمَا أُوتِيتُهُ عَلَى عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي" ("I have only been given it because of the knowledge I possess" - Surah Al-Qasas, verse 78). In contrast, the special servants of Allah, wherever they reach, say: "هَذَا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي" ("This is by the grace of my Lord").

Interestingly, Solomon did not only say this upon seeing the Queen of Sheba's throne before him, but he added that this is so Allah may test him: am I grateful or not?

Earlier in this same Surah, we read that Solomon considers all his blessings to be from Allah and humbly turns to His threshold, saying: "O Lord! Inspire me to be grateful for all these blessings and grant me the success to be able to seek Your pleasure through them."

Yes, this is the criterion for distinguishing sincere monotheists from arrogant world-seekers, and this is the way of men of great capacity and character compared to the self-centered of little capacity.

Although it has become common for some pretenders to write only this meaningful phrase of Solomon (هَذَا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي) over the entrances of their tyrannical palaces without believing in it—and with little reflection of it in their actions—the important thing is that it should be both over the door of the house and on the forehead of one's entire life and in one's heart. One's actions should show that they consider everything to be from the grace of Allah and strive to be grateful for it—not just gratitude with the tongue, but gratitude with action and with one's entire being. [^1]

5- How did Asif bin Barkhiya bring the throne?

This is not the first supernatural event we see in the story of Solomon or in the lives of the Prophets in general. Those who think they must alter such expressions with justifications and interpretations away from their literal meaning, giving them metaphorical or spiritual aspects, must settle their account once and for all with the miracles of the Prophets.

Do they truly consider the performance of supernatural acts by Prophets or their successors to be impossible and deny it entirely? Such a view is compatible neither with the principle of Monotheism and the power of the Lord who governs the laws of existence, nor with the explicit text of the Quran in many verses.

But if they accept that such a thing is possible, it makes no difference whether the discussion is about raising the dead and healing the blind from birth by Christ عليه السلام, or bringing the throne of the Queen of Sheba by Asif bin Barkhiya.

Undoubtedly, mysterious relationships and unknown causes are at work here that we, with our limited knowledge, are unaware of; but we know this much: this task is not impossible.

Did Asif, with his spiritual power, transform the Queen of Sheba's throne into light waves, bring it there in an instant, and then transform it back into its original matter? It is not exactly clear to us.

[^1]: Refer to Usul al-Kafi and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn.

[^1]: Regarding the importance of gratitude and its effect on the increase of blessings and the types of gratitude (existential and legislative gratitude), a detailed discussion is found in Volume 10, page 278 onwards (under verse 7 of Surah Ibrahim).

We only know that today, through common scientific methods, man performs tasks that two hundred years ago might have been considered among the impossibilities. For example, if someone several centuries ago were told that a time would come when a person speaks in the East and in the West at that very moment their words are heard and their face is seen by all, they would have considered it delirium or a disturbed dream.

This is because man wants to evaluate everything with his own limited knowledge and power, while beyond his knowledge and power, many secrets lie hidden.

Ayahs

قَالَ نَكِّرُوا۟ لَهَا عَرْشَهَا نَنظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِىٓ أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ (٤١)
He said, ‘Disguise her throne for her, so that we may see whether she is discerning or if she is one of the undiscerning ones.’ (41)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ قِيلَ أَهَـٰكَذَا عَرْشُكِ ۖ قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ ۚ وَأُوتِينَا ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ (٤٢)
So when she came, it was said [to her], ‘Is your throne like this one?’ She said, ‘It seems to be the same, and we were informed before it, and we had submitted.’ (42)
وَصَدَّهَا مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٤٣)
She had been barred [from the way of Allah] by what she used to worship besides Allah, for she belonged to a faithless people. (43)
قِيلَ لَهَا ٱدْخُلِى ٱلصَّرْحَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَكَشَفَتْ عَن سَاقَيْهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ صَرْحٌ مُّمَرَّدٌ مِّن قَوَارِيرَ ۗ قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٤)
It was said to her, ‘Enter the palace.’ So when she saw it, she supposed it to be a pool of water, and she bared her shanks. He said, ‘It is a palace paved with crystal.’ She said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’ (44)

Commentary

The Light of Faith in the Heart of the Queen of Sheba

In these verses, we encounter another scene from the cautionary saga of "Solomon" (peace be upon him) and the "Queen of Sheba."

To test the intellect and discernment of the Queen of Sheba, and also to pave the way for her faith in God, Solomon ordered that her throne—which had already been brought—be altered and made unrecognizable: "He said: 'Disguise her throne for her; let us see whether she will be guided [to recognize it] or will be of those who are not guided.'" (قالَ نَكِّرُوا لَها عَرْشَها نَنْظُرْ أَ تَهْتَدِي أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ لا يَهْتَدُونَ).

Although the mere transport of the throne from the land of Sheba to Syria was enough to make it difficult for her to recognize easily, Solomon nevertheless ordered changes to be made to it. These changes might have involved rearranging some of its features and jewels, or altering some of its colors and the like.

However, the question arises: what was Solomon's objective in testing the intelligence and discernment of the Queen of Sheba?

It is possible the test was conducted so he would know with what logic he should confront her, and what kind of evidence he should provide to prove the foundations of belief to her.

Or perhaps he intended to propose marriage to her and wanted to see if she truly possessed the merit to be his spouse. Or he truly intended to entrust her with a responsibility after she embraced faith, and thus needed to know the extent of her capacity to accept responsibilities.

Regarding the phrase "whether she will be guided" (أَ تَهْتَدِي), two interpretations have been mentioned: some have said it refers to recognizing her own throne, while others have said it refers to being guided to the path of God by witnessing this miracle.

However, the first meaning appears more apparent, even though the first meaning itself served as a prelude to the second.

In any case, "when the Queen of Sheba arrived, someone gestured toward the throne and said: 'Is your throne like this?'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَتْ قِيلَ أَ هكَذا عَرْشُكِ). It appears that the speaker was not Solomon himself; otherwise, the expression "it was said" (قِيلَ) would not be appropriate, as Solomon's name appears before and after this, and his words are introduced with "he said" (قالَ).

Furthermore, it would not have befitted Solomon's majesty to initiate such a conversation immediately upon her arrival.

Regardless, the Queen of Sheba gave the most clever and calculated of answers and "said: 'It is as if it were it!'" (قالَتْ كَأَنَّهُ هُوَ).

If she had said, "It is similar to it," she would have erred; and if she had said, "It is exactly it," it would have been a statement lacking caution, because given such a vast distance, the arrival of her throne in Solomon's land was impossible through ordinary means, unless a miracle had occurred.

Beyond this, it is mentioned in histories that she had placed her precious throne in a secure location, in her private palace, in a room guarded by many sentries and secured with sturdy doors.

Yet, despite all this, the Queen of Sheba was able to recognize it notwithstanding all the alterations made to the throne.

And she immediately added: "And we were given knowledge before this, and we had [already] submitted [to Allah]!" (وَ أُوتِينَا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِها وَ كُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ).

Meaning: if Solomon's purpose with these arrangements is for us to realize his miraculous power, we had already become aware of his truthfulness through other signs, and we had embraced faith even before seeing this extraordinary wonder; thus, there was not much need for this act.

And in this way, Solomon "prevented her from what she used to worship besides Allah" (وَ صَدَّها ما كانَتْ تَعْبُدُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ) [1], even though "before that, she was of a disbelieving people" (إِنَّها كانَتْ مِنْ قَوْمٍ كافِرِينَ).

[1]: Regarding who the subject of "prevented" (صدّ) is, and whether "ma" (ما) in "ma kanat" is a relative pronoun or an infinitive particle, commentators have said much. A group considers the subject to be Solomon, as we mentioned above, and some consider it to be Allah—which does not differ much in terms of the result. According to these two interpretations, "ha" (her) is the first object, and "ma kanat" stands in place of the second object with the preposition omitted. The estimation would be: (Solomon prevented her) or (Allah prevented her from what she used to worship besides Allah). However, another group considers "ma kanat..." to be the subject of "prevented," making the meaning: "Her idols had prevented her from worshipping the Truth." But considering that the context here is about her faith, not her disbelief, the first interpretation seems more appropriate. The word "ma" could be either a relative pronoun or an infinitive particle.

Indeed, by witnessing these clear signs, she bid farewell to her dark past and stepped into a new stage of life filled with the light of faith and certainty.

In the final verse under discussion, another scene of this event is recounted: the entry of the Queen of Sheba into Solomon's special palace. Solomon had ordered the courtyard of one of the palaces to be made of crystal, with running water placed beneath it.

When the Queen of Sheba arrived there, "she was told: 'Enter the palace courtyard'" (قِيلَ لَهَا ادْخُلِي الصَّرْحَ) [1]. When the Queen saw that scene, she thought it was a pool of water and bared her shins to pass through that water (while she was deeply astonished as to what a stream of water was doing there) "But when she saw it, she thought it was a body of water and bared her shins" (فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَ كَشَفَتْ عَنْ ساقَيْها) [2]. But "Solomon said to her: 'It is a palace courtyard paved with smooth crystal'" (this is not water that requires baring the feet to pass through) (قالَ إِنَّهُ صَرْحٌ مُمَرَّدٌ مِنْ قَوارِيرَ) [3]. Here, an important question arises: why would Solomon, who was a great Prophet of Allah, possess such extraordinary luxury and grandeur? It is true...

[1]: "Sarh" (صرح) sometimes means a vast open space, and sometimes a lofty building or high palace; here it apparently means the palace courtyard. [2]: "Lujjah" (لجة) originally comes from the root "lujaj" meaning persistence in doing something; then it was applied to the resonance of sound in the throat, and the dense waves of the sea that move back and forth are also called "lujjah." In the verse under discussion, it refers to deep, surging water. [3]: "Mumarrad" (ممرّد) means smooth, and "qawarir" (قوارير) is the plural of "qarurah," meaning crystal or glass.

...that he was a sultan and a ruler, but could he not have had a simple lifestyle like other prophets?

However, what is the harm if Solomon—in order to bring to submission the Queen of Sheba, who saw all her power and greatness in a beautiful throne, crown, magnificent palace, and glittering establishment—showed her a scene that would make all her luxurious apparatus seem trivial and small in her eyes? This could be a turning point in her life to reconsider her values and criteria for character. What is the harm if, instead of launching a costly and bloody military campaign, he so stunned and overwhelmed the Queen's mind and intellect that she would not even think of such a thing—especially since she was a woman and gave importance to such ceremonial matters?

Particularly, many commentators have specified that Solomon ordered such a palace to be built before the Queen of Sheba reached the land of Syria, and his goal was a display of power to bring about her submission. This act showed that a great power in terms of outward strength was at Solomon's disposal, enabling him to achieve such feats.

In other words, this expense was not a significant matter compared to the security and peace of a vast region, the acceptance of the true religion, and the prevention of the extraordinary costs of war.

Therefore, when the Queen of Sheba saw this scene, "she said: 'My Lord! Indeed, I have wronged myself!'" (قالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي).

"And I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (وَ أَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

In the past, I prostrated to the sun, I worshipped idols, I was drowned in luxury and adornment, and I considered myself the highest human in the world.

But now I understand how small my power was, and fundamentally, these gold and ornaments...

...do not satisfy the human soul.

O Allah! I have come to Your threshold along with my leader Solomon; I am regretful of the past and I bow my head in submission to Your threshold.

It is interesting that she uses the word "with" (مع) here (along with Solomon) to make it clear that on the path of God, all are brothers and equals—unlike the way of tyrants where some dominate others and a group is captive in the clutches of another. Here, there is no victor or vanquished; everyone stands in one rank after accepting the Truth.

It is true that the Queen of Sheba had declared her faith before this as well, for we heard from her in the previous verses: "And we were given knowledge before this, and we had [already] submitted [to Allah]" (وَ أُوتِينَا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِها وَ كُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ).

But here, the Queen's Islam reached its peak; therefore, she declared her submission with greater emphasis.

She had previously seen numerous signs of the truthfulness of Solomon's call: - The arrival of the Hoopoe in that specific manner. - The refusal of the massive gift sent by the Queen. - The bringing of her throne from that far distance in a short time. - And finally, witnessing Solomon's extraordinary power and greatness, yet combined with a specific character that bore no resemblance to the character of kings.

Key Points

1. The Ultimate Fate of the Queen of Sheba

What is mentioned in the Holy Quran regarding the Queen of Sheba is only as much as we read above: she eventually believed and joined the ranks of the righteous. But as to whether she returned to her country after believing and continued her rule on behalf of Solomon? Or stayed with Solomon and married him? Or, upon Solomon's recommendation, entered into marriage with one of the kings of Yemen known as "Tubba'"? The Quran makes no mention of these, as they do not pertain to the primary goal of the Quran, which is educational and moral matters. However, commentators and historians have each chosen a path, the investigation of which is not necessary. Although according to some commentators, the most famous and well-known account is her marriage to Solomon [1], we find it necessary to remind that many legends and myths have been told about Solomon, his army, and his government, as well as the characteristics of the Queen of Sheba and the details of her life. Sometimes distinguishing these from historical facts becomes difficult for the masses, and sometimes they cast a dark shadow over the essence of this historical event, damaging its authenticity. This is the sinister result of superstitions being mixed with truths, which must be carefully guarded against.

2. A General Summary of Solomon's Story

The portion of Solomon's life mentioned in the above 30 verses expresses many issues, some of which we read within the discussions, and we will make a passing reference to another part below:

  1. This story begins with the gift of abundant knowledge that Allah placed at the disposal of Solomon and David, and ends with monotheism (Tawhid) and submission to the command of the Lord—a monotheism whose foundation is also "knowledge."

  2. This story shows that sometimes the disappearance of a bird and its exceptional flight over a region can change the course of a nation's history, leading them from polytheism to faith and from corruption to righteousness. This is an example of the Lord's display of power and an example of the government of Truth!

  3. This story shows that the light of monotheism shines in all hearts, and even a seemingly silent bird reports on the deep secrets of monotheism.

  4. To make a human aware of their true value and to guide them toward Allah, one must first break their pride and arrogance so that the dark veils are removed from before their realistic eyes—just as Solomon broke the Queen of Sheba's pride by doing two things: bringing her throne and causing her to err regarding the construction of part of the palace!

  5. The ultimate goal in the government of the Prophets is not territorial expansion; rather, the goal is what we read in the last verse above: that the rebellious confess their sins and bow their heads before the Lord of the Worlds. Therefore, the Quran ends the story with this very point.

  6. The spirit of "faith" is "submission" (Taslim); for this reason, Solomon emphasizes it in his letter, and the Queen of Sheba emphasizes it at the end of the matter.

  7. Sometimes a human possessing the greatest possible power may find themselves in need of a weak creature like a bird; they take help not only from its knowledge but also from its work. And sometimes an ant, with all its weakness and inability, humbles him!

  8. The revelation of these verses in Mecca, when Muslims were under severe pressure from enemies and all doors were closed to them, had a specific meaning. Its meaning was to strengthen their morale, comfort them, and make them hopeful of the Lord's grace and mercy and future victories.

[1]: Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَإِذَا هُمْ فَرِيقَانِ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٤٥)
Certainly We sent to Thamud Salih, their brother, [with the summons:] ‘Worship Allah!’ But thereat they became two groups contending with each other. (45)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لِمَ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ ۖ لَوْلَا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٤٦)
He said, ‘O My people! Why do you press for evil sooner than for good? Why do you not plead to Allah for forgiveness so that you may receive His mercy?’ (46)
قَالُوا۟ ٱطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَن مَّعَكَ ۚ قَالَ طَـٰٓئِرُكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ (٤٧)
They said, ‘We take you and those who are with you for a bad omen.’ He said, ‘Your bad omens are from Allah. Indeed, you are a people being tested.’ (47)

Commentary

Salih Against the People of Thamud

Following the mention of parts of the life stories of "Moses," "David," and "Solomon" in the preceding verses, the fourth prophet whose life and people are discussed in this Surah is Prophet "Salih" and the people of Thamud.

First, He says: "And We sent to Thamud their brother Salih, [saying], 'Worship Allah'" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا إِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَالِحًا أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ) [^1]. As mentioned previously, the expression "their brother" (أخاهم), which appears in the stories of many prophets, refers to their utmost love and compassion toward their people; in some cases, it additionally refers to their kinship with those tribes.

In any case, the entire mission and call of this great prophet is summarized in the phrase "Worship Allah" (أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ); indeed, the servitude of God is the essence of all the teachings of the Lord's messengers.

Then He adds: "But at once they were two parties quarreling with each other" (one side being the believers and the other the stubborn deniers) (فَإِذَا هُمْ فَرِيقَانِ يَخْتَصِمُونَ) [^2]. In Surah Al-A'raf, verse 75, these two groups are referred to as the "arrogant" and the "oppressed":

"The eminent among those who were arrogant from his people said to those who were oppressed—to those who believed among them, 'Do you [actually] know that Salih is sent from his Lord?' They said, 'Indeed we, in that with which he was sent, are believers.' Those who were arrogant said, 'Indeed we, in that which you have believed, are disbelievers'" (Al-A'raf: 75-76).

Of course, this conflict between the two groups of believers and disbelievers holds true for many prophets, although some were deprived of even this many supporters, and almost everyone joined the ranks of the deniers.

To awaken them, Salih began to warn them and cautioned them against painful divine punishments. However, not only did they fail to take heed and wake up, but they used this very point as a pretext for their stubbornness, insistently asking him: "If you are truthful, why does the divine punishment not overtake us?" (This matter is explicitly mentioned in verse 77 of Surah Al-A'raf).

But Salih said to them: "O my people, why are you impatient for evil before good?" (قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ لِمَ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ الْحَسَنَةِ).

Why do you focus all your thoughts on the arrival of divine punishment? If the divine punishment overtakes you, it will end your lives, and no opportunity for faith will remain. Come and test the truth of my words through the blessings and divine mercy that He promises you under the shadow of faith. "Why do you not seek forgiveness of Allah that you may receive mercy?" (لَوْلَا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ).

Why do you only pursue evil and request the descent of punishment? What is the reason for this stubbornness and obstinacy?

It was not only the people of Salih who, in the position of denying his call, requested the promised punishment; this matter is seen repeatedly in the Holy Quran, including regarding the people of Hud (Al-A'raf: 70).

Regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and some of the prejudiced and stubborn polytheists, we read:

"And [remember] when they said, 'O Allah, if this should be the truth from You, then rain down upon us stones from the sky or bring us a painful punishment'" (Al-Anfal: 32).

And it is truly strange that a human would want to test the truthfulness of a claimant to prophethood through destructive punishment rather than through a request for mercy, while they certainly felt the possibility of the truthfulness of these prophets in their hearts, even if they denied it with their tongues.

This is exactly like a person claiming to be a physician, saying, "This medicine is healing, and that medicine is lethal," and for the sake of testing him, we go for the medicine he described as lethal, not the healing one.

This is the height of ignorance and prejudice, but ignorance produces many such results.

In any case, instead of listening with their souls to the compassionate advice of this great prophet and acting upon it, these rebellious people rose to oppose him with a series of vain words and baseless conclusions, including that "They said, 'We consider you a bad omen, you and those with you'" (قَالُوا اطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَنْ مَعَكَ).

Apparently, that year was one of drought and shortage of crops and food supplies. They said, "These troubles and problems of ours are all due to your unlucky arrival and that of your companions; you are an ill-omened people and have brought misfortune to our society." By resorting to the weapon of the "bad omen"—which is the weapon of superstitious and stubborn individuals—they wanted to crush his powerful logic.

But in response, he said: "Your omen [i.e., fate] is with Allah" (قَالَ طَائِرُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ).

It is He who has afflicted you with these calamities because of your deeds, and it is your actions that have caused such punishment before Him.

This is, in fact, a great divine trial for you; indeed, "rather, you are a people being tried" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ).

These are divine trials; these are warnings and wake-up calls, so that those who possess the merit and capacity may wake up from the sleep of negligence, correct their wrong path, and return to Allah.

Key Points "Tatiyyur" and "Tafa'ul"

"Tatiyyur" (تطير), as we know, is derived from the root "tayr" (طير), meaning bird. Since the Arabs often took omens from birds, the term "tatiyyur" came to mean taking a bad omen, in contrast to "tafa'ul" (تفال), which means taking a good omen.

The Quran repeatedly mentions that superstitious polytheists resorted to this weapon against divine prophets. For instance, regarding Moses عليه السلام and his companions, we read: "But if evil afflicted them, they ascribed it to evil omens from Moses and those with him" (Al-A'raf: 131).

In the verses under discussion, the polytheists of the people of Thamud showed the same reaction toward Salih.

And in Surah Ya-Sin, we read that against the messengers of Christ (to Antioch), the polytheists also accused them of being ill-omened (Ya-Sin: 18).

Fundamentally, a human cannot remain indifferent to the causes of events; ultimately, one must seek a cause for every occurrence. If one is a monotheist and a worshiper of God, and attributes the causes of events to His Pure Essence—who, according to His wisdom, does everything based on a plan—and also relies on science regarding the chain of natural causes and effects, his problem is solved. Otherwise, he will fabricate a series of superstitious, imaginary, and baseless causes for them—superstitions that have no boundaries, one of the clearest being the taking of bad omens.

For example, the pre-Islamic Arabs took the movement of a bird going from right to left as a good omen and a sign of victory, and if it moved from left to right, they took it as a bad omen and a sign of defeat and failure! They had many such superstitions and myths.

Today, even in societies that do not believe in God—even if they have achieved many victories in terms of modern science and knowledge—these kinds of superstitions and myths are abundant. To the extent that sometimes the falling of a salt shaker on the ground deeply upsets them, and a house, room, or chair numbered thirteen plunges them into terror. The market for fortune-tellers and palmists is still hot and thriving among them, and the imaginary issue of "luck" and "horoscopes" has many customers among them.

But the Quran says in one short sentence: "Your omen is with Allah" (طَائِرُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ). Your luck, fate, victory, defeat, success, and failure are all with Allah—the God who is Wise and distributes His favors according to merits; merits that are reflections of the faith, actions, words, and deeds of human beings.

In this way, Islam invites its followers from the valley of superstition to reality, and from the bypass to the Straight Path. (Regarding good and bad omens, we have had a detailed discussion in Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 316 to 319, under verse 131 of Surah Al-A'raf).

[^1]: The phrase "أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ" is in the genitive case with a preposition that is implied; originally it was: "And We sent to Thamud their brother Salih with the worship of Allah."

[^2]: Although "فريقان" (two parties) is dual, its verb "يختصمون" (quarreling) appears in the plural form because each party consists of a group and together they form a collective.

Ayahs

وَكَانَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ (٤٨)
There were nine persons in the city who caused corruption in the land and did not set things right. (48)
قَالُوا۟ تَقَاسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ لَنُبَيِّتَنَّهُۥ وَأَهْلَهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَقُولَنَّ لِوَلِيِّهِۦ مَا شَهِدْنَا مَهْلِكَ أَهْلِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (٤٩)
They said, ‘Swear by Allah that we will attack him and his family by night. Then we will tell his heir that we were not present at the murder of his family and that we speak the truth.’ (49)
وَمَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرًا وَمَكَرْنَا مَكْرًا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٥٠)
They devised a plot, and We [too] devised a plan, but they were not aware. (50)
فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ مَكْرِهِمْ أَنَّا دَمَّرْنَـٰهُمْ وَقَوْمَهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥١)
So observe how was the outcome of their plotting, as We destroyed them and all their people. (51)
فَتِلْكَ بُيُوتُهُمْ خَاوِيَةًۢ بِمَا ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٢)
So there lay their houses, fallen in ruin because of their wrongdoing. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who have knowledge. (52)
وَأَنجَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٥٣)
And We delivered those who had faith and were Godwary. (53)

Commentary

The Conspiracy of the Nine Corrupt Groups in Wadi al-Qura

Here, we read another part of the story of Salih and his people, which completes and concludes the previous section. it pertains to the conspiracy to assassinate Salih by nine groups of disbelievers and hypocrites, and the subsequent foiling of their plot.

It says: "And there were in the city (Wadi al-Qura) nine groups who caused corruption in the land and would not reform" (وَ كانَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا يُصْلِحُونَ).

Considering that "raht" (رهط) linguistically refers to a group of fewer than ten or fewer than forty people, it becomes clear that these small groups, each following its own path, shared one common goal: spreading corruption on earth and disrupting the social order, as well as the foundations of belief and morality. The phrase "and would not reform" (لا يُصْلِحُونَ) serves as an emphasis on this matter; for sometimes a person commits a corrupt act but later regrets it and seeks to reform, but true corrupters are not like this—they continuously persist in corruption and never intend to reform.

This is especially evident given that "yufsidun" (يفسدون) is a present continuous verb, indicating that this was their habitual and constant practice.

Each of these nine groups had a head and a leader, and likely each was affiliated with a specific tribe.

Undoubtedly, with the emergence of Salih and his pure, reformative religion, the field became narrow for these groups. It was here that, according to the next verse, "They said: 'Take an oath by Allah that we will surely attack him and his family by night and kill them; then we will say to his legal heir that we did not witness the destruction of his family, and indeed, we are truthful!'" (قالُوا تَقاسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ لَنُبَيِّتَنَّهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ ثُمَّ لَنَقُولَنَّ لِوَلِيِّهِ ما شَهِدْنا مَهْلِكَ أَهْلِهِ وَ إِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ).

"Taqasamu" (تقاسموا) is an imperative verb, meaning: all of you participate in taking an oath and commit yourselves to carrying out this great conspiracy—a commitment from which there is no turning back or flexibility.

It is interesting that they swore by "Allah," which shows that besides worshipping idols, they also believed in Allah as the Creator of the heavens and the earth, and in important matters, they would swear by His name. It also shows that they were so intoxicated and arrogant that they intended to carry out this great crime in the name of God!
