As if they wanted to perform an act of worship or a service pleasing to God; and such is the way of the arrogant who are oblivious to God and gone astray.

"Lanubayittannahu" (لنبيتنه) is derived from "tabyit" (تبييت), meaning to launch a night raid or a surprise nocturnal attack. This expression shows that they simultaneously feared Salih's supporters and were terrified of his kinsmen and tribe. Therefore, to achieve their goal while avoiding the wrath of his supporters, they were forced to plan a night attack. They conspired that whenever they were approached—since their opposition to Salih was already known—they would collectively swear that they had absolutely no involvement in this plan and were not even witnesses to the scene!

It is recorded in histories that their conspiracy was as follows: near the city, there was a mountain with a cave that served as Salih's place of worship, where he would sometimes go at night to pray and supplicate to his Lord. They decided to lie in ambush there and kill Salih when he arrived. After his martyrdom, they planned to attack his house and finish off his family that same night, then return to their homes and express ignorance if questioned.

However, Allah foiled their conspiracy in a wondrous way and turned their plans to naught.

While they were lying in ambush in a corner of the mountain, the mountain collapsed, and a massive boulder rolled down from the peak, crushing and destroying them in a brief moment! Thus, the Quran says in the next verse: "And they devised a plan, and We devised a plan, while they perceived not" (وَ مَكَرُوا مَكْراً وَ مَكَرْنا مَكْراً وَ هُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ).

Then it adds: "Then see how was the end of their plan: that We destroyed them and their people, all of them" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ مَكْرِهِمْ أَنَّا دَمَّرْناهُمْ وَ قَوْمَهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

The word "makr" (مكر), as we have previously stated (in Volume 2, page 226), means any kind of planning or devising a solution in Arabic literature; it is not exclusive to the diabolical and harmful plots for which it is used in modern Persian. Therefore, it is used for both harmful plots and for good solutions.

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Al-makr is to divert another from what he intends."

"Makr is when someone is prevented from reaching their goal."

Therefore, when this word is used regarding Allah, it means neutralizing harmful conspiracies, and when used regarding corrupters, it means obstructing reformative programs.

Then the Quran describes the manner of their destruction and their end as follows: "So these are their houses, empty because of the wrong they did" (فَتِلْكَ بُيُوتُهُمْ خاوِيَةً بِما ظَلَمُوا).

No sound is heard from them. No movement or activity exists there. And no trace remains of all that glitter, luxury, blessings, and sinful gatherings. Yes, the fire of injustice and oppression fell upon them, burning and destroying everything.

"Indeed in that is a sign for a people who know" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ).

However, in the midst of this, the "dry and the wet" did not burn together, and the innocent did not burn in the fire of the sinner: "And We saved those who believed and used to fear Allah," and they never suffered the grim fate of the evildoers (وَ أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ كانُوا يَتَّقُونَ).

Key Points

1- The Punishment of the People of Thamud

In the verses of the Quran, regarding this rebellious people, it sometimes says: "The earthquake seized them and crushed them" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ) (Al-A'raf 78).

And sometimes it says: "The thunderbolt seized them" (فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ) (Adh-Dhariyat 44).

And sometimes it says: "The heavenly blast overtook those who did wrong" (وَ أَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ) (Hud 67).

However, there is no contradiction between these three expressions, because a thunderbolt (sa'iqah) is that same massive electrical spark exchanged between clouds and the earth; it is accompanied by a great sound (sayhah) and also by a severe shaking of the surrounding lands (further explanations regarding the heavenly blast were provided in Volume 9, page 164, under verse 67 of Surah Hud).

2- Some commentators have narrated that the companions of Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) who were saved with him numbered four thousand. By the command of the Lord, they left that polluted region full of corruption and migrated toward Hadramaut. [^1]

3- "Khawiyah" (خاوية) is derived from "khawa'" (خواء), which sometimes means falling and being destroyed, and sometimes means becoming empty. This expression is also used regarding falling stars (meteors); they say "khawa al-najm," meaning the star fell.

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: The original meaning of "khawa" is to be empty; this expression is used for hungry stomachs, hollow walnuts, and stars devoid of rain (the pre-Islamic Arabs believed that every star appearing on the horizon brought rain with it).

4- It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: "I have well understood from the Quran that injustice and oppression destroy houses," then he argued using the above verse: "So these are their houses, empty because of the wrong they did" (فَتِلْكَ بُيُوتُهُمْ خاوِيَةً بِما ظَلَمُوا). [^1] Truly, the effect of injustice in destroying cities and societies is incomparable to anything else. Injustice is a deadly thunderbolt; injustice is a devastating earthquake; injustice is like a death-dealing heavenly blast. History has proven time and again that the world may continue with disbelief (kufr), but it cannot endure with injustice (zulm).

5- Undoubtedly, the general punishment of the people of Thamud occurred after the killing of Salih's she-camel, as we read in verses 65 to 67 of Surah Hud: When they hamstrung the she-camel, he said to them: "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days; that is a promise not to be belied." And when Our command came, We saved Salih and those who believed with him, and the heavenly blast overtook the wrongdoers, and they lay prostrate in their homes, dead.

Therefore, it was not only after the conspiracy to kill Salih that the punishment descended; rather, in all likelihood, in the incident of the conspiracy to kill the Prophet, only the group of conspirators was destroyed. They were then given a further respite, and after the killing of the she-camel, all the wrongdoers and faithless sinners were eliminated. This is the result of reconciling the verses of this Surah with what appears in Surah Hud and Surah Al-A'raf.

In other words, in the verses under discussion, their destruction follows the conspiracy to kill Salih and his family, while in the verses of Surah Al-A'raf and Hud, their destruction follows the killing of Salih's she-camel. The result of these two is that they first plotted to kill him, and when they did not succeed, they proceeded to kill the she-camel, which was his great miracle, and after a three-day respite, their painful punishment arrived.

There is also the possibility that they first killed the she-camel, and because Salih threatened them with divine punishment after three days, they thought of destroying him personally, but they failed and were destroyed. [^1]

[^1]: Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Qurtubi in his famous Tafsir, under the verses discussed. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ (٥٤)
[We also sent] Lot, when he said to his people, ‘What! Do you commit this indecency while you look on? (54)
أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ (٥٥)
Do you approach men with [sexual] desire instead of women?! Indeed, you are a senseless lot!’ (55)

Commentary

The Deviation of the People of Lut

After mentioning glimpses of the lives of Musa, Dawud, Sulayman, and Salih along with their respective peoples, the fifth prophet whose life is referenced in this Surah is the great Prophet of Allah, Hazrat Lut عليه السلام.

This is one of several times the Quran has addressed this subject; previously, in Surahs Al-Hijr, Hud, Ash-Shu'ara, and Al-A'raf, material regarding this matter has been presented.

This repetition, and others like it, is because the Quran is not a history book that narrates an event in its entirety once and never returns to it. Rather, it is a book of education and human-building. We know that in educational matters, circumstances sometimes necessitate that an event be reminded time and again, viewed from different angles, and concluded upon from various perspectives.

In any case, the life of the people of Lut—who are famous in the world for sexual deviation, homosexuality, and other foul and shameful habits—as well as their painful end, can serve as a vivid portrait for those immersed in the quagmire of lust. The spread of this pollution among humans necessitates that this story be pointed out repeatedly.

In the verses under discussion, it first says: "And remember Lut, when he said to his people: 'Do you commit a most lewd and shameful act while you see (its ugliness and its ominous consequences)?' (وَ لُوطاً إِذْ قالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَ تَأْتُونَ الْفاحِشَةَ وَ أَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ‌) [1]" As we have previously noted, "Fahishah" refers to acts whose ugliness and indecency are clear and manifest, and here it refers to homosexuality and the shameful act of sodomy.

The phrase "while you see" (أَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ) indicates that you see with your own eyes the ugliness and atrocity of this act and its ominous consequences—how it has polluted your society from head to toe, such that even your children and infants are not safe. Why do you see this and yet not wake up?! As for the possibility some have suggested—that it refers to them committing such acts in front of one another's eyes—it is not very consistent with the apparent meaning of the expression. This is because Lut عليه السلام intends to awaken their conscience and make the inner voice reach their ears; his words are about insight, seeing the deadly consequences of this act, and becoming vigilant.

[1]: "Lutan" (لوطا) may be in the accusative case (mansub) due to the verb "Arsalna" (We sent) mentioned in previous verses, or due to an implied verb such as "Udhkur" (Remember). However, the second possibility seems more appropriate given the phrase "when he said..." (اذ قال ...).

He then adds: "Do you indeed approach men with lust instead of women?" (أَ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجالَ شَهْوَةً مِنْ دُونِ النِّساءِ).

In reality, he first mentions this act as "Fahishah" (a lewd act), then describes it more clearly so that no ambiguity remains; this is one of the techniques of eloquence for explaining important matters.

And to make it clear that the motive for this act is ignorance and lack of knowledge, he adds:

"Nay, but you are a people who are ignorant" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ‌).

Ignorance of Allah, ignorance of the purpose of creation and the laws of nature, and ignorance of the ominous effects of this shameful sin. If you were to reflect well, you would reach the truth of how ignorant this act is. Mentioning this sentence in the form of an "interrogative" is so that they may hear the answer from within their own conscience, making it more effective.

Beginning of Juz 20 of the Holy Quran (Remainder of Surah Al-Naml)

Ayahs

۞ فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوٓا۟ ءَالَ لُوطٍ مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ (٥٦)
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Expel Lot’s family from your town! They are indeed a puritanical lot.’ (56)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ قَدَّرْنَـٰهَا مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٥٧)
So We delivered him and his family, except his wife. We ordained her to be among those who remained behind. (57)
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (٥٨)
Then We poured down upon them a rain [of stones]. Evil was that rain for those who had been warned! (58)
قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ عِبَادِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ۗ ءَآللَّهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٩)
Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, and Peace be to the servants whom He has chosen.’ Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]? (59)

Commentary

Where Purity is a Great Defect!

In the preceding discussions, we observed the powerful logic of the great Prophet of God, Lut (peace be upon him), facing the corrupt deviants. We saw with what eloquent and reasoned expression he sought to restrain them from the shameful act of homosexuality, demonstrating to them that this behavior is the result of ignorance, lack of knowledge regarding the laws of creation, and a disregard for all human values.

Now let us see what this filthy and polluted people said in response to Lut’s logical discourse.

The Quran states: "They had no answer except that they said to one another:

'Expel the family of Lut from your city and land, for they are people who keep themselves pure'" and are not willing to harmonize themselves with us!! (فَما كانَ جَوابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا أَخْرِجُوا آلَ لُوطٍ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ إِنَّهُمْ أُناسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ).

This was an answer that reflected their intellectual degradation and extraordinary moral collapse.

Indeed, in a polluted environment, purity is a crime and a defect! They cast the chaste Josephs into prison for the crime of modesty and piety, and they exile the family of the great Prophet of God, Lut, because of their avoidance of pollution and shame; yet the Zulaikhas are free and hold positions of power! And the people of Lut must remain at ease in their city and land! This is a clear manifestation of what the Quran says regarding the misguided:

We set a seal upon their hearts—because of their deeds—and We place a veil over their eyes, and in their ears is a heaviness.

There is also the possibility regarding the phrase "أُناسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ" (people who keep themselves pure) that, due to being submerged in this quagmire of corruption and becoming accustomed to filth, they said this to the family of Lut out of mockery—implying that these people imagine our actions are impurity and their avoidance is purity! What a strange and ridiculous thing! And it is not surprising that a person's sense of discernment changes as a result of becoming accustomed to a shameful act. We have heard the famous story of "the tanner who was constantly dealing with foul-smelling hides and whose sense of smell had become accustomed to it; when he passed through the perfume-sellers' market, he fainted from the 'unpleasant' scent of perfume! And the wise man's instruction was to take him back to the tanners' market so he would regain consciousness and be saved from death." This is a striking sensory example for this logical point.

It is mentioned in narrations that Lut preached to them for about thirty years, but none believed in him except his family (and even then, with the exception of his wife, who shared the beliefs of the polytheists) [1]. Naturally, such a group, for whom there is no hope of reform, has no place in the world of the living, and the scroll of their life must be folded up. Therefore, in the next verse, it says: "We delivered Lut and his family, except his wife, whom We decreed to be among those who remained behind" (فَأَنْجَيْناهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ قَدَّرْناها مِنَ الْغابِرِينَ) [2].

And after they departed at the appointed time (at dawn on a night when the city was drowned in corruption and shame), once the morning arrived, "We rained down upon them a rain (of stones)" (under which they were all buried, and this was after a terrifying earthquake completely overturned their land) (وَ أَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَراً).

"And how terrible and evil is the rain of those who were warned" (فَساءَ مَطَرُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ).

Regarding the people of Lut, their fate, and the sinister effects of homosexuality, we had detailed discussions in Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 178–198 (under verses 77–83 of Surah Hud), which need not be repeated here.

We only deem it necessary to mention one point:

The law of creation has determined a path for us, the traversing of which is the cause of our perfection and life, and the opposition to which is the cause of our fall and death.

The law of creation placed sexual attraction—as a factor for the survival of the human race and the peace of his soul—between two opposite sexes. Diverting its path toward "homosexuality" disrupts both spiritual peace and social stability. Since these social laws have roots in primordial nature (fitrah), this violation causes disharmony in the organization of human existence.

Lut, the great Prophet of God, drew the attention of the deviant people to this very natural root, saying to them: "Do you approach an indecency while you see?" This is the ignorance and lack of awareness of the law of life, and ignorance in the sense of folly, that has led you to this deviation.

And it is not surprising that other laws of creation should also change regarding this people; instead of a rain of life-giving water, a rain of stones descended upon them, and their peaceful land was overturned by earthquakes, so that not only were they destroyed, but their traces were also erased.

In the final verse under discussion, after concluding the account of five great Prophets of God and the fate of their peoples, the discourse turns to the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family). As a conclusion from the past and a prelude to the dialogue with the polytheists, it states: "Say: Praise be to Allah" (قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ).

Praise belongs to the God who destroyed shameful peoples like the people of Lut, lest the extent of their pollutions encompass the entire earth.

Praise and thanks are for Him who sent corruptors like the people of Thamud and the arrogant Pharaohs to the realm of non-existence, so that their ways and customs would not become a tradition and a model for others.

And finally, praise and thanks belong to Him who granted all this power and blessing to believing servants like David and Solomon (peace be upon them) and guided the misguided, such as the people of Saba, through them.

Then it adds: "And peace be upon His chosen servants" (وَ سَلامٌ عَلى‌ عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى‌).

Peace be upon Moses, Salih, Lut, Solomon, and David (peace be upon them), and peace be upon all the Prophets and their true followers.

Then it asks: "Is Allah, who possesses all this ability, power, bounty, and blessing, better, or the idols they associate with Him, which have absolutely no effect?" (آللَّهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ) [1].

[^1]: "آللَّه" was originally "أ الله", where one of the two hamzas was changed to an alif, resulting in a madd. The phrase "أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ" was originally "أم ما يشركون", where "أم" is for interrogation and "ما" is a relative pronoun, which have been merged.

In these stories of the previous Prophets, we saw that the idols were never able to provide the slightest help to their worshippers during the descent of calamities, whereas Allah never left the believers alone in any of these difficulties, and His infinite grace came to their aid.

Ayahs

أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ حَدَآئِقَ ذَاتَ بَهْجَةٍ مَّا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَن تُنۢبِتُوا۟ شَجَرَهَآ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ (٦٠)
Is He who created the heavens and the earth, and sends down for you water from the sky, whereby We grow delightful gardens, whose trees you could never cause to grow...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Indeed, they are a lot who equate [others with Allah]. (60)
أَمَّن جَعَلَ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَجَعَلَ خِلَـٰلَهَآ أَنْهَـٰرًا وَجَعَلَ لَهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ حَاجِزًا ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦١)
Is He who made the earth an abode [for you], and made rivers [flowing] through it, and set firm mountains for it [’s stability], and set a barrier between the two seas...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Indeed, most of them do not know. (61)
أَمَّن يُجِيبُ ٱلْمُضْطَرَّ إِذَا دَعَاهُ وَيَكْشِفُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٦٢)
Is He who answers the call of the distressed [person] when he invokes Him and removes his distress, and makes you successors on the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Little is the admonition that you take. (62)
أَمَّن يَهْدِيكُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ وَمَن يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ تَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٣)
Is He who guides you in the darkness of land and sea and who sends the winds as harbingers of His mercy...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Exalted is Allah above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (63)
أَمَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ وَمَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٦٤)
Is He who originates the creation, then He will bring it back, and who provides for you from the sky and the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Say, ‘Produce your evidence, if you are truthful.’ (64)

Commentary

With all these proofs, do you still associate partners with Him?

In the final verse of the previous discussion—after mentioning the soul-stirring segments of the lives of five great Prophets—a short yet profound question was posed: "Is Allah, with all His power and ability, better, or the worthless idols they have fashioned?"

The verses under discussion elaborate on this, and through five verses—each beginning with a calculated question—the polytheists are brought under interrogation and trial. The clearest proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) are presented across these five verses, referencing twelve examples of Allah’s great blessings.

First, it addresses the creation of the heavens and the earth, the descent of rain, and the blessings resulting from it, saying: "Is [your idols better or] He who created the heavens and the earth and sent down for you water from the sky, whereby We caused to grow wonderful gardens of delight?" (أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ أَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَنْبَتْنا بِهِ حَدائِقَ ذاتَ بَهْجَةٍ) ^1. Hada'iq is the plural of hadiqah. As many commentators have stated, it refers to a garden surrounded by a wall and protected from all sides, much like the hadaqah (pupil) of the eye, which is enclosed within the eyelids. Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Hadiqah originally refers to land where water has collected, just as the pupil of the eye always contains water."

From these two explanations, it can be concluded that a hadiqah is a garden that possesses both a wall and sufficient water.

Bahjah refers to the beauty of color and outward excellence that overwhelms the observer with joy. ^1 At the end of the verse, addressing the servants, He says: "It was not in your power to cause its trees to grow" (ما كانَ لَكُمْ أَنْ تُنْبِتُوا شَجَرَها).

Your task is merely sowing the seeds and irrigation; however, the One who created life within the heart of this seed, and who commands the sunlight, the life-giving drops of rain, and the particles of soil to make this seed grow, is Allah alone.

These are realities that no one can deny or attribute to anyone other than Allah. He is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, the Sender of rain, and the Source of all this beauty and grace in the world of life.

Even contemplating the coloring of a beautiful flower and the delicate, orderly petals encircling the central core—proclaiming the song of life—is sufficient to acquaint man with the greatness, power, and wisdom of its Creator. These are the things that stir the human heart and call it toward Him.

In other words: Monotheism in creation (Tawhid of the Creator) and Monotheism in Lordship (Tawhid of the Disposer of this world) are considered the foundation for the "Monotheism of Worship."

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He asks: "Is there any deity with Allah?" (أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ).

"Nay, but they are a people who deviate [from the truth and set up equals to Him who have no power]" (بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ) [^2].

^1: This verse actually has an omitted part, and the estimation is: "Is what they associate [with Him] better, or He who created the heavens and the earth?" In the previous verse, the question was: Is Allah, who is the source of salvation for His servants, better, or the idols they associate with Him? But in this verse, the question begins with the idols, asking: "Are they better, or Allah who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth?"

^1: The word dhat in dhata bahjah appears in the singular form while hada'iq, which it describes, is plural. This is because hada'iq is a broken plural, and a broken plural sometimes carries the concept of a "group" (singular), thus its adjective will also be singular.

[^2]: Ya'dilun may be derived from 'udul, meaning deviation and turning away from truth toward falsehood; it may also be from 'adl, meaning equal, similar, or peer. In the first case, the meaning is that they deviate from the One Unique God to others; in the second case, it means they assign equals and peers to Him.

In the second question, discussing the blessing of the stability and tranquility of the earth and man's dwelling place in this world, He says: "Are their fabricated deities better, or He who made the earth a stable place, and placed rivers in its midst, and set for it firm mountains?!" (so that they might hold the earth's crust from shaking like a coat of armor) (أَمَّنْ جَعَلَ الْأَرْضَ قَراراً وَ جَعَلَ خِلالَها أَنْهاراً وَ جَعَلَ لَها رَواسِيَ) ^1 and also "placed a barrier between the two seas (of fresh and salt water)" so they do not mix (وَ جَعَلَ بَيْنَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ حاجِزاً).

Thus, four great blessings are mentioned in this verse, three of which speak of stability and tranquility:

The tranquility of the earth itself, which, despite its rapid movement around itself, around the sun, and within the solar system, is so uniform and calm that its inhabitants do not feel it at all—as if it were bolted in one place, standing still without the slightest movement.

Another is the existence of mountains—which we previously mentioned surround the earth, their roots interconnected to form a powerful armor. They resist the internal pressures of the earth and the external tidal movements caused by the moon's gravity, and they also serve as barriers against massive storms that would disrupt the earth's tranquility. ^1

Another is the natural veil and barrier placed between sections of fresh water and the salt water of the oceans. This invisible barrier is nothing other than the difference in the density of salt and fresh water—the so-called difference in "specific gravity"—which causes the water of great rivers flowing into the sea to remain unmixed with salt water for a long time. Consequently, the tides push this fresh water over vast coastal areas ready for cultivation, irrigating them. We have provided a detailed explanation of this under verse 53 of Surah Al-Furqan in this same volume.

At the same time, within the various parts of the earth, He has placed rivers of water—the essence of life and the source of freshness, lushness, cultivated fields, and fruitful gardens. Part of the source of these waters is in the mountains, and part is within the layers of the earth's crust.

Can this system be the product of blind, deaf chance and a source lacking intellect and wisdom?

Do idols have any role in this exquisite and wondrous system? Even the idolaters do not make such a claim.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He repeats the question: "Is there any deity with Allah?" (أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ).

Nay, "but most of them do not know" (بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

^1: Khilal originally means the gap between two things, and rawasi is the plural of rasiyah, meaning firm and stable.

^1: Regarding the role of mountains in the stability of the earth and its other blessings, we have also discussed this in Volume 10, page 113 (under verse 3 of Surah Ar-Ra'd).

In the third question of these five—which together form a spiritual interrogation and trial—the discussion turns to the resolution of problems, the breaking of deadlocks, and the answering of prayers. He says:

"Are your worthless deities better, or He who answers the distressed when he calls upon Him and removes the evil and affliction?!" (أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ وَ يَكْشِفُ السُّوءَ).

Indeed, at that moment when all doors of the world of means are closed to man, when he is driven to his wits' end and becomes helpless and distressed from every side, the only One who can turn the key to his problems, remove the deadlocks, scatter the light of hope in hearts, and open the doors of mercy to the helpless is His Pure Essence and none other.

Since this reality exists as an innate feeling within the souls of all humans, even idolaters, when caught amidst the roaring waves of the sea, forget all their deities and cling to the grace of "Allah," as the Quran says: "And when they board a ship, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion" (Al-Ankabut: 65).

Then He adds:

He not only removes problems and distresses but also "makes you successors of the earth" (وَ يَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ).

"Is there any deity with Allah?" (أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ).

"But little do you remember" and take heed from these clear proofs (قَلِيلًا ما تَذَكَّرُونَ) ^1. Regarding the concept of the "distressed" (mudtarr) and the issue of the fulfillment of prayer and its conditions, discussions will follow in the "Key Points" at the end of these verses.

The meaning of "successors of the earth" (khulafa' al-ard) may refer to the inhabitants of the earth and its owners, for God, with all those blessings and means of comfort, ease, and tranquility He placed on earth, has made man the ruler of this terrestrial globe and prepared him to dominate it.

Especially when man falls into distress and turns to the threshold of God, and He, by His grace, removes afflictions and obstacles, the foundations of this succession become firmer (and from here, the relationship between these two parts of the verse becomes clear).

It may also refer to the fact that God has established the law of life such that nations constantly come and succeed other nations; if it were not for this rotation, no evolution would take place. [^2]

In the fourth question, the issue of guidance is raised: "Are these idols better, or He who guides you in the darkness of the land and the sea (by means of the stars)?" (أَمَّنْ يَهْدِيكُمْ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَ الْبَحْرِ). "And He who sends the winds as heralds of glad tidings before His mercy (rain)?" (وَ مَنْ يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ).

Winds that signal the coming of rain and move ahead like a special messenger of glad tidings; in reality, their work is also to guide people toward the descent of rain.

The expression bushran (heralds of glad tidings) regarding the winds and the expression rahmah (mercy) regarding the rain are both fascinating, for it is the winds that lift the moisture and clouds from the surface of the oceans, carry them on their shoulders to dry and thirsty lands, and announce the arrival of rain.

Likewise, it is the rain that sings the song of life across the entire globe, and wherever it descends, it creates goodness, mercy, blessing, and life. ^1 (A more detailed explanation of the role of winds in the descent of rain is found in Surah Al-A'raf under verse 57, Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 214).

At the end of the verse, He addresses the polytheists once more: "Is there any deity with Allah?!" (أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ).

Then, without waiting for their answer, He adds: "Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him" (تَعالَى اللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ).

^1: The word ma in qalilan ma tadhakkarun is apparently redundant (za'idah), and we know the benefit of redundant particles in many cases is emphasis. Qalilan is an adjective for an omitted infinitive, and the estimation was: "You remember with a little remembering."

[^2]: Therefore, khulafa' al-ard means "successors on the earth."

^1: Bushr (rhyming with 'ushr), as previously mentioned, is a contraction of bushur (rhyming with kutub), the plural of bashur (rhyming with qabul), meaning a bringer of glad tidings.

In the final verse under discussion, the fifth question—concerning the Origin and the Return—is posed as follows: "Are your deities better, or He who originates creation and then repeats it?!" (أَمَّنْ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ). "And He who, between this beginning and end, provides for you from the heaven and the earth?" (وَ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

In spite of this, do you still believe that "there is a deity with Allah?" (أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ).

"Say: Bring your proof if you are truthful" (قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ).

In fact, the previous verses all spoke of the Origin and the signs of God's greatness in the universe and His gifts and blessings; but in the final verse, the discussion transitions through a subtle passage to the issue of the Resurrection (Ma'ad), for the beginning of creation is itself a proof of its repetition, and the power over creation is considered a clear proof for the Resurrection.

From here, the answer to a question raised by many commentators becomes clear: the polytheists addressed in these verses generally did not believe in the Resurrection (the physical Resurrection); how then is it possible to ask them and seek their admission?

The answer is that this question is accompanied by a proof that compels the opponent to admit; for as soon as they confess that the beginning of creation is from Him, and all these provisions and blessings are from His side, it is sufficient for them to accept that the possibility of a return to life on the Day of Judgment exists.

Furthermore, "provision from heaven" refers to rain, sunlight, and the like, while "provision from the earth" refers to plants and various food materials that grow directly from the earth or originate indirectly from it, such as livestock, as well as minerals and various materials that man utilizes in his life.

Key Points

1- Who is the distressed one whose prayer is answered?

Although Allah answers the prayers of everyone—whenever the conditions are met—the above verses specifically emphasize the title "distressed" (mudtarr). This is because one of the conditions for the fulfillment of prayer is that man completely turns his eyes away from the world of means and places his entire heart and soul at the disposal of Allah, seeing everything as belonging to Him and seeing the solution to every problem in His hand. This realization and vision occur in a state of distress.

It is true that the world is a world of means, and the believer exerts his utmost effort in this regard, but he is never lost in the world of means. He sees everything as a blessing from His Pure Essence; "he has a piercing eye that penetrates the means, uprooting them from their base," and behind the veil of means, he sees the Essence of the "Causer of Causes" and asks everything from Him.

Indeed, if man reaches this stage, he has fulfilled the most important condition for the answering of prayer.

Interestingly, in some narrations, this verse is interpreted as the uprising of Hazrat Mahdi—صلى الله عليه وآله.

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he said: "By Allah, it is as if I am looking at the Qa'im while he has leaned his back against the [Black] Stone and is adjuring Allah for his right... He said: By Allah, he is the 'distressed' in the Book of Allah in His saying: 'Or He who answers the distressed when he calls upon Him and removes the evil and makes you successors of the earth.'" ^1

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "This was revealed concerning the Qa'im from the family of Muhammad عليهم السلام; by Allah, he is the distressed one when he prays two units of prayer at the Station [of Abraham] and supplicates to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; so He answers him, removes the evil, and makes him the successor on the earth." ^1

Undoubtedly, the purpose of this interpretation—as we have seen in many similar instances—is not to restrict the concept of the verse to the blessed existence of Mahdi عليه السلام. Rather, the verse has a broad concept, one of the clearest instances of which is the existence of Mahdi عليه السلام. At that time when corruption has covered everywhere, doors are closed, humanity is at its wits' end and in a severe deadlock, and the state of distress is apparent in the whole world—at that moment, in the most sacred spot on earth, he raises his hands in prayer and requests the removal of evil. Allah makes this prayer the starting point of his sacred global revolution and, according to "and makes you successors of the earth," makes him and his companions the successors on the earth. [^2]

Regarding the importance of "prayer" and the "conditions for its fulfillment," and why some prayers are sometimes not answered, we have discussed this in detail in Volume 1 under verse 186 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

2- Everywhere an invitation to logical reasoning

Repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, we read that proof is demanded from the opponents. Specifically, the phrase "Bring your proof" (hatu burhanakum) appears in four places in the Holy Quran (Al-Baqarah: 111, Al-Anbiya: 24, Al-Naml: 64, and Al-Qasas: 75). In other instances, the word "proof" (burhan) is specifically emphasized (burhan refers to the strongest evidence that is always accompanied by truth).

This logic of "demanding proof" in Islam speaks of its strong and rich content, as it seeks to have a logical encounter even with its opponents. How is it possible to demand proof from others while being indifferent to it oneself? The verses of the Holy Quran are filled with logical arguments and scientific proofs at various levels for different issues.

This is exactly contrary to what distorted modern Christianity emphasizes, considering religion to be solely the work of the "heart" and introducing the intellect as a stranger to it, even accepting intellectual contradictions (such as Monotheism in Trinity) within religion. For this reason, it allows all kinds of superstitions to enter religion. However, if religion is separated from intellect and reasoning, there will be no proof for its truth, and that religion and its opposite will be the same.

The greatness of this Islamic program (reliance on proof and inviting opponents to logical reasoning) becomes more apparent when we consider that Islam emerged from an environment where the majority of the intellectual content consisted of baseless superstitions and illogical issues.

3- Summary and review of the previous verses

In the verses that passed, the Quran relied on "Monotheism of the Creator" and "Monotheism of the Lord" (Tawhid of creation and Tawhid of disposal) to prove the "Monotheism of Worship," speaking of twelve great signs of God in the universe (the heavens and the earth, the descent of rain, its vital effects, the tranquility of man's dwelling, flowing rivers, massive and firm mountains, the barrier between salt and fresh water, answering the calls of servants, guiding them in the darkness of land and sea, winds as heralds of rain, the renewal of the life of creatures, and providing for man from the heaven and the earth).

These twelve blessings were expressed through five verses and under five questions, which respectively addressed the following five issues: creation, tranquility, resolution of problems, guidance, and the return to a new life.

And at the end of each of these five questions, this phrase was repeated: "Is there any deity with Allah?" (أَ إِلهٌ مَعَ اللَّهِ).

Following this question, the first verse referred to their deviation from the truth, the second to their ignorance, the third to their lack of reflection, the fourth to their intellectual decline, and the fifth to the demand for reasoning from them—which together present a comprehensive and cohesive set.
