Ayahs

قُل لَّا يَعْلَمُ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْغَيْبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (٦٥)
Say, ‘No one in the heavens or the earth knows the Unseen except Allah, and they are not aware when they will be resurrected.’ (65)
بَلِ ٱدَّٰرَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهَا ۖ بَلْ هُم مِّنْهَا عَمُونَ (٦٦)
Do they comprehend the knowledge of the Hereafter? No, they are in doubt about it. Indeed, they are blind to it. (66)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا وَءَابَآؤُنَآ أَئِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ (٦٧)
The faithless say, ‘What! When we and our fathers have become dust shall we be raised [from the dead]? (67)
لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا هَـٰذَا نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦٨)
We and our fathers were promised this before. [But] these are just myths of the ancients.’ (68)

Commentary

Since the final verses of the previous discussion touched upon the Day of Resurrection, the verses under consideration examine this issue from various perspectives.

First, it addresses a question that the polytheists had repeatedly raised, asking: When will the Resurrection take place?

It states: "No one in the heavens and the earth knows the unseen except Allah, and they do not perceive when they will be resurrected" (قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَ ما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ‌).

Undoubtedly, knowledge of the unseen—including the date of the occurrence of the Resurrection—is exclusive to Allah. However, this does not contradict the fact that Allah may grant a portion of the knowledge of the unseen to whomever He wills, as we read in verses 26 and 27 of Surah Al-Jinn: "The Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to a messenger with whom He is pleased and has chosen for prophethood" (عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ‌).

In other words, knowledge of the unseen, inherently, independently, and in an unlimited form, belongs exclusively to Allah, and what others know is from Him. Nevertheless, the specific timing of the Resurrection is an exception even to this, and no one is aware of it. [^1]

Then, regarding the polytheists' lack of knowledge about the Resurrection and their doubt, uncertainty, and ignorance, it states: "They have no correct information about the world after death; rather, they are in doubt about its very essence; nay, they are blind to it!"

[^1]: We have had detailed discussions regarding the knowledge of the unseen in Volume 5, page 245, and Volume 7, page 46 onwards.

(بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْها بَلْ هُمْ مِنْها عَمُونَ‌).

"Iddaraka" (ادَّارَكَ) was originally "Tadaraka" (تدارك), which means to follow one after another. Therefore, the meaning of the phrase "ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ" is that they exhausted all their knowledge regarding the Hereafter but reached nowhere. Thus, it follows by saying: "they are in doubt about it, nay, they are blind." This is because the signs of the Hereafter are manifest even in the life of this world: the return of the dead earth to life in the spring season, the blossoming of trees that had ceased to function in the winter, and observing the greatness of Allah's power in the entirety of creation—all of these are evidence for the possibility of life after death. Yet, like the blind, they pass by these scenes.

Of course, other interpretations have been mentioned for the above phrase, including:

The meaning of "ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ" is that the means of acquiring knowledge about the Hereafter are abundant and follow one after another, but they do not have the eyes to see.

Some have also said it means that in the other world, when the veils are removed, they will become sufficiently aware of the realities.

However, among these three interpretations, the first is more consistent with the other phrases of the verse and the discussions that follow in the subsequent verses.

Accordingly, three stages are mentioned for the ignorance of those who deny the Hereafter:

First, their denial and objection are because they are ignorant of the specifics of the Hereafter; "and because they did not see the truth, they struck the path of myth."

Second, they doubt the very existence of the Hereafter, and their question about the time of its realization stems from this doubt.

Third, this ignorance and doubt are not because they lack sufficient evidence for the Hereafter; rather, the evidence is plentiful, but they have closed their eyes and do not see.

The next verse expresses the logic of the deniers of the Resurrection in a single sentence: "The disbelievers said: 'When we and our forefathers have become dust, shall we really be brought forth [from the earth]?'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَ إِذا كُنَّا تُراباً وَ آباؤُنا أَ إِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ‌).

They contented themselves with the mere notion that it is far-fetched for a human to become dust and then return to life, whereas on the first day they were dust and rose from dust. What is there to prevent them from returning to dust again and then finding a new life?

It is interesting that in eight instances in the Quran, we encounter similar expressions where they pursued the path of denial regarding the Resurrection based solely on it being "far-fetched."

Then they add: "This is a baseless promise that we and our forefathers were given before" and no trace of it has ever appeared (لَقَدْ وُعِدْنا هذا نَحْنُ وَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ‌). "These are nothing but the fables of the ancients"; these are nothing more than illusions and superstitions (إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

Therefore, they first began with the idea of it being far-fetched and then made that the basis for absolute denial, as if they expected the Resurrection to be realized immediately, and because they did not witness it during their own lifetimes, they rejected it.

In any case, these expressions are all signs of their arrogance and negligence.

Furthermore, with this expression, they intended to belittle the Prophet's words regarding the Resurrection, suggesting that these are old and baseless promises that others had also given to our ancestors, and it does not seem to be a new matter worthy of investigation or study.

Ayahs

قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٦٩)
Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the guilty.’ (69)
وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُن فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ (٧٠)
Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their guile. (70)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٧١)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (71)
قُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٧٢)
Say, ‘Perhaps there is right behind you some of what you seek to hasten.’ (72)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
Indeed your Lord is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (73)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٤)
Your Lord knows whatever their breasts conceal, and whatever they disclose. (74)
وَمَا مِنْ غَآئِبَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٧٥)
There is no invisible thing in the heaven and the earth but it is in a manifest Book. (75)

Commentary

Do Not Be Anxious About Their Plots

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the denial of the Resurrection by the prejudiced disbelievers who rejected the Day of Judgment with mockery and ridicule.

Since logical debate with such an obstinate people is futile—and moreover, since numerous proofs for the Resurrection have been established in other verses of the Quran, proofs that are observed even in daily life within the world of plants, the realm of the fetus, and the like—the verses under discussion, instead of citing further proofs, threaten and warn them of the divine punishments that lie ahead.

Addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), Allah says: "Say, 'Travel through the land and observe the remains of those before you, and see what was the end of the criminals and sinners?'" (قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

You claim that these promises were also given to our ancestors and they paid them no heed and suffered no harm; however, if you travel a little through the world and look at the remains of these criminal and sinful predecessors who denied Monotheism and the Resurrection—remains located specifically around your own land of Hijaz—you will realize that is not the case.

Your turn will also come; do not be in a hurry. If you do not rectify your path, you too will be afflicted by the same ill fate.

The Holy Quran repeatedly invites people to travel through the earth and observe the remains of those past: the ruined lands of nations afflicted by punishment, the shattered palaces of kings, the crumbled graves and decayed bones of the arrogant, and the ownerless wealth and treasures of the conceited rich. It specifically clarifies that studying these remains—which are the living, eloquent, and tangible history of predecessors—awakens hearts and gives sight to the eyes. Indeed, it is so; sometimes observing one of these remains creates such a storm in a person's heart and soul that studying several thick history books does not produce such an effect.

We have discussed this subject in detail under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran (Volume 3, page 102 onwards).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, instead of "deniers" (of the Resurrection), the word "criminals" is used, indicating that their denial was not because they made a mistake in research and investigation, but rather its source was obstinacy, enmity, and being tainted with various types of crimes.

Since the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) suffered from their denial and opposition, and his heart truly burned for them as he was passionate about their guidance and awakening—and on the other hand, he was constantly faced with their plots—the next verse consoles the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), saying: "Do not grieve over their denial and rejection, and do not sorrow much for them" (وَ لا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ).

And do not be anxious about their plots, "and let not your breast be distressed" because of them, for We are your Supporter and Helper (وَ لا تَكُنْ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ).

Yet these obstinate deniers, instead of taking heed and counsel from this warning of the compassionate and kind Prophet and observing the end of the criminals, again resort to mockery and ridicule, "and they say, 'If you are truthful, when will this promise of divine punishment occur?'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ مَتى‌ هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ). Although the addressee was the Prophet, they use the plural form because the true believers were also in accord with the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) in this statement, and naturally, they are also addressed.

Here, the Quran addresses this mocking statement of theirs with a completely realistic tone, saying: "Tell them, 'Perhaps some of what you seek to hasten is close behind you!'" (قُلْ عَسى‌ أَنْ يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُمْ بَعْضُ الَّذِي تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ). Why do you hurry? Why do you belittle divine punishment? Why do you not have mercy on yourselves? After all, God's punishment is no joke! Consider the possibility that because of these very words of yours, God's wrath and anger may have cast its shadow over you and may descend upon you very soon and destroy you; what is all this stubbornness for?

"Radifa" is from the root "radf" (rhyming with harf), meaning to be positioned behind something. Therefore, a person who sits behind a horse rider is called a "radif"; similarly, this word is used for individuals and objects that are positioned one after another.

Regarding what this punishment refers to, some say it is the severe blow dealt to these obstinate criminals in the Battle of Badr—the first battle of the Muslims against the disbelievers—where seventy of their leaders fell to the dust and seventy were taken prisoner.

There is also the possibility that it refers to a painful general punishment, which was ultimately averted from them due to the presence of the Prophet, who was a "Mercy to the worlds," and verse 33 of Surah Al-Anfal bears witness to this: (وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيُعَذِّبَهُمْ وَ أَنْتَ فِيهِمْ) "But Allah would not punish them while you are among them."

The expression "'asa" (perhaps) is from the tongue of the Prophet, and even in the Divine Word—contrary to what some have imagined—there is no issue with this expression, as it refers to the existence of the preliminaries and requirements of something, even though these preliminaries might be accompanied by an obstacle and not reach the final result (reflect on this).

Then, it proceeds to state the reality that if Allah does not hasten your punishment, it is because of His grace and mercy upon you, so that you may have sufficient respite to reform yourselves and compensate for the past. It says: "Indeed, your Lord is full of grace toward people, but most of them are not grateful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَ لَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَشْكُرُونَ).

And if they imagine that the delay in their punishment is because Allah is unaware of the evil intentions and wicked thoughts they harbor in their heads, it is a great mistake, for "Indeed, your Lord knows well what their breasts conceal and what they declare" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ ما تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَ ما يُعْلِنُونَ) [1]. He is as aware of their inner secrets as He is of their outward actions; fundamentally, the hidden and the manifest, the unseen and the seen, are the same to Him. It is our limited knowledge that has fashioned and crafted these concepts; otherwise, in the face of an Infinite Being, these concepts quickly lose their color.

Here, Allah's knowledge of the secrets within the hearts is mentioned before the knowledge of outward actions; this is due to the importance of intention and will, and it may also be because outward actions stem from hidden inner intentions, and knowledge of the cause precedes knowledge of the effect.

Then it adds: Not only does Allah know their inner and outer secrets, but His knowledge is so vast and extensive that "There is nothing hidden in the heaven and the earth but that it is recorded in a Clear Book (the Lord's knowledge)" (وَ ما مِنْ غائِبَةٍ فِي السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ) [1]. It is clear that "gha'ibah" has a broad meaning encompassing everything hidden from our senses, including the secret deeds of servants, their inner intentions, the secrets hidden across the expanse of the heavens and the earth, as well as the rising of the Resurrection, the time of the descent of punishment, and the like; there is no reason to interpret it as specifically one of these matters as some commentators have done. The "Clear Book" (Kitabun Mubin) refers to the Preserved Tablet (Lawh-e Mahfuz), the very page of God's infinite knowledge, which we discussed under verse 59 of Surah Al-An'am (refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 271).

[1]: "Tukunnu" is from the root "kann" (rhyming with jann), referring to something in which objects are kept protected and concealed; what is meant here are the secrets, thoughts, and plots that the disbelievers kept hidden in their hearts.

[1]: "Gha'ibah" has a descriptive aspect, and according to a group, the ta in it is not for femininity but for emphasis (mubalaghah), referring to things that are exceptionally veiled and hidden. However, there is also the possibility that the ta is for femininity and its described noun is "things" or "traits" or the like, which has been omitted.

Key Points

An analysis of the above verses shows that the deniers of the Resurrection, in order to shirk the burden of faith in the Day of Judgment and the responsibilities arising from it, approached from three paths:

  1. The perceived impossibility of returning to life after turning to dust, as they believed dust could not be the source of life.
  2. The antiquity of this belief and its lack of novelty.
  3. The non-descent of punishment for the deniers of the Resurrection; for if the deniers were truly to be afflicted by a punishment in this world, why does this punishment not seize them?

However, the Quran leaves the first and second answers to their own obviousness, for we constantly see with our eyes that dust becomes the source of life; we were dust in the beginning and then became living beings.

The antiquity of something never diminishes its importance, for the primary laws of this universe are fixed and established from eternity to eternity. In philosophical principles, mathematical issues, and other sciences, there are many fixed principles. Is the antiquity of the principle of non-contradiction or the Pythagorean multiplication table a reason for its weakness? Or if we see that justice is good and oppression is bad, and it has always been so and will always be so, is that a reason for its invalidity? Fundamentally, it is often the case that antiquity is proof of something's authenticity.

Regarding the third objection, it answers: Do not hurry for the arrival of punishment; it is God's grace that gives you respite and does not punish you swiftly. But be alert: "Though God's punishment comes late, it shall come!"

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَقُصُّ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَكْثَرَ ٱلَّذِى هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٧٦)
This Quran recounts for the Children of Israel most of what they differ about, (76)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٧)
and it is indeed a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (77)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُم بِحُكْمِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٧٨)
Your Lord will decide between them by His judgement, and He is the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (78)
فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٧٩)
So put your trust in Allah, for you indeed stand on the manifest truth. (79)
إِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَلَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٨٠)
You cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf listen to your call when they turn their backs, (80)
وَمَآ أَنتَ بِهَـٰدِى ٱلْعُمْىِ عَن ضَلَـٰلَتِهِمْ ۖ إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٨١)
nor can you lead the blind out of their error. You can make only those hear you who believe in Our signs and have submitted. (81)

Commentary

The Deaf and Blind Do Not Accept Your Word!

In the preceding verses, the discussion revolved around the "Origin" (Creation) and the "Resurrection." The verses under discussion complete this discourse by raising the issue of "Prophethood" and the truthfulness of the Quran.

Furthermore, while the previous discussions touched upon the infinite knowledge of Allah, the current verses provide a more detailed explanation of His knowledge.

Additionally, whereas the previous address was directed at the polytheists, here it speaks of other disbelievers, such as the Jews, and their internal disputes.

First, He says: "Indeed, this Quran relates to the Children of Israel most of that over which they differ" (إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَقُصُّ عَلى‌ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ أَكْثَرَ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

The Children of Israel had many disagreements among themselves regarding "Mary" and "Jesus," and concerning the Prophet whose glad tidings were given in the Torah—specifically, who he would be.

They also had disputes regarding many religious and legal rulings. The Quran came and delivered the truth in this regard, stating: Christ (عليه السلام) explicitly introduced himself, saying: "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet" (قَالَ إِنِّي عَبْدُ اللَّهِ آتَانِيَ الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلَنِي نَبِيًّا) [Maryam: 30].

It also clarified that Christ was born of a mother alone without a father, and that this is not an impossibility, for Allah created Adam without a father or a mother, solely from dust: "Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created him from dust" (إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَى عِنْدَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ خَلَقَهُ مِنْ تُرَابٍ) [Al-Imran: 59].

Regarding the Prophet whose attributes are mentioned in the Torah, the Quran clearly applies them to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), as they apply to no one as perfectly as they do to him.

In any case, one of the missions of the Quran is to combat the divisions that arose due to the mixing of "superstitions" with the "truths of the teachings of the Prophets." Every Prophet has the duty to end the disputes resulting from distortions and the blending of truth and falsehood. Since such a task could not have originated from an unlettered individual arising from an environment of ignorance, it becomes clear that it is from Allah.

And since combating any form of division is a source of guidance and mercy, the next verse states as a general principle: "And indeed, it is a guidance and a mercy for the believers" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَهُدىً وَ رَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌). Yes, it is guidance and mercy in terms of resolving disputes and combating superstitions.

It is guidance and mercy because the proof of its truthfulness lies within the greatness of its content.

It is guidance and mercy because it shows both the path and the manner of traversing that path.

The specific mention of "the believers" here is for the reason we have stated previously: until a stage of faith—meaning a readiness to accept the truth and submission before the Lord—exists within a person, they will not benefit from this overflowing Divine source.

And since a group of the Children of Israel continued to resist the truths recounted by the Quran and did not submit, the next verse adds: "Indeed, your Lord will judge between them by His decree, and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ بِحُكْمِهِ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ‌).

Although this verse does not explicitly state that the final judgment is on the Day of Resurrection, by the evidence of two other verses that speak precisely of the disputes of the Children of Israel and Allah's judgment among them—and explicitly mention the Day of Resurrection—it becomes clear that the objective in the verse under discussion is the same.

In Surah Al-Jathiyah, verse 17, it is stated: "Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

A similar meaning appears in Surah Yunus, verse 93.

Describing Allah as "All-Mighty" (Al-Aziz) and "All-Knowing" (Al-Alim) refers to two attributes necessary for a judge: sufficient knowledge and the power to execute the judgment. Allah is the most knowledgeable of all and the most powerful of all.

And since these words, in addition to stating the greatness of the Quran and threatening the Children of Israel, are a means of peace and tranquility for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the next verse adds: "So rely upon Allah" (فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ‌). Rely upon the God who is All-Mighty and invincible, and who is All-Knowing of everything; rely upon the God who has placed a Quran of such greatness at your disposal.

Rely upon Him and do not fear their opposition, "for indeed, you are upon the clear truth" (إِنَّكَ عَلَى الْحَقِّ الْمُبِينِ‌).

Here, the question arises: if the Quran is the clear truth, then why do so many oppose it? The subsequent verses actually answer this question. It says: if they do not accept this clear truth, and your warm words do not affect their cold hearts, it is no surprise, "for indeed, you cannot make the dead hear" (إِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى‌) [1]. Your audience is the living—those who have a living, awake, and truth-seeking spirit—not the "living dead" whose prejudice, stubbornness, and persistence in sin have shut down their thought and intellect.

"Even those who are alive but whose ears are deaf, you cannot make them hear your call, especially when they turn their backs and go away" (وَ لا تُسْمِعُ الصُّمَّ الدُّعاءَ إِذا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ‌). If they were near you, it might be possible to put your head to their ear and shout, and some of your sound waves might reflect in their heavy hearing; but they are deaf ones who have turned away and are constantly moving further.

Furthermore, if instead of a hearing ear they had a seeing eye, then although the sound might not reach them, it might be possible to find the Straight Path through signs and gestures. But alas, they are also blind: "And you cannot guide the blind out of their error" (وَ ما أَنْتَ بِهادِي الْعُمْيِ عَنْ ضَلالَتِهِمْ‌). Thus, all paths to perceiving the truth are closed to them: their hearts are dead, their ears are deaf, and their eyes are blind.

You "can only make those hear who believe in Our verses and are submissive [to the truth]" (إِنْ تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِآياتِنا فَهُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ‌).

[1]: A group of commentators have taken this sentence and the following ones as evidence for the necessity of reliance (Tawakkul) and the Prophet's lack of despair, while the apparent meaning is that it is an answer to the question raised regarding the Quran being "clear truth."

In reality, the two verses above recount a clear set of the factors of cognition and the ways of human connection with the external world:

"The sense of discernment" and an awake intellect, as opposed to dead-heartedness. "A hearing ear" to absorb the words of truth through the sense of hearing. And "a seeing eye" to observe the face of truth and falsehood through the sense of sight.

However, stubbornness, self-will, blind imitation, and sin blind the truth-seeing eye of man, deafen his ear, and sometimes even disable his intellect and heart. For such people, even if all the Prophets, Saints, and Angels were mobilized for their guidance, it would be ineffective, because their connection with the world outside their existence is completely severed, and they are entirely sunken into themselves.

Similar expressions appear in Surah Al-Baqarah, Surah Ar-Rum, and some other chapters of the Quran. We have had another discussion regarding the importance of the "blessing of the tools of cognition" in the commentary of Surah An-Nahl under verse 78 (Volume 11, page 336).

Additionally, we remind the reader again that the meaning of faith and submission is not that they have already accepted the truths of religion (which would make the effort redundant), but rather the goal is that as long as a person does not possess the spirit of truth-seeking and humility before Allah's command, they will never listen to the words of the Prophets.

Key Points

1- Motives for Reliance (Tawakkul)

"Tawakkul" (Reliance), derived from the root "Wakalat," in the logic of the Quran means trusting and leaning upon Allah, making Him one's Guardian and Trustee, and as a result, not fearing the volume of problems and the mass of obstacles. This is one of the important signs of faith and one of the factors for victory and success in struggles.

Interestingly, in the verses above, the reason for reliance is attributed to two things: first, the power and knowledge of the One upon whom the person relies; and second, the clarity of the path the person has chosen.

In fact, it says: there is no reason to allow weakness, lethargy, fear, or terror into yourself. Your support is the God who is both All-Mighty and invincible, as well as All-Knowing and aware. On the other hand, you are upon the path of clear truth. Why should someone who defends the clear truth have any fear or terror?

And if you see a group opposing you, never be worried; they do not have seeing eyes, hearing ears, or living hearts, and they are essentially outside the scope of preaching and guidance. Only the truth-seekers, the lovers of God, and those thirsty for justice will follow the clear spring of your Quran to be quenched by it.

2- Death and Life in the Logic of the Quran

Many words convey different meanings based on different perspectives. Among these are the terms "life" and "death," which in a materialistic view only mean physiological life and death—meaning when the heart works, blood reaches the organs, and there is sensation, movement, absorption, and excretion in the human body, one is alive. But when these movements cease, it is evidence of definitive death, which can be determined in a few moments with a precise test.

However, from the perspective of the Quran, there are many individuals who are physiologically alive but are counted among the dead, such as those referred to in the verses above. Conversely, there are those who are apparently dead but are eternally alive, such as the martyrs.

The reason for this different interpretation is that Islam, in addition to considering the criterion of human life and personality to be in his spiritual values, recognizes the issue of the presence or absence of influence and benefit as a criterion for the existence or absence of life.

One who is apparently alive but so sunken in desires that he hears neither the cry of the oppressed nor the voice of the caller to truth, sees neither the face of the destitute nor the signs of the Lord's greatness in the scene of creation, and does not even for a moment think about his future or past—such a person is dead in the logic of the Quran. But those whose works, after death, have encompassed a world, and whose thoughts, path, and way are a leader, guide, and model—such people are eternally alive [1]. Beyond all this, according to many documents in our possession, Islam believes in the "Barzakh" (purgatory) life of humans. It is strange that a group of uninformed "Wahhabis" insist on denying any form of life, knowledge, or awareness even from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) after his passing. One of their reasons for denying "Tawassul" (intercession) is this; they say: he is dead, and nothing can be done by the dead. Even stranger is that they seek help from the verses under discussion for this purpose! Meanwhile, some others among them have explicitly stated that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) possesses a type of Barzakh life after his passing—a life superior to the life of the martyrs which is explicitly mentioned in the Quran—and they have even said that he hears the greetings of those who send peace upon him [2].

[1]: Regarding "spiritual death and life," we have had another detailed discussion under verse 24 of Surah Al-Anfal (Volume 7, page 126).

[2]: The second treatise from the treatises of "Al-Hadiyyah al-Saniyyah" (by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab), page 41.

Numerous narrations in the books of both Shi'a and Sunni traditions exist in this regard, stating that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) (and the Imams) hear the words of those who greet them from near and far, respond to them, and even that the deeds of the Ummah are presented to them [1]. In a Hadith found in Sahih Bukhari regarding the story of the Battle of Badr, we read: After the defeat of the disbelievers and the end of the battle, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came with a group of his companions to the edge of a well where the bodies of the slain polytheists had been thrown. He called them by their names and said: "Would it not have been better if you had obeyed Allah and His Messenger? We have found what Allah promised us to be true; have you found what your Lord promised you to be true?" At this point, Umar said: "O Messenger of Allah! You are speaking to bodies that have no souls." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied:

"By Him in whose hand is the soul of Muhammad, you do not hear what I say better than they do" [2].

In the history of the Battle of the Camel, we read that after the defeat of the companions of the Camel, Ali (عليه السلام) was passing among the slain. He reached the body of "Ka'b ibn Sur," the judge of Basra. He said: "Sit him up." They sat him up. He said: "Woe to you, O Ka'b! You had knowledge and learning, but it did not benefit you, and Satan led you astray and sent you to the fire of Hell" [3]. In Nahj al-Balagha, we also read that when Ali (عليه السلام) was returning from Siffin, he reached a cemetery behind the wall of the city of Kufa. He addressed them, speaking words about the instability of the world, then said: "This is the news we have; what news is with you?" Then he added:

"If they were permitted to speak, they would inform you that the best provision [for the hereafter] is piety (Taqwa)."

This itself is evidence that they hear the words and are capable of responding, but they are not permitted [4]. All these expressions refer to the Barzakh life of humans.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to the book "Kashf al-Irtiyab" written by Sayyid Muhsin Amin al-Amili, page 109.

[2]: Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 5, page 97 (Chapter on the killing of Abu Jahl).

[3]: Sharh Nahj al-Balagha by Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Vol. 1, page 248.

[4]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 130.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِذَا وَقَعَ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنَا لَهُمْ دَآبَّةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا لَا يُوقِنُونَ (٨٢)
When the word [of judgement] falls upon them, We shall bring out for them an Animal from the earth who shall tell them that the people had no faith in Our signs. (82)
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجًا مِّمَّن يُكَذِّبُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ (٨٣)
On that day We shall resurrect from every nation a group of those who denied Our signs, and they will be held in check. (83)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُو قَالَ أَكَذَّبْتُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِى وَلَمْ تُحِيطُوا۟ بِهَا عِلْمًا أَمَّاذَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٨٤)
When they come, He will say, ‘Did you deny My signs without comprehending them in knowledge? What was it that you used to do?’ (84)
وَوَقَعَ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِم بِمَا ظَلَمُوا۟ فَهُمْ لَا يَنطِقُونَ (٨٥)
And the word [of judgement] shall fall upon them for their wrongdoing, and they will not speak. (85)

Commentary

Since the preceding verses spoke of the disbelievers' haste regarding the punishment or the realization of the Resurrection—impatiently awaiting its occurrence and asking the Prophet: "Why does this punishment you promise us not overtake us? Why does the Resurrection not begin?"—the verses under discussion point to a portion of the events that will take place on the threshold of the Resurrection, visualizing the painful fate of these stubborn deniers.

It says: "And when the word [of punishment] befalls them and they stand on the threshold of the Resurrection, We shall bring forth for them a moving creature from the earth that will speak to them, for indeed, people did not believe in Our signs with certainty" (وَ إِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ النَّاسَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا لا يُوقِنُونَ‌).

The meaning of "وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ‌" (the word befalls them) is the issuance of Allah's command and the punishment that was promised to them, or the occurrence of the Resurrection and the appearance of its signs—signs upon seeing which everyone becomes humble and submissive, gaining certainty that the Divine promises were true and the Resurrection is near. At that moment, the doors of repentance are closed, for faith under such circumstances takes on a forced and involuntary nature.

Of course, these two meanings are not separate from one another, as the approach of the Resurrection is coupled with the punishment and retribution of the sinners.

But as for what or who this "moving creature of the earth" (Dabbat al-Ard) is, and what its program and mission are, the Quran has expressed it in a veiled manner, as if intending to pass over it briefly; sometimes the impact of a statement lies in speaking of a terrifying matter behind a veil.

It says only this much: it is a moving and living being that Allah brings forth from the earth on the threshold of the Resurrection; it speaks to the people, and its speech is that people did not believe in Allah's signs.

In other words, its task is to separate the ranks and distinguish the deniers and hypocrites from the believers; this is its program.

Naturally, upon seeing this scene, the deniers will come to their senses and regret their dark past, but what use is it when the way of return is closed?

Regarding the details of the "Dabbat al-Ard" and its precise characteristics, many accounts have been recorded in Islamic narrations, in the books of commentary and Hadith of both the Shi'a and the Ahl al-Sunnah, which we will point to, Allah willing, in the section of "Key Points" following these verses.

Then, pointing to another sign of the Resurrection, it says: "And [remind them of] the Day when We shall gather from every nation a group of those who denied Our signs, and they shall be kept in ranks [until they are joined by others]" (وَ يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجاً مِمَّنْ يُكَذِّبُ بِآياتِنا فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ‌).

"Hashr" means to move or bring out a group from their place and drive them toward a battlefield or elsewhere.

"Fawj," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means a group that moves with speed.

"Yūza‘ūn" means to hold back a crowd so that one group joins another, and this expression is usually used for large populations, just as we read a similar term regarding the armies of Solomon in this same Surah.

Therefore, from the totality of the verse, it is understood that a day will come when Allah will gather a group from every people and population and prepare them for the punishment and retribution of their deeds.

Many great scholars consider this verse to be an allusion to the issue of Raj'ah (the Return)—the return of a group of the wicked and the righteous to this very world on the threshold of the Resurrection. For if it were an allusion to the Resurrection itself, the expression "مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجاً" (from every nation a group) would not be correct, because in the Resurrection, everyone is gathered, as the Quran says in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 47: وَ حَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً: "And We shall gather them and not leave behind from them anyone."

Another piece of evidence is that before this verse, the talk was about the signs of the Resurrection at the end of this world, and the upcoming verses also point to the same subject. Therefore, it seems unlikely that the verses before and after speak of events prior to the Resurrection, while the middle verse speaks of the Resurrection itself; the harmony of the verses requires that all be about events before the Day of Judgment.

In this regard, we also have numerous narrations that will be mentioned in the discussion of "Key Points" while explaining the meaning of "Raj'ah."

However, the commentators of the Ahl al-Sunnah usually consider the verse to refer to the Resurrection, and they interpret the word "Fawj" as referring to the leaders and heads of every group. Regarding the disharmony of the verses that arises from this interpretation, they have said that the verses are subject to "advancement and delay" (taqdim wa ta'khir), as if verse 83 were placed after verse 85! But we know that both the interpretation of "Fawj" in that sense is contrary to the literal meaning, and the explanation of the disharmony through "advancement and delay" is also problematic.

"Finally, they bring this group to the reckoning, and Allah says to them: 'Did you deny My signs while you had no knowledge of them and did not seek to investigate them?!'" (حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُ قالَ أَ كَذَّبْتُمْ بِآياتِي وَ لَمْ تُحِيطُوا بِها عِلْماً) "'And what was it that you used to do?'" (أَمَّا ذا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌) [^1] The speaker of these words is Allah, and the "signs" refer to the miracles of the prophets, or Divine commands, or all of these.

The meaning of the phrase "وَ لَمْ تُحِيطُوا بِها عِلْماً" is that you proceeded to deny them without investigating them or gaining knowledge of the reality of the matter, and this is the height of ignorance—that a person seeks to deny something without research or scientific encompassment.

In reality, they are asked two things: one about their denial without investigation, and the other about the deeds they used to perform.

If the above verse is about the Resurrection, its meaning is clear. But if it points to the issue of Raj'ah—as the harmony of the verses requires—it indicates that upon the return of a group of the wicked to this world, the one who is the representative of Allah and the Guardian of the Affair (Wali al-Amr) will interrogate them, then punish them with worldly retribution according to their desert. This will not prevent their punishment in the Hereafter, just as many criminals receive a legal penalty (Hadd) in this world, yet their punishment in the Hereafter remains preserved if they do not repent.

It is obvious that these criminals have no answer to either of these two questions.

[^1]: The phrase "أَمَّا ذا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌" is an interrogative sentence. "Amma" is composed of "am," which is a coordinating conjunction usually appearing after an interrogative hamza to equate two things, and "ma," which is interrogative. The total meaning is: "Or what thing was it that you were doing?"

Therefore, in the last verse under discussion, it adds: "And the word [of punishment] will befall them because of the wrongs they did, and they will not [be able to] speak!" (وَ وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِما ظَلَمُوا فَهُمْ لا يَنْطِقُونَ‌).

This punishment means worldly punishment if we take the verse to mean Raj'ah, and it means the punishment of the Hereafter if we take the verse to mean the Resurrection.

Key Points

1- What is the Dabbat al-Ard?

"Dabbah" means "moving creature" and "Ard" means "earth." Contrary to what some imagine, "Dabbah" is not applied only to non-human creatures; rather, it has a broad meaning that includes humans as well, as we read in Surah Hud, verse 6: وَ ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ رِزْقُها: "There is no moving creature on earth but its provision is upon Allah."

And in Surah An-Nahl, verse 61: وَ لَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللَّهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ما تَرَكَ عَلَيْها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ: "If Allah were to punish people for their wrongdoing, He would not leave a moving creature upon its surface."

And in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 22: إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الصُّمُّ الْبُكْمُ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ‌: "Indeed, the worst of moving creatures in the sight of Allah are the deaf and dumb who do not understand."

However, regarding the application of this word, as we explained in the commentary of the verse, the Quran has left it veiled, as if the intent was brevity and ambiguity. The only description mentioned for it is that it speaks to the people and identifies the faithless in general. But in Islamic narrations and the words of commentators, many discussions are found in this regard, which can be summarized into two interpretations:

1- A group considers it an extraordinary living, moving creature of a non-human species with a strange form, and they have narrated wonders about it similar to the supernatural acts and miracles of the prophets. This creature appears at the end of time, speaks of disbelief and faith, exposes the hypocrites, and places a mark upon them.

2- Another group, following numerous narrations recorded in this regard, considers it to be a human—an extraordinary human, a moving, active, and dynamic human whose primary tasks include separating the ranks of the Muslims from the hypocrites and marking them. It is even understood from some narrations that the Staff of Moses and the Seal of Solomon are with him. We know that the Staff of Moses is a symbol of power and miracle, and the Seal of Solomon is a symbol of Divine government and authority; thus, he is a powerful and exposing human!

In a Hadith from Hudhayfah from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله describing the "Dabbat al-Ard," it is said: "No seeker can overtake it and no fleer can escape it. It marks the believer between his eyes and writes 'believer' between his eyes, and it marks the disbeliever between his eyes and writes 'disbeliever' between his eyes. With it are the Staff of Moses and the Seal of Solomon." [^1]

In numerous narrations, it has been applied to the person of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام: In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "A man said to Ammar ibn Yasir: 'There is a verse in the Quran that has troubled my mind and cast me into doubt.'" Ammar asked: "Which verse?" He said: "The verse: 'And when the word befalls them, We shall bring forth for them a moving creature from the earth that will speak to them, for indeed, people did not believe in Our signs with certainty.' Which creature is this?"

Ammar said: "By Allah, I shall not sit on the ground, nor eat food, nor drink water until I show you the Dabbat al-Ard!" Then he went with the man to Ali عليه السلام while he was eating. When the Imam's عليه السلام eyes fell upon Ammar, He said: "Come." Ammar came, sat, and ate food with the Imam عليه السلام.

The man fell into deep astonishment and watched this scene with disbelief, for Ammar had promised him and sworn that he would not eat until he fulfilled his promise; it was as if he had forgotten his word and oath. When Ammar stood up and bid farewell to Ali عليه السلام, the man turned to him and said: "Strange! You swore that you would not eat, nor drink, nor sit on the ground until you showed me the Dabbat al-Ard?" Ammar replied: "I showed him to you, if only you understood!" [^1]

A similar Hadith is narrated in the commentary of al-Ayyashi from Abu Dharr رحمة الله عليه. [^2]

Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar narrates with a reliable chain from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that Ali عليه السلام was sleeping in the mosque. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came there, woke Ali عليه السلام, and said: "Rise, O Dabbat Allah!" (Rise, O Moving Creature of Allah!). One of the companions asked: "O Messenger of Allah, do we have the right to call one another by such a name?"

The Prophet said: "No, this name is specific to him. He is the 'Dabbat al-Ard' whom Allah mentioned in the Quran: 'And when the word befalls them, We shall bring forth for them a moving creature from the earth...' Then He said: 'O Ali! At the end of time, Allah will bring you to life in the best form, and in your hand is a tool with which you shall mark the enemies.'" [^1]

According to these narrations, the above verse relates to Raj'ah (the Return) and is harmonious with the verse that comes later regarding Raj'ah.

The late Abu al-Futuh al-Razi writes in his commentary under the above verse: "According to the reports narrated through our companions, 'Dabbat al-Ard' is an allusion to Imam al-Mahdi, the Master of the Age عليه السلام." [^2]

Taking this Hadith and the aforementioned ones into account, one can derive a more general concept from "Dabbat al-Ard" that applies to any of the great leaders who rise and make an extraordinary movement at the end of time, distinguishing truth from falsehood and believer from disbeliever.

The expression in the narrations that the Staff of Moses and the Seal of Solomon—symbols of power, victory, and governance—are with him is a clue that the Dabbat al-Ard is a highly active human, not an animal.

Also, the fact that narrations state he marks the believer and the disbeliever and distinguishes their ranks is consistent with a human.

Speaking to the people, which is mentioned in the text of the Quranic verse as his description, is also appropriate for this meaning.

In summary, we reach the conclusion that while on one hand the word "Dabbah" is mostly used for non-humans (though it is repeatedly used in the Quran in a general sense or regarding humans), on the other hand, there are multiple clues within the verse itself, and numerous narrations in the commentary of the verse, indicating that the "Dabbat al-Ard" here is a human with the characteristics we mentioned above—a highly active human, a definer of the line between truth and falsehood, believer, hypocrite, and disbeliever; a human who appears on the threshold of the Resurrection and is himself one of the signs of the Lord's greatness.

2- "Raj'ah" in the Book and the Sunnah

Among the matters observable in the verses under discussion is the appearance of some of these verses regarding the issue of Raj'ah.

Raj'ah is one of the well-known beliefs of the Shi'a, and its explanation in a short phrase is this: After the appearance of al-Mahdi عليه السلام and on the threshold of the Resurrection, a group of "pure believers" and "extremely wicked disbelievers and tyrants" will return to this world. The first group will traverse stages of perfection, and the second group will face severe punishments.

The late Sayyid Murtada, who is among the great Shi'a scholars, says: "Allah the Almighty, after the appearance of Imam al-Mahdi, will bring back to this world a group of those who had previously passed away, so they may share in the reward and the honors of helping him and witnessing the government of truth over the entire world, and He will also bring back a group of fierce enemies to take revenge upon them."

He then adds: "The proof for the correctness of this school is that no rational person can deny Allah's power over this matter, for this is not an impossible issue, whereas some of our opponents deny this subject so much as if they consider it impossible."

He further adds: "The proof for establishing this belief is the consensus (ijma') of the Imamiyyah, for none of them has opposed this belief." [^1]

Of course, from the words of some early Shi'a scholars and also from the words of the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, it appears that a very small minority of the Shi'a opposed this belief and interpreted Raj'ah as the return of the state and government of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, not the return of individuals and the reviving of the dead. However, their opposition is such that it does not harm the consensus.

In any case, there are many discussions here which we bring in a condensed form to avoid departing from the style of exegetical discussion:

1- Without doubt, the reviving of a group of the dead in this world is not among the impossibilities, just as the reviving of all humans in the Resurrection is completely possible. Wondering at such a thing is like the wonder of a group of the pagans of the Age of Ignorance regarding the issue of the Hereafter, and mocking it is like their mockery of the Hereafter, for reason does not see such a thing as impossible, and Allah's power is so vast and expansive that all these matters are easy before Him.

2- In the Holy Quran, the occurrence of Raj'ah is mentioned generally in five instances regarding previous nations:

A- Regarding "the prophet who passed by a township while its walls had collapsed and the corpses and bones of its people were scattered everywhere, and he asked himself how Allah would bring these to life after death. But Allah caused him to die for a hundred years and then revived him and asked him: 'How long have you tarried?' He said: 'A day or part of a day.' He said: 'Nay, but you have tarried a hundred years'" (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 259). Whether this prophet was Uzair or another prophet makes no difference; what is important is the Quran's explicitness regarding life after death in this very world (فَأَماتَهُ اللَّهُ مِائَةَ عامٍ ثُمَّ بَعَثَهُ‌).

B- In verse 243 of the same Surah Al-Baqarah, the Quran speaks of another population who "went out of their homes for fear of death (and according to commentators, on the pretext of a plague to avoid participating in Jihad). Allah commanded them to die and then revived them" (فَقالَ لَهُمُ اللَّهُ مُوتُوا ثُمَّ أَحْياهُمْ‌). Although some commentators who could not tolerate the occurrence of such an extraordinary event have considered it merely a parable, it is clear that such interpretations are unacceptable in the face of the appearance, or rather the explicitness, of the verse regarding the occurrence of this matter.

C- In verses 55 and 56 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we read regarding the "Children of Israel" that a group of them, after requesting to see Allah, were struck by a deadly thunderbolt and died. Then Allah returned them to life so they might be grateful for His blessing (ثُمَّ بَعَثْناكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

D- In verse 110 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, while enumerating the miracles of Jesus عليه السلام, we read: وَ إِذْ تُخْرِجُ الْمَوْتى‌ بِإِذْنِي‌: "And when you brought forth the dead by My permission." This expression shows that the Messiah عليه السلام utilized this miracle of his (reviving the dead); rather, the use of the present tense verb (tukhriju) is evidence of its repetition, and this itself is considered a type of Raj'ah for some.

E- Finally, in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 73, regarding a slain person among the Children of Israel over whose killer a dispute had arisen, the Quran says: "It was commanded to slaughter a cow with specific traits and strike a part of it against the body of the dead man so that he would return to life (and identify his killer, ending the dispute)" (فَقُلْنا اضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِها كَذلِكَ يُحْيِ اللَّهُ الْمَوْتى‌ وَ يُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

In addition to these five cases, other instances are seen in the Holy Quran, such as the story of the People of the Cave, which was also something similar to Raj'ah, and the story of the four birds of Abraham عليه السلام which, after being slaughtered, returned to life once more to visualize for him the possibility of the Resurrection for humans—which is also noteworthy in the issue of Raj'ah.

In any case, how is it possible for someone to accept the Quran as a Heavenly Book and yet deny the possibility of Raj'ah despite all these clear verses? Fundamentally, is Raj'ah anything other than a return to life after death? Is Raj'ah not considered a small sample of the Resurrection in this small world? How can someone who accepts the Resurrection on its vast scale draw a red line through the issue of Raj'ah? Or mock it? Or say, like the Egyptian Ahmad Amin in the book Fajr al-Islam: "Another Judaism appeared in the Shi'a school because of the belief in Raj'ah!" [^1] Truly, what is the difference between this statement of Ahmad Amin and the wonder and denial of the pagan Arabs in the face of the physical Resurrection?!

3- What we have said so far established the possibility of Raj'ah. What confirms its occurrence are the many narrations reported from a group of reliable narrators from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام. Since our discussion does not have the capacity to quote them all, it is sufficient to recount the statistics that the late Allamah Majlisi gathered from them. He says: "How is it possible for someone to believe in the truthfulness of the words of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام and not accept the mutawatir (frequently narrated) Hadiths of Raj'ah? Explicit Hadiths whose number reaches about two hundred, which more than forty reliable narrators and prominent scholars have recorded in more than fifty books... If these Hadiths are not mutawatir, then what Hadith is mutawatir?!" [^1]

3- The Philosophy of Raj'ah

The most important question raised against this belief is: what is the purpose of Raj'ah before the general Resurrection of humans?

According to what is understood from Islamic narrations, this subject is not universal; rather, it is specific to the righteous believers who are at a high stage of faith, and also the disbelievers and oppressive tyrants who are at a low stage of disbelief and oppression. It seems that the re-return of these two groups to the life of the world is for the purpose of completing a cycle of perfection for the first group and tasting worldly punishment for the second group.

In other words, for a group of pure believers who encountered obstacles and barriers in their spiritual perfection during their lives and whose perfection remained incomplete, Divine wisdom requires that they continue their evolutionary path through a re-return to this world, to be witnesses and observers of the global government of truth and justice and to participate in the building of this government, for participating in the formation of such a government is among the greatest honors.

Conversely, a group of fierce hypocrites and tyrants, in addition to their specific punishment in the Resurrection, must see punishments in this world similar to what rebellious nations like the people of Pharaoh, 'Ad, Thamud, and the people of Lot saw, and the only way for that is Raj'ah.

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says in a Hadith: "Indeed, Raj'ah is not general; it is specific. None shall return except those who were purely faithful or purely polytheistic." [^1]

It is possible that verse 95 of Surah Al-Anbiya: وَ حَرامٌ عَلى‌ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْناها أَنَّهُمْ لا يَرْجِعُونَ‌: "And it is forbidden for a town which We have destroyed that they shall return," is also an allusion to this meaning, for it speaks of the non-return of those who reached their severe punishment in this world, and from that it becomes clear that the group who did not see such punishments must return and be punished (reflect on this).

There is also the possibility that the return of these two groups at that specific juncture of human history serves as two great lessons and two important signs of Allah's greatness and the issue of the Resurrection (the Beginning and the Return) for humans, so that by witnessing it, they may reach the peak of spiritual perfection and faith and have no deficiency from any perspective.

5- Some have imagined that the belief in Raj'ah is not consistent with the principle of human free will and choice. From what we said above, it becomes clear that this is a total mistake, because their return to this world is under normal conditions and they enjoy full freedom.

And as for those who say it is possible for the tyrants and fierce disbelievers to repent after Raj'ah and return to the truth, the answer is that such individuals are so deeply immersed in oppression, corruption, and disbelief that these matters have become part of the fabric of their being, and no return is conceivable for them.

Just as the Quran says in response to a group of the people of Hell who, on the Day of Judgment, request a return to the world to compensate for their errors: وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ‌: "And if they were returned, they would certainly revert to that which they were forbidden" (Surah Al-An'am, verse 28).

Also, as for those who have said: Raj'ah is not consistent with verse 100 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, because according to this verse the polytheists request a return to the world to perform righteous deeds and say: "رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صالِحاً فِيما تَرَكْتُ‌" (My Lord, send me back that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind), but they are given a negative response and told "كَلَّا إِنَّها كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قائِلُها" (No! It is but a word he is saying)—the answer to this becomes clear considering that this verse is general while Raj'ah is specific. (Reflect on this).

6- The final word is that the Shi'a, while believing in Raj'ah which they have taken from the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, do not consider those who deny Raj'ah to be disbelievers, because Raj'ah is among the necessities of the Shi'a school, not the necessities of Islam. Therefore, they do not sever the bond of Islamic brotherhood with others because of it, but they continue the logical defense of their belief.

It is also noteworthy that occasionally many superstitions have been mixed with the issue of Raj'ah, which has distorted its face in the eyes of some. It is necessary to base the foundation on authentic Hadiths and avoid damaged and doubtful ones.

What we have said here was a summary of the discussions related to Raj'ah, and for information on other specifics and details, one should refer to the books written in this field.

Given the amount we have provided here, the response to the uninformed attacks of some commentators of the Ahl al-Sunnah against the Shi'a (such as what al-Alusi brought in Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses in question) becomes clear—that these critics, "since they did not see the truth, took the path of fable!"

Ayahs

أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ لِيَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٦)
Do they not see that We made the night that they may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (86)
وَيَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ دَٰخِرِينَ (٨٧)
The day when the trumpet is blown, whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will be terrified, except such as Allah wishes, and all will come to Him in utter humility. (87)
وَتَرَى ٱلْجِبَالَ تَحْسَبُهَا جَامِدَةً وَهِىَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ ٱلسَّحَابِ ۚ صُنْعَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٨٨)
You see the mountains, which you suppose, to be stationary, while they drift like passing clouds—the handiwork of Allah who has made everything faultless. He is indeed well aware of what you do. (88)

Commentary

The Motion of the Earth—A Scientific Miracle of the Quran

Once again, these verses address the issue of the Origin and the Resurrection, the signs of Allah's power and greatness in the universe, as well as the events of the Day of Judgment, stating: "Did they not see that We made the night for them to rest therein" (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِيَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ).

"And the day giving light" (وَ النَّهارَ مُبْصِراً).

"Indeed, in these matters are clear signs and proofs of the Lord's power and wisdom for those who are ready to accept faith" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

This is not the first time the Quran speaks of the life-giving effects of night and day and the system of light and darkness, nor will it be the last. This is because the Quran is a book of education, upbringing, and human-building. We know that the principles of education sometimes require repeating a subject at different intervals and reminding people of it so that it is fully impressed upon the mind and, so to speak, "settles in."

The tranquility that arises from the darkness of the night is an established scientific reality. The curtains of nocturnal darkness are not only a compulsory means for suspending daily activities but also have a profound effect on the nervous systems of humans and other living beings, plunging them into rest and deep sleep—or, in the Quranic expression, "tranquility" (Sukun).

Similarly, the relationship between the light of day and the effort, movement, and activity that are characteristic of the radiance of light is also beyond doubt from a scientific perspective. Not only does sunlight illuminate the scene of life and make the human eye active, but it also awakens every particle of the human being and compels them toward activity.

This verse clarifies a corner of "Monotheism of Lordship" (Tawhid al-Rububi). Since the true object of worship is the Lord and Regulator of the universe, it draws a line of falsehood across the faces of idols and urges polytheists to reconsider their beliefs.

It is also necessary to note that a human must harmonize himself with this system: resting at night and engaging in effort and endeavor during the day to remain healthy and active, unlike those seekers of pleasure who stay awake at night and fall into sleep during the day until near noon.

Interestingly, "mubsiran," which originally means "seeing" or "perceiving," is used as an attribute for the day, whereas it is actually a description of humans during the day. This is a beautiful type of emphasis, just as sometimes the night is described as sleeping, and they say "laylun na’im" (a sleeping night)!

The difference in expression observed in the verse regarding the benefit of night and day—where in one place it says "that they may rest therein" (li-yaskunu fihi) and in the other "giving light" (mubsiran)—might be an indication that the primary goal of the night is rest and tranquility, but the goal of the day's light is not merely seeing; rather, seeing is a tool for reaching the blessings of life and benefiting from them (reflect carefully).

In any case, although this verse directly speaks of Monotheism and the regulation of the universe, it can also contain a subtle allusion to the issue of the Resurrection, for sleep is like death, and awakening is like life after death.

The next verse addresses the Resurrection and its preliminaries, saying: "And [remind them of] the Day the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will be terrified, except whom Allah wills. And all shall come to Him humbled" (وَ يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ وَ كُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ داخِرِينَ).

From the collection of Quranic verses, it is understood that the "blowing of the Trumpet" occurs two or three times:

  1. Once at the end of the world and the threshold of the Resurrection, when terror grips everyone.
  2. The second time, everyone expires and dies upon hearing it (these two blowings might be one).
  3. The third time is at the moment of the Rising and Resurrection, when all the dead return to life with the blowing of the Trumpet and begin a new life.

There is discussion among commentators as to whether the above verse refers to the first and second blowings or the third. There are indications within the verse itself and subsequent verses for both views; some have also considered it an allusion to all these blowings.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse indicates it refers to the first blowing that occurs at the end of the world, because "faza‘," which means the fear and terror that overwhelms the entire human heart, is counted among the effects of this blowing. We know that in the blowing of the Resurrection, the fear and terror are due to deeds, accounting, and retribution, not due to the impact of the blowing itself.

In other words, the appearance of the "fa" of consequence in "fa-fazi‘a" implies that this terror and panic result from the blowing of the Trumpet, and this is specific to the first blowing. The final blowing is not only not terror-inducing but is the source of life, movement, and activity; if there is any terror, it stems from the human's own deeds.

As for the concept of "Nafkh al-Sur," "Nafkh" means "blowing," and "Sur" means "trumpet" or "horn." Regarding what is meant by this expression here, there are many discussions that, Allah willing, will come under the commentary of verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar.

As for the phrase "except whom Allah wills" (إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ), which is mentioned as an exception to this general terror, it refers to the virtuous and pure, including angels and believers in the heavens and the earth. In the light of faith, they possess a special tranquility; neither the first blowing of the Trumpet terrifies them nor the last. In the following verses, we also read that those who come to Allah's presence with a hand full of good deeds are safe and secure from the terror of that day: "Whoever comes with a good deed will have better than it, and they will be safe from the terror of that Day" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها وَ هُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ).

However, the phrase "And all shall come to Him humbled" (وَ كُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ داخِرِينَ) is apparently general and contains no exception, for even the Prophets and Saints are humble and small before Him. If we see that in verse 127 of Surah As-Saffat it says: "Indeed, they will be brought [for punishment], except the chosen servants of Allah" (فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ), it does not contradict the generality of the verse under discussion. This is because the verse under discussion refers to the principle of presence in the Gathering and before Allah, while the second refers to presence on the scene of accounting and the examination of deeds.

The next verse points to another sign of Allah's greatness in the expanse of the universe, saying: "And you see the mountains and think them solid and stationary, while they pass away like the passing of clouds" (وَ تَرَى الْجِبالَ تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً وَ هِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ).

"This is the artistry of Allah, who perfected all things" (صُنْعَ اللَّهِ الَّذِي أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ) [1]. He whose program of creation contains all this calculation and order "is surely Aware of what you do" (إِنَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَفْعَلُونَ).

Many commentators believe that the above verse refers to the events at the threshold of the Resurrection, for we know that at the end of this world and the beginning of the next, great earthquakes, explosions, and massive transformations occur, and the mountains are pulverized. This point is explicitly mentioned in many of the final Surahs of the Quran.

The placement of this verse among the verses of the Resurrection is evidence and proof for this interpretation.

However, there are numerous indications in the verse that support another interpretation: that the above verse is among the verses of Monotheism and the signs of Allah's greatness in this very world, referring to the "motion of the planet Earth," which is not perceptible to us.

Explanation: 1. The above verse says: "You think the mountains are solid and stationary, while they move like clouds." It is clear that this expression is not compatible with the events of the beginning of the Resurrection, for those events are so manifest that, in the words of the Quran, "on seeing them, every nursing mother will forget her nursling, and every pregnant woman will drop her load, and people will appear drunk from the intensity of terror, though they are not drunk!" (Surah Al-Hajj, verse 2).

  1. The comparison to the movement of clouds is appropriate for uniform, gentle, and noiseless motions, not for massive explosions whose thunderous sound deafens the ears.

  2. The above expression shows that while the mountains are apparently stationary, they are actually moving rapidly at that same moment (i.e., it describes two states of one thing simultaneously).

  3. The term "itqan," which means organizing and making firm, is also appropriate for the time when the system of the world is established, not the time when this system collapses and is pulverized and destroyed.

  4. The phrase "He is Aware of what you do" (إِنَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَفْعَلُونَ), especially considering that "taf‘alun" is a present/future tense verb, shows that it relates to this world, as it says: "He is aware of the deeds you are doing now and in the future." If it were related to the end of this world, it should have said "ma fa‘altum" (what you did) (reflect carefully).

From the sum of these indications, it is precisely understood that this verse describes another of the wonders of creation and is, in fact, similar to what appeared two verses prior (أَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِيَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ).

Therefore, the verses under discussion are partly about Monotheism and partly about the Resurrection.

The conclusion we draw from this interpretation is that these mountains, which we perceive as stationary, are moving at great speed. Certainly, the movement of mountains without the movement of the other lands connected to them is meaningless. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes that the Earth moves with speed, just like the movement of clouds! According to the calculations of modern scientists, the speed of the Earth's rotation around itself is nearly 30 kilometers per minute, and its orbital speed around the sun is even greater than this.

But why did the Quran make "mountains" the center of the discussion? Perhaps because mountains are proverbial for their heaviness, weight, and stability, and they serve as a better example for explaining Allah's power. When mountains of such greatness and weight move by Allah's command (along with the Earth), His power over all things is proven.

In any case, the above verse is one of the scientific miracles of the Quran, for we know that the first scientists to discover the motion of the planet Earth were Galileo of Italy and Copernicus of Poland, who revealed this belief in the late 16th and early 17th centuries AD, even though the church authorities severely condemned and pressured them!

But the Holy Quran, about a thousand years before them, pulled back the curtain from this reality and presented the motion of the Earth in the above form as a sign of Monotheism.

Some Islamic philosophers, while accepting the second interpretation—i.e., the reference to the movement of mountains in this world—have considered the verse to be observant of the "substantial motion" (al-harakat al-jawhariyyah) of things and consider it a harmony with and support for the famous theory of substantial motion [1]. However, the expressions of the verse are not compatible with that, because the comparison to the movement of clouds is appropriate for movement in space (locomotion), not movement in substance. Therefore, the apparent meaning of the verse accepts only one interpretation, and that is the mechanical motion of the Earth (around itself or around the sun).

[1]: "Sun‘a Allah" is in the accusative case due to a hidden verb such as "Observe" or "It is the work of."

[1]: By "substantial motion," it is meant that the objects of the material world, in addition to various changes in quality, quantity, place, and the like, have a motion within their very essence. That is, their essence is a fluid and moving existence, and outward changes are a reflection of their continuous internal changes. In other words, we have two types of existence that are essentially different: stable existence (metaphysical existence) and fluid/moving existence (material existence). The most important proof for this theory is considered to be the issue of material beings having time, as well as the inseparability of outward changes from internal changes, the explanation of which is outside the scope of our discussion.

Ayahs

مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا وَهُم مِّن فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ ءَامِنُونَ (٨٩)
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] better than it; and on that day they will be secure from terror. (89)
وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَكُبَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِى ٱلنَّارِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٠)
But whoever brings vice—they shall be cast on their faces into the Fire [and told:] ‘Shall you not be requited for what you used to do?’ (90)
