[Say], ‘I have been commanded to worship the Lord of this city who has made it inviolable and to whom all things belong, and I have been commanded to be among those who submit [to Allah], (91)
وَأَنْ أَتْلُوَا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ (٩٢)
and to recite the Quran.’ Whoever is guided is guided only for his own good, and as for him who goes astray, say, ‘I am just one of the warners.’ (92)
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ سَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ فَتَعْرِفُونَهَا ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٣)
And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah. Soon He will show you His signs, and you will recognize them.’ Your Lord is not oblivious of what you do. (93)

Commentary

The Final Mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)

In the preceding verses, there was mention of the deeds of the servants and Allah's awareness of them. In the first verses under discussion, the focus is on the reward for actions and their safety from the terror of the Day of Resurrection.

He says: "Whoever brings a good deed shall have better than its worth, and they will be safe from the terror of that Day" (مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها وَ هُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ‌).

Regarding what is meant by "good deed" (hasanah), commentators have offered various interpretations:

Some have interpreted it as the word of Monotheism (Tawhid), "There is no god but Allah," and faith in God.

Others consider it a reference to the Wilayah (guardianship) of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), and the Infallible Imams. This meaning has been emphasized in numerous narrations reaching us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام). Among them is a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) where we read that one of the companions of Ali (عليه السلام) named "Abu Abdullah Jadali" came to him. The Imam said: "Shall I inform you of the meaning of this word of Allah: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها ... (until the end of the next verse)?" He replied: "Yes, may I be sacrificed for you, O Commander of the Faithful!" The Imam said:

The good deed (al-hasanah) is the recognition of the Wilayah and our love, the Ahl al-Bayt; and the evil deed (al-sayyi'ah) is the denial of the Wilayah and hatred toward us, the Ahl al-Bayt. [^1]

Of course, as we have stated many times, the meaning of the verses is broad and extensive. "Good deed" and "evil deed" here also have a wide concept that encompasses all righteous acts, including faith in Allah, the Prophet of Islam, and the Wilayah of the Imams (عليهم السلام), which stands at the peak of every good deed. This does not prevent other righteous deeds from being included in the verse as well.

As for those who were concerned about this generality because of the word "better" (khayr)—asking: "Can anything better than faith in Allah be found to serve as its reward?"—the answer is clear: for the pleasure and satisfaction of the Lord is even higher than faith. In other words, all of these are preliminaries for that, and the objective is superior to the preliminary.

Another question raised here is that the literal meaning of some verses (such as verse 2 of Surah Al-Hajj) suggests that the terror of the Resurrection encompasses everyone; how then are the possessors of good deeds exempted from it?

Verse 103 of Surah Al-Anbiya provides the answer to this question, as it says: "The righteous believers are safe from the great terror."

And we know that the "great terror" is the terror of the Day of Resurrection and the terror of entering Hell, not the terror that occurs at the time of the blowing of the Trumpet (reflect on this).

Then, addressing the opposite of this group, He says: "And whoever brings an evil deed, they will be cast down on their faces into the Fire!" (وَ مَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَكُبَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِي النَّارِ). And they cannot expect anything else: "Are you recompensed for anything other than what you used to do?" (هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

"Kabbat" is derived from the root "kabb," which originally means throwing something on its face to the ground. Therefore, the mention of "faces" (wujuh) in the above verse is for emphasis.

Casting this group on their faces into the Fire is described as the worst type of punishment.

Furthermore, because when they were faced with the Truth, they turned their faces away and welcomed sin with those same faces, they must now be afflicted with such a punishment.

The phrase "Are you recompensed for anything other than what you used to do?" might be an answer to a question that could be raised here: if someone says this punishment is severe, the response is that these are your very deeds that have caught up with you; you have no recompense except your own actions (reflect on this).

Then, in the final three verses of this Surah, the discourse turns to the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and recounts truths that essentially express this reality: "Tell them: I am performing my duties, whether you stubborn polytheists believe or not." First, He says: "I have only been commanded to worship the Lord of this city (the city of Mecca)!" (إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ رَبَّ هذِهِ الْبَلْدَةِ).

This sacred city in which all your pride and existence are summarized; the sacred city that Allah bestowed upon you with His blessings, yet you show ingratitude instead of thanking Him for His bounty.

The sacred city which is the secure sanctuary of Allah, the noblest spot on earth, and the oldest temple of Monotheism.

Yes, I am commanded to "worship the Lord who has made it sacred" (الَّذِي حَرَّمَها).

He has placed specific characteristics for it, with special honors, laws, and prohibitions that do not exist for other cities in the world.

But do not imagine that only this land is the property of Allah; rather, "to Him belongs everything in existence" (وَ لَهُ كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ).

And the second command given to me is: "I am commanded to be of the Muslims"—absolute submission to the command of the Lord and none other than Him (وَ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ‌).

In this way, he expresses his two primary missions: "worshiping the One God" and "absolute submission to His command."

Then, he describes the means of achieving these two goals as follows: "And I am commanded to recite the Quran" (وَ أَنْ أَتْلُوَا الْقُرْآنَ‌). To take light from its flame, to drink draughts from its fountain of the water of life, and to rely on its guidance in all programs. Yes, this is my means for reaching those two sacred goals and for fighting against every kind of polytheism, deviation, and misguidance.

Following that, he adds: Do not imagine that your believing benefits me, or even higher, benefits the Great God; no, "whoever is guided is only guided for himself" (فَمَنِ اهْتَدى‌ فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ‌).

And all the benefits of guidance, whether in this world or the next, return to him.

"And whoever goes astray, its burden and consequence are upon himself; say: I am only of the warners" (وَ مَنْ ضَلَّ فَقُل| إِنَّما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ‌).

Its evil consequences will not affect me; my duty is the clear delivery of the message (al-balagh al-mubin). My duty is to show the path and insist on traversing it, but he who wishes to remain in misguidance has only made himself miserable.

It is interesting that regarding guidance, He says whoever is guided, it is for his own benefit, but regarding misguidance, He does not say it is to his own detriment; rather, He says: "I am of the warners." This difference in expression may indicate that I will never remain silent before the misguided, nor will I leave them to themselves; rather, I will constantly warn and continue this work without getting tired, for I am a warner. (Of course, in some other verses of the Quran, both expressions appear similar, but we know that differences in expression harmonize with differences in context and are sometimes used to convey various and distinct meanings).

It is noteworthy that this Surah begins with a mention of the importance of the Quran and ends with an emphasis on the recitation of the Quran; the beginning and the end are the Quran.

Finally, in the last verse, He commands the Prophet to praise and glorify Allah for all these great blessings, especially the blessing of guidance. He says: "Say: Praise be to Allah" (وَ قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ‌). This praise and glorification refers back to the blessing of the Quran, to divine guidance, and can also be a prelude to the next sentence which says:

"Soon He will show you His signs so that you shall recognize them" (سَيُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ فَتَعْرِفُونَها).

This expression indicates that with the passage of time and the progress of human science, knowledge, and intellect, every day the veil is lifted from new signs and fresh secrets of the universe, and day by day you become more acquainted with the greatness of the Lord's power and the depth of His wisdom. This presentation of signs never ceases and continues throughout the life of humanity.

However, if despite all this, you still follow the path of opposition and deviation, know that "your Lord is never unaware of what you do" (وَ ما رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Do not think that if Allah delays your punishment out of His grace, it is because He is unaware of your deeds or that the record of them is not preserved.

The sentence "And your Lord is not unaware of what you do," which is repeated exactly—or with slight variation—in nine places in the Holy Quran, is a short sentence but a meaningful threat and a warning to all human beings.

With the final verse of Surah An-Naml, Volume 15 of Tafsir Nemouneh concludes. It is now the sunset of the last day of the month of Sha'ban 1403, near the sighting of the crescent of the holy month of Ramadan. O Lord! By the sanctity of these dear months of Yours, we implore You to grant us the success of sincere servitude, absolute submission to Your command, and the recitation of the verses of Your Holy Quran.

O Allah! Show us a new corner of the signs of Your greatness every day so that we may know You better and more each day, and engage in thanking You for all these bounties You have bestowed upon us.

O Lord! A multitude of problems has surrounded our Islamic society, and enemies within and without are striving hard to extinguish Your light.

You are the One who gave Solomon all that power and gave Moses all that strength against the Pharaohs; grant us victory over these enemies and shatter those who are not capable of being guided, just as You did with the people of 'Ad, Hud, Thamud, and the people of Lot! And praise be to Allah, Lord of the Worlds. 30 Sha'ban, the year 1403.

June 12, 1983. End of Volume 15 of Tafsir Nemouneh.
